source.title: Operator Resilience Safety-net and Playbook
source.author: Aisha Reynolds
source.body:
Summary
This playbook codifies operational safety-nets and a lightweight runbook lifecycle engineered to reduce operator fatigue while preserving rapid restoration for Redwood Inference services. It combines: 1) pragmatic runbook trimming, 2) safety-net automation, 3) a synthetic incident lab for continuous readiness, and 4) customer-facing status templates.

When to use this playbook
- Primary: multi-region latency regressions, cache-thrashing events, and burst-rate capacity exhaustion where immediate bypass or temporary mitigation is required.
- Secondary: learning exercises and runbook pruning sprints.

Scope and goals
- Restore service within SLO windows: P99 latency < 250ms for inference proxies; request success >= 99.5% over 5m.
- Reduce mean time-to-diagnose (MTTD) by 30% through automated evidence capture.
- Limit oncall cognitive load: surface 3 prioritized actions per runbook.

Owner & Roles
- Primary owner: Runtime Reliability (team: eng-sre).
- Oncall bridge owner (role): Service steward who opens the bridge and owns customer notices.
- Escalation lane: Tier 1 responder -> Runtime SME -> Oncall Lead -> Engineering Manager.
- Contact matrix: pager (PagerDuty), slack channel (#ops-bridge), phone escalation for Sev1.

Safety-net patterns (automations to reduce manual steps)
1) Circuit breaker: automatic 10% traffic diversion to warm fallback model when downstream latency > 600ms for 2m.
   - Implementation: routing-policy "latency-fallback-v1" in the router config.
2) Prefix-KV cache warmup: on serve node restart, prefetch most common 50 prefixes from hotset (TTL 1h).
3) Auto-evidence snapshot: when SLO alarm triggers, runbook agent captures traces, last 500 logs, and current config (store TTL 30d) with:
   - Example command: rwi collect-evidence --service inference-proxy --window 5m --out /tmp/evidence-{{ts}}.tgz
4) Rate-limit soft-cut: temporary per-tenant limit to 80% of baseline if noisy neighbor detected.

Runbook pruning & fatigue mitigation
- Rule: Each runbook must present exactly three escalation steps: an immediate containment action, a short-term mitigation, and a durable fix path.
- Quarterly pruning: Runbooks with >6 steps or >750 words are flagged for a 1-hour pruning lab.
- Heatmap review: track operator interventions per runbook; deprecate or automate steps that are executed >50% automatically.
- Shadow policy: new runbooks need 2 shadow oncall validations before marked production.

Synthetic incident lab (Game-day variant)
Purpose: validate safety-net automations and human-in-the-loop paths on a quarterly cadence.
Preparation checklist:
1) Select a realistic scenario (e.g., cache eviction cascade in eu-west-1).
2) Define measurable objectives: time-to-detect, time-to-divert, correct customer message selection.
3) Create controlled faults using chaos-tooling (example):
   - chaos inject --service inference-proxy --region eu-west-1 --fault latency --p99-ms 1200 --duration 10m
4) Playbook run: execute 30m simulated incident; observe whether circuit breaker and prefetch run; measure manual steps taken.
Post-lab actions:
- 48h retro with runbook owner to adjust steps and update automation thresholds.
- Update runbook metrics and add a test-case to CI (synthetic regression).

Escalation ladder (decision table)
| Symptom | Severity | Immediate owner | Escalation if not resolved | Customer impact |
|---|---:|---|---|---|
| 5m sustained P99 > 600ms across region | Sev 1 | Bridge owner -> Runtime SME within 10m | Oncall Lead after 30m | Large customer latency |
| Partial tenant errors (>=1%) | Sev 2 | Tier 1 responder | Runtime SME after 20m | Affected tenants only |

Status page & customer comms templates
- Use the following templated flows. Always include: impact, scope, mitigation steps, ETA, contact, and follow-ups.

Status page template (short):
Title: Degraded inference latency in eu-west-1
Impact: Customers in eu-west-1 may experience increased response times and occasional failures.
Scope: ~10% of requests routed through inference-proxy-eu-west-1.
Mitigation: Traffic is being diverted to eu-west-2 fallback pools; autoscaling engaged.
ETA: Ongoing; next update in 30 minutes.
Contact: status.redwood.ai and support@redwood.ai
Customer email template (for higher-impact incidents):
Subject: Incident: Inference latency affecting eu-west-1 (Ticket #{{id}})
Hi <Customer name>,
We’re investigating increased latency impacting inference calls routed to eu-west-1. Our mitigation includes: diversion to fallback pools and targeted autoscaling. We estimate stabilization within 60 minutes. We’ll provide a follow-up summary with root cause analysis and any credits (if applicable).
Regards,
Redwood Inference Support

Runbook example: "Inference-proxy high-latency (eu-west-1)"
Scope: P99 > 600ms sustained for 5m, or error rate >= 1% for same window.
Steps (3 prioritized actions):
1) Contain: toggle routing-policy to "latency-fallback-v1" (CLI)
   - CLI: rwi router set-policy --name latency-fallback-v1 --enable
2) Mitigate: scale dedicated pool by +30% via dedicated API
   - API: curl -X POST https://internal-api.redwood/ops/scale -d '{"pool":"dedicated-eu-west-1","delta":30}'
3) Fix & follow-up: collect evidence (see Auto-evidence) and open a postmortem within 24h if Sev1.
Postmortem intake checklist
- Timeline with timestamps (UTC)
- Evidence bundle link (auto-evidence snapshot)
- Who was paged (PD incident id), who owned bridge, decisions made
- Customer impact: tenants, duration, credits or remediation promised
- Root cause hypothesis and verification steps
- Action items (owner, ETA)

Operational metrics to collect
- Time-to-first-ack (target < 2m)
- Time-to-contain (target < 15m for Sev1)
- Runbook step success rate (target > 90%)
- Automation-trigger rate vs manual action rate

Appendix: quick commands and alerts
- Evidence capture: rwi collect-evidence --service inference-proxy --window 5m --out s3://evidence-bucket/{{ts}}.tgz
- Toggle routing: rwi router set-policy --name latency-fallback-v1 --enable
- Scale pool: curl -X POST https://internal-api.redwood/ops/scale -d '{"pool":"dedicated-eu-west-1","delta":30}' -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>'

Notes and references
- See runbook governance page for lifecycle rules.
- Link to SLO definitions: /confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets-2024

Revision history
- 2026-02-14: Created by Aisha Reynolds.
- 2026-03-30: Added auto-evidence snapshot template (Marcus Li).
- 2026-09-20: Updated game-day checklist and routing-policy play (Priya Natarajan).

source.title: Postmortem: SEV-2 Scheduler starvation due to continuous batching bug (2026-03-29)
source.author: Rishi Malhotra
source.customer_impact: A subset of tenants experienced elevated tail latency on chat requests (p99 from ~2.8s to ~9.4s) and intermittent request timeouts (max 1.1%) during the incident window.
source.summary: A scheduler fairness bug introduced in a continuous batching optimization caused starvation for long-context requests when short requests arrived continuously.
source.timeline:
08:17 UTC: Tail latency alert fired for chat in us-east (p99).
08:22 UTC: On-call confirmed queue metrics showed long-context requests waiting disproportionately.
08:35 UTC: Disabled continuous batching optimization via feature flag for affected pool.
08:48 UTC: Tail latency decreased; timeout rate returned to baseline.
09:09 UTC: Incident resolved after 20 minutes of stable metrics.
source.root_cause: The schedulers queue selection logic prioritized shortest estimated remaining time without an aging/fairness factor. Under sustained short-request arrival, long-context requests accumulated and starved.
source.detection: Tail latency alert (p99) and scheduler queue depth dashboard.
source.mitigation: Feature-flag disabled the continuous batching optimization for chat pools and temporarily routed long-context traffic to a separate pool with conservative batching.
source.what_went_well: Feature-flag allowed rapid mitigation without a rebuild. Dashboards made starvation visible.
source.what_went_wrong: Fairness regression was not covered by perf-canary scenarios; the change was validated only on average latency.
follow_ups: Implement aging/fairness in scheduler selection to prevent starvation (Serving Runtime; due 2026-04-25).
Add perf-canary scenario with mixed short/long arrivals and guardrail on p99 (Serving Runtime; due 2026-04-20).
Update release checklist: any scheduler policy change must report tail latency deltas by bucket (Release Engineering; due 2026-04-15).

title: Postmortem: SEV-2 Scheduler starvation due to continuous batching bug (2026-03-29)
preview: A scheduler fairness bug introduced in a continuous batching optimization caused starvation for long-context requests when short requests arrived continuously.
text: A scheduler fairness bug introduced in a continuous batching optimization caused starvation for long-context requests when short requests arrived continuously.

source.title: Postmortem: SEV-2 Runtime kernel selection caused latency spike (2026-03-18)
source.author: Ethan Park
source.customer_impact: p95 latency increased by ~2.1x for a subset of long-context requests (8kk tokens) in eu-west. Error rate remained low (<0.3%), but SLO burn occurred.
source.summary: A new kernel selection heuristic preferred a kernel variant that performed poorly for a specific sequence length distribution on A100s, causing latency regressions.
source.timeline:
15:12 UTC: SLO burn alert for eu-west chat long-context bucket fired.
15:20 UTC: On-call compared perf-canary data and saw regression after runtime build 2026.03.18.0.
15:33 UTC: Disabled new heuristic via feature flag; kernel selection reverted.
15:45 UTC: Latency returned near baseline; continued monitoring.
16:08 UTC: Incident resolved.
source.root_cause: The heuristic used average sequence length rather than a percentile-based feature, leading it to select a kernel optimized for uniform 8k sequences. Real traffic had a bimodal distribution with heavy tail, making the choice suboptimal.
source.detection: SLO burn alert and perf-canary comparison.
source.mitigation: Feature-flag rollback of the heuristic and forced kernel selection to the prior stable variant for the affected pool.
source.what_went_well: Feature flags allowed rapid rollback without rebuild. Canary data provided quick correlation.
source.what_went_wrong: The heuristic change lacked traffic-representative validation for eu-west. Documentation did not specify the assumptions behind the new metric.
follow_ups: Update kernel selection model features to use p90 sequence length and tail weight (Serving Runtime; due 2026-04-10).
Add eu-west long-context perf-canary gating for runtime releases (Release Engineering; due 2026-04-01).
Document kernel selection assumptions and safe rollback steps (Serving Runtime; due 2026-04-05).

title: Postmortem: SEV-2 Runtime kernel selection caused latency spike (2026-03-18)

text: A new kernel selection heuristic preferred a kernel variant that performed poorly for a specific sequence length distribution on A100s, causing latency regressions.

source.title: Postmortem: Dedicated capacity starvation on gpu-pool-delta (2026-03-10)
source.author: Connor O'Brien
source.content:
## Summary
On 2026-03-10, several Dedicated customers experienced elevated queueing delays and request timeouts due to capacity starvation in gpu-pool-delta. The pool had sufficient total GPUs, but an imbalance in GPU fragmentation and incorrect priority weights prevented timely scheduling of certain large-model replicas.

## Customer impact
- Duration: 1 hour 26 minutes
- Symptoms: increased queue depth; p95 latency +55%; intermittent 504s
- Scope: Dedicated customers mapped to gpu-pool-delta in us-east-1

## Detection
- Alert: dedicated queue depth > threshold
- Customer Success escalation: "timeouts on pinned dedicated endpoint"

## Timeline (UTC)
- 17:42: Queue depth alerts fire
- 17:55: Infra on-call identifies fragmentation + weight misconfig
- 18:10: Mitigation: move two tenants to gpu-pool-echo; restart scheduler with corrected weights
- 19:08: Queue depth returns to normal

## Root cause
The dedicated scheduler's priority weights were updated without considering large-model GPU packing constraints. The resulting placement favored smaller replicas, fragmenting GPU availability and starving large replicas that required contiguous resources.

## Contributing factors
- No packing simulation in CI for scheduler weight changes
- Capacity dashboards did not show fragmentation metrics

## Follow-up action items
1. Add fragmentation metric to capacity dashboards ("largest contiguous block" and "packing efficiency"). Owner team: Infrastructure. Priority: P0.
2. Implement CI simulation for scheduler weight changes using representative tenant/model mixes. Owner team: Infrastructure. Priority: P1.
3. Add SLO alert for dedicated queueing time (not just depth) and tie to paging policy. Owner team: SRE. Priority: P0.
4. Document dedicated pool rebalancing runbook including safe tenant move procedures and communication template. Owner team: Customer Success. Priority: P2.
5. Add safeguard: prevent weight rollout if predicted starvation risk exceeds threshold. Owner team: Platform. Priority: P1.

## Lessons learned
Dedicated reliability depends on scheduler correctness and visibility into fragmentation. Simulations and better metrics are needed to prevent subtle capacity starvation failures.

5. Add safeguard: prevent weight rollout if predicted starvation risk exceeds threshold. Owner team: Platform. Priority: P1.

## Lessons learned
Dedicated reliability depends on scheduler correctness and visibility into fragmentation. Simulations and better metrics are needed to prevent subtle capacity starvation failures.

source.title: Operational Rotation Capacity and Customer Status Playbook
source.author: Marisol Ortega
source.body:
Overview:

This playbook defines how Redwood scales on-call capacity against SLOs, how we choreograph rapid escalations, and the exact cadence + templates for customer-facing status updates during multi-tenant incidents. It is intended for SREs, on-call engineers, product stewards, and customer-facing support teams.

Goals:
- Ensure incidents are handled within SLO-driven timeboxes (see SLO section) with predictable staffing cost.
- Provide a standard escalation choreography that minimizes firefighting and accelerates decisions.
- Provide clear, templated, and consistent customer communications for each incident class.
- Capture evidence and postmortem artifacts to close the learning loop.

Scope:
- Applies to all Redwood production-facing inference services (hosted API, Dedicated control plane, and Private control-plane endpoints).
- Does NOT replace service-level runbooks; it augments them with capacity calculations and status cadence.

Key Definitions:
- Incident Class: P0 (full outage), P1 (severe degradation impacting >20% tenants), P2 (partial degradation), P3 (minor / localized).
- Timebox: maximum interval the operator should spend in triage before escalating (see SLOs).

SLOs and Escalation Thresholds:
- P0: Restore control-plane availability to >=99.9% within 60 minutes. Initial acknowledgement: < 2 minutes. Escalate to Exec Bridge at 15 minutes if not stabilized.
- P1: Latency/throughput regressions hitting >30% of baseline traffic: Initial acknowledgement < 5 minutes; mitigation path or rollout rollback within 45 minutes.
- P2: Tenant-affecting errors < 60 minutes diagnosis step, remediation within 4 hours.
- P3: Non-urgent functional regressions; triage within 4 hours, plan in next working-day sprint.

Responsible Roles (single-line roster format):
- First Responder: engineer on rotation who performs initial triage and runbook invocation.
- Service Owner: code/infra owner for the service, responsible for root cause analysis and fix.
- Bridge Lead: coordinates cross-team comms and runbook steps during an active incident.
- Customer Liaison: member of Customer Success or Support who drafts and posts customer-facing status.
- Contingency Lead: SRE on-call with permission to activate capacity/policy fallbacks (e.g., cross-region failover, capacity burst).

Runbook Invocation Criteria (short checklist):
1) Alert firing + metric breach equals runbook invocation candidate.
2) First Responder confirms impact to customer-facing requests within first 5 minutes.
3) If P0/P1 candidate, immediately open an incident channel and tag Bridge Lead.
4) If transient metric spike without user-visible error, monitor for two collection intervals before escalation.

Escalation choreography (numbered procedure):
1. Rapid Triage (0–10 minutes):
   - First Responder acknowledges alert, captures: time, alert ID, initial symptom, key metrics (error rate, p95 latency, request QPS).
   - Set severity: P0/P1/P2/P3.
   - If P0/P1: open Incident Channel (Slack/X) + Incident Doc (shared Google doc link).
2. Stabilize (10–30 minutes):
   - Run mitigation checklist: scale up reserved pools, enable fallback model routing, disable experimental rollouts.
   - Contingency Lead authorizes capacity injections or cross-region routing.
3. Communicate (within first 15 minutes for P0/P1):
   - Post initial customer status (Template A) on Status Page and notify active customers via configured channel (email/SMS/webhook).
4. Diagnose and Remediate (30–90 minutes):
   - Service Owner performs deeper debugging, captures trace samples, heap/disk snapshots, and KV cache metrics.
   - If fix is code-level, prepare canary patch or rollback plan.
5. Escalate if necessary (15/45/90 minute gates):
   - At 15 minutes (P0) or 45 minutes (P1) with no stabilization: notify Exec and expand incident bridge.
   - At 90 minutes without mitigation: invoke customer credit and formal executive brief protocol.
6. Close & Follow-up:
   - Declare incident resolved when service performance returns to post-incident baseline for two consecutive collection intervals (configurable).
   - Post incident: assemble evidence and resume postmortem schedule.

Rotation Capacity Model (practical formula and examples):
- Goal: keep probability of simultaneous two-person shortage < 1% for critical bridges.
- Definitions: N = active on-call engineers for a domain; R = average daily pages per engineer; S = target maximum sustained pages/day tolerated without burnout (we set S=6).
- Simple sizing heuristic: N_required = ceil( (E * H) / (S * W) ) where E = expected daily alerts, H = average handling time (hours), W = overlap factor accounting for planned/unplanned absences (we use 0.85).
- Example: E=18 alerts/day, H=0.5h per alert, W=0.85, S=6 => N = ceil((18*0.5)/(6*0.85)) = ceil(9/5.1) = 2.
- Capacity Buffer: maintain a reserve pool of 1 engineer per 6 primary on-call to cover vacations and game-day spikes.

On-call Shadowing and Handoff (compact checklist):
- Pre-shift: incoming on-call reads last 24h incident digest and outstanding P1s/P2s.
- Handoff note includes: active incidents, mitigations in progress, outstanding runbook steps, and contact list (Bridge Lead, Contingency Lead, Service Owner).
- Shadowing: new responders shadow two real incidents and one game-day simulation before being allowed solo responses.

Game Day Choreography (scenarios and scoring):
- Purpose: validate routing, KV cache warmup, and capacity injection in a controlled window.
- Core scenarios: KV-cache eviction cascade, multi-region latency spike, model-rollback failure, and tenant-isolation breach.
- Scoring rubric: triage time (target < 10m), mitigation applied (yes/no), customer status posted (yes/no), postmortem drafted (yes/no).
- After action: each game day produces a 1-page synthesis, action owners, and scheduled remediation tickets.

Customer Status Page Cadence and Templates:
- Rules: always post public status page updates for P0/P1 incidents. P2 may get a brief public note if >30 minutes. P3 internal only unless escalated by CS.
- Update cadence: initial (0–15m), follow-up (30–60m), hourly while active, post-incident resolution + 24h follow-up with root-cause summary.

Templates (copy/paste friendly):
- Template A (Initial public notice — P0/P1):
  Subject: [Redwood] Service Degradation — Investigating (Impact: [brief])
  Body: We are currently investigating an issue affecting [service area]. Impact: [short summary — latency/errors/throughput]. We have engaged our on-call team and are actively triaging. No workaround is available at this time. Next update: in 30 minutes.

- Template B (Customer-facing interim update):
  Subject: [Redwood] Update — Progress on Incident (Impact: [brief])
  Body: We have applied [mitigation e.g., capacity injection / rollback / routing change] and are measuring results. Current metrics: p95 latency [value], error rate [value]. We will post another update at [time].

- Template C (Resolution + RCA preface):
  Subject: [Redwood] Incident Resolved — Summary & Next Steps
  Body: The incident affecting [service area] has been resolved at [time]. Impact: [summary]. Root cause: [high-level]. Actions taken: [brief]. A full postmortem will be published in 7 business days. If you have questions, contact support@redwood.com.

Postmortem evidence checklist (minimum artifacts):
- Incident timeline with UTC timestamps and actor actions.
- Alert/metric graphs (error rates, p95 latency, QPS) with annotation.
- Traces and representative trace IDs (3–5).
- Logs and structured error messages for the first 30 minutes.
- Dry-run scripts and rollback plan used (if any).
- Customer impact mapping (tenant IDs, regions, request counts).
- Communications: public status updates and internal bridge logs.

Runbook Catalog & Invocation Tags (searchable tags):
- runbook-kv-cache-eviction, runbook-model-rollback, runbook-capacity-injection, runbook-region-failover, runbook-rate-limit-throttle, runbook-db-connection-pool.
- Tagging rules: each runbook must include Severity, InvocationCriteria, MitigationSteps (ordered), and ReversionSteps.

Metrics to collect during an active incident (minimum):
- p50/p95/p99 latency per region and per model variant.
- Error rate (5s, 1m, 5m windows).
- Queue length and in-flight request counts.
- Model KV cache hit ratio and cache eviction rate.
- Pod/GPU utilization and OOM events.

Review cadence and continuous improvement:
- Postmortem published: within 7 business days for P0/P1; at least a 1-page synthesis for P2 within 10 business days.
- Quarterly rotation capacity review: SRE leads review alert volumes, adjust N_required, and re-run staffing model.
- Runbook pruning: retire or update runbooks older than 12 months or with >3 failed-invocations in the last quarter.

Appendix A — Quick Action Cheat-sheet (one-line actions):
- "Scale GPU pool" => allocate reserved burst capacity (Console > Capacity > Burst).
- "Switch model routing" => set fallback policy to compatible-quantized variant.
- "Enable prefix cache" => toggle cache flag in Serving Runtime for top-ten tenants.

Appendix B — Escalation contact list (abridged):
- Service Owner: service-owner@redwood.com
- Contingency Lead: contingency-oncall@redwood.com
- Bridge Lead: bridge-ops@redwood.com
- CS Liaison: cs-liaison@redwood.com

Related artifacts and links:
- See runbook catalog (internal link) and incident evidence chain template for forensic collection.

How to use this playbook:
- Bookmark and follow steps in order. For any ambiguity, the Bridge Lead has final decision authority for escalation. This document is subject to quarterly review by SRE with owners assigned in the runbook catalog.

Document history and change log:
- 2025-11-02: Created (Marisol Ortega).
- 2026-03-15: Added rotation capacity model and customer templates; reviewers updated (Ethan Park, Aisha Raman, Devon Patel).

source.title: Postmortem: SEV-2 Usage metrics lag impacted billing exports (2026-03-05)
source.author: Kimberly Park
source.customer_impact: Billing exports for a subset of enterprise contracts were delayed by up to 6 hours. No incorrect invoices were sent, but several Finance Ops workflows were blocked.
source.summary: A backlog in the usage metrics pipeline caused downstream billing exports to lag behind real-time token usage.
source.timeline:
02:10 UTC: Automated job for hourly billing exports reported staleness >60 minutes.
02:20 UTC: Data Platform on-call engaged; identified increasing Kafka consumer lag.
03:05 UTC: Root cause traced to a schema change increasing message size and triggering slower deserialization.
03:30 UTC: Increased consumer resources and rolled back the schema change.
05:40 UTC: Backlog began clearing; staleness dropping.
06:35 UTC: Pipeline caught up; exports resumed on schedule.
source.root_cause: A usage event schema change added an uncompressed prompt preview field which increased message size and CPU cost for consumers. Autoscaling thresholds were based on QPS, not processing time, so the consumer group did not scale fast enough.
source.detection: Automated staleness check on billing exports.
source.mitigation: Rolled back the schema change, temporarily increased consumer resources, and replayed affected partitions to ensure completeness.
source.what_went_well: Staleness check prevented incorrect exports. Replay tooling worked as intended.
source.what_went_wrong: Schema review did not consider downstream CPU impact; autoscaling policy was not aligned with processing latency.
follow_ups: Add schema-change checklist requiring downstream throughput impact assessment (Data Platform; due 2026-03-25).
Update consumer autoscaling to include processing time / lag slope (Data Platform; due 2026-04-05).
Move prompt preview field behind a separate topic for debug-only sampling (Data Platform; due 2026-04-15).

title: Postmortem: SEV-2 Usage metrics lag impacted billing exports (2026-03-05)
preview: A backlog in the usage metrics pipeline caused downstream billing exports to lag behind real-time token usage.
text: A backlog in the usage metrics pipeline caused downstream billing exports to lag behind real-time token usage.

source.title: P1 Incident Postmortem: Private deployments upgrade regression (auth handshake failures)
source.author: Soojin Lee
source.body:
## Summary
On 2026-02-27, a subset of Private (VPC) deployments experienced intermittent auth handshake failures after upgrading the private gateway. The issue was resolved by rolling back a TLS library change and issuing a patched build.

## Severity
P1

## Customer impact
- 4 Private deployments saw 401/handshake errors for ~41 minutes.
- Hosted API was not impacted.

## Timeline (UTC)
- 21:02: First report from customer SE channel.
- 21:10: Engineering engaged; suspect gateway upgrade.
- 21:24: Rollback initiated for affected deployments.
- 21:43: Error rate returns to baseline.

## Root cause
A TLS renegotiation setting changed behavior for clients using older OpenSSL versions, causing the gateway to terminate connections during token exchange.

## Detection
Detected via customer reports; existing dashboard did not separate handshake failures by client TLS fingerprint.

## Follow-up action items (count these)
1) Add automated compatibility test matrix for gateway TLS with OpenSSL 1.0.2/1.1.1 and common JVM clients. Owning team: Eng Private Deployments.
2) Add metric breakdown of handshake failures by client fingerprint; alert on spikes. Owning team: Eng SRE.
3) Update private upgrade playbook with explicit TLS compatibility check and rollback criteria. Owning team: Eng Private Deployments.
4) Pin TLS library version in private gateway build and require security review for upgrades. Owning team: Security.

## References
- Private gateway release: 2026.02.27

source.title: P1 Incident Postmortem: Metrics pipeline dropped tracing spans
source.author: Evelyn Hart
source.body:
## Summary
On 2026-02-25, Redwood Console traces for several services showed missing spans and incomplete request graphs. The underlying services were healthy, but the observability pipeline dropped a portion of spans under burst load.

## Severity
P1

## Customer impact
- Internal impact: on-call and support debugging slowed due to partial traces for ~2 hours.
- No direct serving impact; data plane SLOs were met.

## Timeline (UTC)
- 12:08: First report from on-call: trace graphs missing child spans.
- 12:22: SRE identifies span-ingester backpressure and queue drops.
- 12:44: Mitigation: increase ingester replicas + raise queue limits temporarily.
- 14:05: Drop rate returns to baseline; backlog drains.

## Root cause
The span ingester queue was configured with a low max size and a drop-on-full policy. A parallel deploy increased span volume per request (additional attributes), pushing the queue into frequent drops during peaks.

## Detection
Detected via manual observation; there was no alert on span drop rate.

## Follow-up action items (count these)
1) Add alert on span drop rate (drop_rate > 0.5% for 5 minutes) and page SRE. Owning team: Eng SRE.
2) Add load test to observability pipeline release checklist (span volume burst + attribute size). Owning team: Eng SRE.
3) Add safe defaults and documentation for span attribute limits in the instrumentation SDK wrapper. Owning team: Eng Platform.

## References
- Service: span-ingester
- Dashboard: Observability > Tracing pipeline health

source.title: Postmortem: 2026-02-17 Rollout latency regression — auto-rollback did not trigger due to missing/stale monitors
source.author: Rafael Mendes
source.body:
## Summary
On 2026-02-17 we ran a controlled rollout (internal production route) of a new model serving profile for `redwood-api/chat-gateway` in `us-east-1` (Self-serve tier), moving traffic from **model version `rw-mistral-7b-instruct-2026.02.10`** (baseline) to **`rw-mistral-7b-instruct-2026.02.17`** (candidate).

During the canary step, the candidate experienced a **p99 latency regression** (tail latency increase) and a brief **5xx spike** under bursty traffic. We expected the rollout controller to auto-pause/auto-rollback based on the configured SLO monitors. No automated action occurred.

The on-call engineer manually paused the rollout after observing the degradation in Grafana. Impact was limited due to low canary percentage and quick manual mitigation, but the event revealed a critical gap: **the SLO monitor query used by the controller returned missing/stale data for the rollout scope**, and the controller treated this as “no breach” instead of “unsafe → stop rollout.”

This postmortem documents what happened, why auto-rollback didn’t trigger, and the corrective actions that directly feed the **Auto-rollback on SLO breach integration** project.

---

## Customer impact
- **Blast radius:** Internal production route (dogfood + a subset of low-risk Self-serve traffic in us-east-1).
- **Traffic affected:** Canary step at 5% of eligible traffic.
- **Observed symptoms:**
  - p99 latency increased from ~1.9s to ~2.6s on candidate traffic.
  - 5xx rate peaked at ~1.2% on candidate traffic for ~4 minutes.
- **User-visible impact:** Minor; a small number of elevated-latency requests and transient errors for canary traffic only.
- **SLA impact:** No SLA breach recorded; this was below paging thresholds for the broader service SLO.

---

## Detection
- **Primary detection:** Manual observation in Grafana by on-call (no paging).
- **Expected detection:** Rollout controller auto-pause/auto-rollback based on SLO burn-rate and error-rate monitors scoped to (route, model version, region, tier).
- **Why paging didn’t happen:** Alerts were configured for overall service SLOs, not rollout-scoped candidate vs baseline comparisons.

---

## Timeline (all times PT)
- **10:03** — Rollout `rollout_id=rl_2026_02_17_1003_chatgw_use1` initiated (baseline → candidate). Step 1 starts: 1% traffic.
- **10:10** — Step advances to 5% traffic (canary). No controller warnings.
- **10:14** — Tail latency begins to drift on candidate requests. p99 + ~250ms vs baseline.
- **10:18** — Candidate p99 reaches ~2.6s; brief saturation observed (GPU queue depth spikes).
- **10:20** — 5xx spike begins (candidate only), correlated with queue spikes.
- **10:22** — On-call notices divergence on per-version dashboard; posts to `#eng-oncall`.
- **10:25** — Manual pause executed in rollout controller.
- **10:28** — Traffic returns to baseline; latency normalizes.
- **10:41** — Initial investigation finds rollout controller shows “SLO evaluator: OK” for the entire interval.
- **11:15** — Deeper look reveals rollout-scoped SLO query returned “no data / stale,” but the controller treated it as non-breaching.
- **13:30** — Root cause confirmed: rollout-scoped monitor missing required labels + stale query cache path.
- **Next day** — Action items triaged into Auto-rollback SLO integration project and release gates.

---

## What happened (technical)
The rollout controller relies on SLO monitors to decide whether to:
1) continue to next step,
2) pause,
3) rollback to last known-good.

For this rollout, the controller attempted to query burn-rate + error-rate monitors for the following scope:
- service: `redwood-api`
- route: `POST /v1/chat/completions` (chat-gateway)
- region: `us-east-1`
- customer_tier: `self_serve`
- model_version: `rw-mistral-7b-instruct-2026.02.17`

However:
- The **latency burn-rate monitor for the rollout scope did not exist** (monitor definition lacked `model_version` as a dimension, and an automated monitor generator failed to create the per-version monitor for the new candidate).
- The SLO toolkit query path returned a cached response for a similar-but-not-identical scope (missing `model_version` label), and that cached response was **stale** (last datapoint timestamp older than our acceptable freshness window).
- The controller’s evaluation code path interpreted “missing/stale monitor data” as **`HEALTHY/UNKNOWN → allow continue`** rather than **`UNSAFE → stop rollout`**.

Net result: auto-mitigation logic never saw a “breach,” and no auto-pause or rollback occurred.

---

## Root cause
### Primary root cause
**Monitor coverage gap + unsafe handling of missing/stale monitor data in rollout evaluation.**

Specifically:
1) A rollout-scoped SLO monitor was missing for candidate traffic because the monitor generator did not create a per-`model_version` monitor for this route.
2) The controller did not treat missing/stale monitor responses as a gating failure (it effectively failed open).

### Contributing factors
- **Ambiguous semantics:** We did not have a documented, enforced rule for “monitor missing/stale → pause vs rollback vs continue.”
- **Insufficient auditability:** The controller UI/logs showed “SLO evaluator: OK” but did not capture that it was operating on missing/stale data.
- **No rollout-scoped alerts:** Our alerting focused on global service SLOs; rollout-specific divergence didn’t page or notify by default.
- **Label mismatch and cardinality caution:** Prior observability guidance discouraged high-cardinality labels, leading to uneven adoption of `model_version`-scoped monitors. We need explicit standards for what dimensions are required for rollout safety, with bounded cardinality strategies.

---

## Why auto-rollback did not trigger (explicit)
Auto-rollback did not trigger because **the evaluator never entered a “breach detected” state**.

Decision chain at the time (reconstructed):
1) Controller requests SLO status for rollout scope.
2) Monitor adapter returns response with `data_freshness=stale` and `monitor_found=false` (internally represented as `UNKNOWN`).
3) Evaluator treats `UNKNOWN` as “no breach” (rather than “cannot prove safe”).
4) Rollout controller continues/presents status as OK.

This is the core gap this project must close: **lack of guardrails around monitor presence/freshness, and lack of safe defaults**.

---

## What went well
- On-call caught the regression quickly via dashboard comparison and paused the rollout.
- Canary percentage limited the blast radius.
- Service owners and SRE were able to reconstruct the failure mode within the same day.
- The incident provided a concrete test case for the Auto-rollback integration PRD/design.

## What went wrong
- The controller effectively failed open when SLO signals were missing/stale.
- Monitor coverage for rollout-critical dimensions (route/model_version/region/tier) was not enforced.
- Logs/UI lacked the detail needed to understand the evaluator inputs (monitor IDs, freshness, sample sizes, query windows).
- No release gate prevented enabling auto-mitigation for a scope lacking the required monitors.

---

## Corrective actions
The following actions are prioritized to directly feed the **Auto-rollback on SLO breach integration** workstream.

### P0 (must-have before broad production enablement)
| Action | Owner | Due | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Define and implement evaluator semantics for `monitor_missing` / `monitor_stale` | Sean Gallagher / Jada Williams | 2026-03-15 | Default should be **pause rollout** (safe stop) with explicit suppression option; no automatic rollback without a valid signal. |
| Add audit trail fields to capture monitor inputs and freshness | Felix Schneider | 2026-03-22 | Include monitor IDs, query windows, last datapoint timestamp, sample sizes, policy version, and decision trace. |
| Add metrics + alerting for `monitor_missing_or_stale` on active rollouts | Aisha Bello / Allison Grant | 2026-03-22 | Notify (non-paging) by default; paging only if rollout is high-tier or >X% traffic. |
| Add release gate: “Required monitors exist + fresh” before enabling auto-rollback for a route/model/region/tier | Connor O'Brien / Benji Okafor | 2026-03-29 | Gate to be used by service teams and Redwood release train checklist. |

### P1 (hardening)
| Action | Owner | Due | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Monitor coverage audit for top 20 routes and all prod regions | Rafael Mendes | 2026-04-05 | Track owners + remediation dates; feed into coverage sheet and quarterly reliability review. |
| Implement minimum sample-size gating + dual-window burn-rate logic (fast/slow) | Jordan Lee | 2026-04-12 | Align with ADR-0127; reduce false positives and prevent oscillation. |
| E2E test: inject synthetic latency + simulate stale monitor responses | Leila Farouk | 2026-04-12 | Must verify: pause occurs, audit logs written, and UI shows “missing/stale” reason. |
| Update observability standard: required labels/dimensions for rollout safety and bounded cardinality guidance | Allison Grant | 2026-04-19 | Clarify when `model_version` is required and how to manage retention/rollups. |

### P2 (process and documentation)
| Action | Owner | Due | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Runbook update: triage steps for missing/stale monitors and override procedure | Kira Thompson / Rafael Mendes | 2026-04-26 | Include “how to verify monitor freshness,” “how to suppress with justification,” and “when to rollback manually.” |
| Game day scenario: stale monitor during real rollout | Rafael Mendes / Sean Gallagher | 2026-05-03 | Validate no fail-open paths remain and alerting routes correctly. |

---

## Follow-ups / design decisions captured
- We will codify the breach evaluation strategy (burn-rate windows, debounce/holdoff, minimum samples) in an ADR and ensure the controller behavior is deterministic and auditable. See: `ADR-0127`.
- We will standardize a rollout auto-action audit event schema (including monitor metadata, snapshots, and decision traces). See: `ADR-0128`.
- We will standardize metric names and labels for rollout evaluations and outcomes to support dashboards/alerts and avoid uncontrolled cardinality. See: `Rollouts SLO metrics naming and labeling`.

---

## Appendix
### A. Evidence and debugging notes (abridged)
- The SLO query for candidate scope returned `monitor_found=false` for latency burn-rate and an error-rate monitor response with `last_datapoint_age > 15m`.
- The controller UI displayed “SLO evaluator: OK” without showing `UNKNOWN` / staleness state.
- Grafana showed clear divergence in p99 for candidate traffic; the regression was strongly correlated with queue depth increases.

### B. Immediate mitigations applied during event
- Manual pause of rollout at 5% canary.
- Candidate removed from traffic; baseline restored.

### C. Open questions
- For high-tier traffic (Dedicated/Private), do we page on missing/stale monitors immediately when a rollout is active, or only after a grace period?
- What is the acceptable maximum data freshness lag per signal type (latency vs errors vs saturation), and does it vary by region/provider?

---

## Ownership
- Incident owner: Rafael Mendes (SRE)
- Service owners: Jada Williams (Rollout controller), Chloe Martin (Console backend), Logan Wright (Telemetry)
- Follow-up tracking: Talia Benson (Program manager)

This postmortem is considered complete once P0 actions are implemented and validated in a game day with both “latency regression” and “monitor stale/missing” scenarios.

| Action | Owner | Due | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Monitor coverage audit for top 20 routes and all prod regions | Rafael Mendes | 2026-04-05 | Track owners + remediation dates; feed into coverage sheet and quarterly reliability review. |
| Implement minimum sample-size gating + dual-window burn-rate logic (fast/slow) | Jordan Lee | 2026-04-12 | Align with ADR-0127; reduce false positives and prevent oscillation. |
| E2E test: inject synthetic latency + simulate stale monitor responses | Leila Farouk | 2026-04-12 | Must verify: pause occurs, audit logs written, and UI shows “missing/stale” reason. |
| Update observability standard: required labels/dimensions for rollout safety and bounded cardinality guidance | Allison Grant | 2026-04-19 | Clarify when `model_version` is required and how to manage retention/rollups. |

### P2 (process and documentation)
| Action | Owner | Due | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Runbook update: triage steps for missing/stale monitors and override procedure | Kira Thompson / Rafael Mendes | 2026-04-26 | Include “how to verify monitor freshness,” “how to suppress with justification,” and “when to rollback manually.” |
| Game day scenario: stale monitor during real rollout | Rafael Mendes / Sean Gallagher | 2026-05-03 | Validate no fail-open paths remain and alerting routes correctly. |

---

## Follow-ups / design decisions captured
- We will codify the breach evaluation strategy (burn-rate windows, debounce/holdoff, minimum samples) in an ADR and ensure the controller behavior is deterministic and auditable. See: `ADR-0127`.
- We will standardize a rollout auto-action audit event schema (including monitor metadata, snapshots, and decision traces). See: `ADR-0128`.
- We will standardize metric names and labels for rollout evaluations and outcomes to support dashboards/alerts and avoid uncontrolled cardinality. See: `Rollouts SLO metrics naming and labeling`.

---

## Appendix
### A. Evidence and debugging notes (abridged)
- The SLO query for candidate scope returned `monitor_found=false` for latency burn-rate and an error-rate monitor response with `last_datapoint_age > 15m`.
- The controller UI displayed “SLO evaluator: OK” without showing `UNKNOWN` / staleness state.
- Grafana showed clear divergence in p99 for candidate traffic; the regression was strongly correlated with queue depth increases.

### B. Immediate mitigations applied during event
- Manual pause of rollout at 5% canary.
- Candidate removed from traffic; baseline restored.

### C. Open questions
- For high-tier traffic (Dedicated/Private), do we page on missing/stale monitors immediately when a rollout is active, or only after a grace period?
- What is the acceptable maximum data freshness lag per signal type (latency vs errors vs saturation), and does it vary by region/provider?

---

## Ownership
- Incident owner: Rafael Mendes (SRE)
- Service owners: Jada Williams (Rollout controller), Chloe Martin (Console backend), Logan Wright (Telemetry)
- Follow-up tracking: Talia Benson (Program manager)

This postmortem is considered complete once P0 actions are implemented and validated in a game day with both “latency regression” and “monitor stale/missing” scenarios.
text:
## Summary
On 2026-02-17 we ran a controlled rollout (internal production route) of a new model serving profile for `redwood-api/chat-gateway` in `us-east-1` (Self-serve tier), moving traffic from **model version `rw-mistral-7b-instruct-2026.02.10`** (baseline) to **`rw-mistral-7b-instruct-2026.02.17`** (candidate).

During the canary step, the candidate experienced a **p99 latency regression** (tail latency increase) and a brief **5xx spike** under bursty traffic. We expected the rollout controller to auto-pause/auto-rollback based on the configured SLO monitors. No automated action occurred.

The on-call engineer manually paused the rollout after observing the degradation in Grafana. Impact was limited due to low canary percentage and quick manual mitigation, but the event revealed a critical gap: **the SLO monitor query used by the controller returned missing/stale data for the rollout scope**, and the controller treated this as “no breach” instead of “unsafe → stop rollout.”

This postmortem documents what happened, why auto-rollback didn’t trigger, and the corrective actions that directly feed the **Auto-rollback on SLO breach integration** project.

---

## Customer impact
- **Blast radius:** Internal production route (dogfood + a subset of low-risk Self-serve traffic in us-east-1).
- **Traffic affected:** Canary step at 5% of eligible traffic.
- **Observed symptoms:**
  - p99 latency increased from ~1.9s to ~2.6s on candidate traffic.
  - 5xx rate peaked at ~1.2% on candidate traffic for ~4 minutes.
- **User-visible impact:** Minor; a small number of elevated-latency requests and transient errors for canary traffic only.
- **SLA impact:** No SLA breach recorded; this was below paging thresholds for the broader service SLO.

---

## Detection
- **Primary detection:** Manual observation in Grafana by on-call (no paging).
- **Expected detection:** Rollout controller auto-pause/auto-rollback based on SLO burn-rate and error-rate monitors scoped to (route, model version, region, tier).
- **Why paging didn’t happen:** Alerts were configured for overall service SLOs, not rollout-scoped candidate vs baseline comparisons.

---

## Timeline (all times PT)
- **10:03** — Rollout `rollout_id=rl_2026_02_17_1003_chatgw_use1` initiated (baseline → candidate). Step 1 starts: 1% traffic.
- **10:10** — Step advances to 5% traffic (canary). No controller warnings.
- **10:14** — Tail latency begins to drift on candidate requests. p99 + ~250ms vs baseline.
- **10:18** — Candidate p99 reaches ~2.6s; brief saturation observed (GPU queue depth spikes).
- **10:20** — 5xx spike begins (candidate only), correlated with queue spikes.
- **10:22** — On-call notices divergence on per-version dashboard; posts to `#eng-oncall`.
- **10:25** — Manual pause executed in rollout controller.
- **10:28** — Traffic returns to baseline; latency normalizes.
- **10:41** — Initial investigation finds rollout controller shows “SLO evaluator: OK” for the entire interval.
- **11:15** — Deeper look reveals rollout-scoped SLO query returned “no data / stale,” but the controller treated it as non-breaching.
- **13:30** — Root cause confirmed: rollout-scoped monitor missing required labels + stale query cache path.
- **Next day** — Action items triaged into Auto-rollback SLO integration project and release gates.

---

## What happened (technical)
The rollout controller relies on SLO monitors to decide whether to:
1) continue to next step,
2) pause,
3) rollback to last known-good.

For this rollout, the controller attempted to query burn-rate + error-rate monitors for the following scope:
- service: `redwood-api`
- route: `POST /v1/chat/completions` (chat-gateway)
- region: `us-east-1`
- customer_tier: `self_serve`
- model_version: `rw-mistral-7b-instruct-2026.02.17`

However:
- The **latency burn-rate monitor for the rollout scope did not exist** (monitor definition lacked `model_version` as a dimension, and an automated monitor generator failed to create the per-version monitor for the new candidate).
- The SLO toolkit query path returned a cached response for a similar-but-not-identical scope (missing `model_version` label), and that cached response was **stale** (last datapoint timestamp older than our acceptable freshness window).
- The controller’s evaluation code path interpreted “missing/stale monitor data” as **`HEALTHY/UNKNOWN → allow continue`** rather than **`UNSAFE → stop rollout`**.

Net result: auto-mitigation logic never saw a “breach,” and no auto-pause or rollback occurred.

---

## Root cause
### Primary root cause
**Monitor coverage gap + unsafe handling of missing/stale monitor data in rollout evaluation.**

Specifically:
1) A rollout-scoped SLO monitor was missing for candidate traffic because the monitor generator did not create a per-`model_version` monitor for this route.
2) The controller did not treat missing/stale monitor responses as a gating failure (it effectively failed open).

### Contributing factors
- **Ambiguous semantics:** We did not have a documented, enforced rule for “monitor missing/stale → pause vs rollback vs continue.”
- **Insufficient auditability:** The controller UI/logs showed “SLO evaluator: OK” but did not capture that it was operating on missing/stale data.
- **No rollout-scoped alerts:** Our alerting focused on global service SLOs; rollout-specific divergence didn’t page or notify by default.
- **Label mismatch and cardinality caution:** Prior observability guidance discouraged high-cardinality labels, leading to uneven adoption of `model_version`-scoped monitors. We need explicit standards for what dimensions are required for rollout safety, with bounded cardinality strategies.

---

## Why auto-rollback did not trigger (explicit)
Auto-rollback did not trigger because **the evaluator never entered a “breach detected” state**.

Decision chain at the time (reconstructed):
1) Controller requests SLO status for rollout scope.
2) Monitor adapter returns response with `data_freshness=stale` and `monitor_found=false` (internally represented as `UNKNOWN`).
3) Evaluator treats `UNKNOWN` as “no breach” (rather than “cannot prove safe”).
4) Rollout controller continues/presents status as OK.

This is the core gap this project must close: **lack of guardrails around monitor presence/freshness, and lack of safe defaults**.

---

## What went well
- On-call caught the regression quickly via dashboard comparison and paused the rollout.
- Canary percentage limited the blast radius.
- Service owners and SRE were able to reconstruct the failure mode within the same day.
- The incident provided a concrete test case for the Auto-rollback integration PRD/design.

## What went wrong
- The controller effectively failed open when SLO signals were missing/stale.
- Monitor coverage for rollout-critical dimensions (route/model_version/region/tier) was not enforced.
- Logs/UI lacked the detail needed to understand the evaluator inputs (monitor IDs, freshness, sample sizes, query windows).
- No release gate prevented enabling auto-mitigation for a scope lacking the required monitors.

---

## Corrective actions
The following actions are prioritized to directly feed the **Auto-rollback on SLO breach integration** workstream.

### P0 (must-have before broad production enablement)
| Action | Owner | Due | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Define and implement evaluator semantics for `monitor_missing` / `monitor_stale` | Sean Gallagher / Jada Williams | 2026-03-15 | Default should be **pause rollout** (safe stop) with explicit suppression option; no automatic rollback without a valid signal. |
| Add audit trail fields to capture monitor inputs and freshness | Felix Schneider | 2026-03-22 | Include monitor IDs, query windows, last datapoint timestamp, sample sizes, policy version, and decision trace. |
| Add metrics + alerting for `monitor_missing_or_stale` on active rollouts | Aisha Bello / Allison Grant | 2026-03-22 | Notify (non-paging) by default; paging only if rollout is high-tier or >X% traffic. |
| Add release gate: “Required monitors exist + fresh” before enabling auto-rollback for a route/model/region/tier | Connor O'Brien / Benji Okafor | 2026-03-29 | Gate to be used by service teams and Redwood release train checklist. |

### P1 (hardening)
| Action | Owner | Due | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Monitor coverage audit for top 20 routes and all prod regions | Rafael Mendes | 2026-04-05 | Track owners + remediation dates; feed into coverage sheet and quarterly reliability review. |
| Implement minimum sample-size gating + dual-window burn-rate logic (fast/slow) | Jordan Lee | 2026-04-12 | Align with ADR-0127; reduce false positives and prevent oscillation. |
| E2E test: inject synthetic latency + simulate stale monitor responses | Leila Farouk | 2026-04-12 | Must verify: pause occurs, audit logs written, and UI shows “missing/stale” reason. |
| Update observability standard: required labels/dimensions for rollout safety and bounded cardinality guidance | Allison Grant | 2026-04-19 | Clarify when `model_version` is required and how to manage retention/rollups. |

### P2 (process and documentation)
| Action | Owner | Due | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Runbook update: triage steps for missing/stale monitors and override procedure | Kira Thompson / Rafael Mendes | 2026-04-26 | Include “how to verify monitor freshness,” “how to suppress with justification,” and “when to rollback manually.” |
| Game day scenario: stale monitor during real rollout | Rafael Mendes / Sean Gallagher | 2026-05-03 | Validate no fail-open paths remain and alerting routes correctly. |

---

## Follow-ups / design decisions captured
- We will codify the breach evaluation strategy (burn-rate windows, debounce/holdoff, minimum samples) in an ADR and ensure the controller behavior is deterministic and auditable. See: `ADR-0127`.
- We will standardize a rollout auto-action audit event schema (including monitor metadata, snapshots, and decision traces). See: `ADR-0128`.
- We will standardize metric names and labels for rollout evaluations and outcomes to support dashboards/alerts and avoid uncontrolled cardinality. See: `Rollouts SLO metrics naming and labeling`.

---

## Appendix
### A. Evidence and debugging notes (abridged)
- The SLO query for candidate scope returned `monitor_found=false` for latency burn-rate and an error-rate monitor response with `last_datapoint_age > 15m`.
- The controller UI displayed “SLO evaluator: OK” without showing `UNKNOWN` / staleness state.
- Grafana showed clear divergence in p99 for candidate traffic; the regression was strongly correlated with queue depth increases.

### B. Immediate mitigations applied during event
- Manual pause of rollout at 5% canary.
- Candidate removed from traffic; baseline restored.

### C. Open questions
- For high-tier traffic (Dedicated/Private), do we page on missing/stale monitors immediately when a rollout is active, or only after a grace period?
- What is the acceptable maximum data freshness lag per signal type (latency vs errors vs saturation), and does it vary by region/provider?

---

## Ownership
- Incident owner: Rafael Mendes (SRE)
- Service owners: Jada Williams (Rollout controller), Chloe Martin (Console backend), Logan Wright (Telemetry)
- Follow-up tracking: Talia Benson (Program manager)

This postmortem is considered complete once P0 actions are implemented and validated in a game day with both “latency regression” and “monitor stale/missing” scenarios.

source.title: Operator action grid and staged customer notification scripts
source.author: Maya Chen
source.body:
Summary:

This document defines an operator action grid that maps common detection signals to immediate runbook steps, time-to-recovery (TTR) targets, and a staged customer notification strategy. It includes ready-to-use status-page JSON, Tiered customer notice scripts, and short game-day exercises to validate operator speed and communication clarity. Use this as the canonical reference for model-serving degradations and silent/slow degradations where user-visible quality or latency regresses without an obvious outage.

Scope and intent:

- Scope: Redwood inference runtime components (request routing, KV cache, batching layer, model variants), API gateway, and customer-facing rate limiting and quota subsystems.
- Intent: accelerate containment, reduce confusion during multi-service incidents, and provide clear, tiered customer communications that scale from internal note to external status page updates.

Operator Action Grid (quick reference):

| Signal (example) | Severity | Immediate operator actions (first 10 minutes) | TTR target | Notification tier |
|------------------|----------|---------------------------------------------|------------|------------------|
| 5x 5xx spike from model-workers (>=5% of requests) | Sev-2 | 1) Open bridge; 2) Identify failing worker pool (gpu-worker-*); 3) Toggle fallback policy to degrade to lower-cost variant; 4) Add traffic-steering rule | 15–30m | Tier A: internal + affected enterprise customers |
| Sustained 200–500ms p50 latency increase for embedding endpoints | Sev-3 | 1) Check batching backlog and queue length; 2) Inspect KV cache hit ratio; 3) Recycle batcher pods if backlog > 3000; 4) Escalate to serving-runtime team if kernel OOMs | 30–90m | Tier B: internal + status page note + customer success outreach for high-touch customers |
| Silent quality drift detected by evals (BLEU/ROUGE drop, or hallucination rate > threshold) | Sev-2 | 1) Run diagnostic prompt-set evals against pinned model version; 2) Pin to previous model variant if quality delta > configured threshold; 3) Create regression ticket in Tracker with reproduction artifacts | 60m | Tier A + targeted CS outreach to customers with regression-sensitive SLAs |
| KV cache thrash with increased miss rate and cache eviction | Sev-3 | 1) Increase cache retention to temporary mode; 2) Purge known bad prefixes; 3) Restart cache nodes sequentially | 45–120m | Tier B |
| Rate-limit cascade from a noisy tenant (burst > 20x baseline) | Sev-2 | 1) Isolate tenant via tenant-rate-limiter; 2) Notify sales/CS; 3) If abuse, apply temporary quota throttle | 20–60m | Tier A + direct customer notice |

Primary runbook template (use for all Sev-2/Sev-3 incidents):

1) Acknowledge and open bridge (first 2 minutes)
   - Bridge tool: #ops-bridge (Slack) and Zoom link generated via /bridge create
   - Primary on-call: (name) — announce role and read the initial evidence from monitoring (metrics, recent deploys, alerts).
2) Rapid evidence collection (2–8 minutes)
   - Paste: relevant Grafana panels (request rate, p50/p95 latency, error rate), last 3 deploys, and tail of worker logs for implicated services.
   - Commands:
     - kubectl -n runtime logs -l app=gpu-worker --since=15m | grep -i error
     - curl -sS https://internal-metrics/api/now?service=model-serving | jq '.metrics'
3) Containment actions (8–20 minutes)
   - Route traffic to compatible variant: /admin/traffic-shift --service model-serving --from=variant-v2 --to=variant-v1 --percent=100
   - Scale up a healthy worker-pool: kubectl scale deploy gpu-worker-spot --replicas=8 -n runtime
   - If memory pressure: restart the busiest pod with kubectl delete pod <pod-name> -n runtime
4) Communication and customer notice (10–40 minutes, depending on tier)
   - Use the templates in the next section. Update status page payload (example below) and post to #status-updates Slack channel.
5) Root-cause analysis and rollback (40–120 minutes)
   - If deploy is implicated, perform an automated rollback via deployment pipeline and verify metrics for 15 minutes.
   - Create post-incident ticket and assign owners for follow-up actions.

Staged customer notification strategy:

- Tier A — High impact / high-touch customers (SLAs, enterprise contracts)
  - Trigger when: any Sev-1/Sev-2 incident that affects >1% of requests for enterprise customers or quality regressions impacting SLO-sensitive features.
  - Channels: direct email + CS phone call + dedicated Slack channel for customer.
  - Script (short form): "We are investigating an issue impacting model responses for your account. We have applied a temporary traffic reroute and expect stability within 30 minutes. You will receive updates every 20 minutes or on state changes. If this impacts production traffic, please reply to this thread and your CS contact will escalate."

- Tier B — Broad-but-non-critical incidents
  - Trigger when: latency/throughput regressions visible on status page but mitigated with routing or scaling.
  - Channels: public status page, scheduled emails to subscribed customers, and a short CS summary.
  - Script (short form): "We've detected elevated latencies on embedding endpoints and implemented traffic mitigation. We're continuing work to restore normal latencies. Next update in 30 minutes."

- Tier C — Internal-only incidents / low impact
  - Trigger when: local experimental clusters or developer-facing tools with minimal customer impact.
  - Channels: internal bridge, internal status notes only.

Status page payload examples (copy+paste):

- Minimal public update (Tier B):

{
  "component": "Embedding service",
  "status": "degraded_performance",
  "incident_id": "INC-2026-0456",
  "short_description": "Elevated latency on embedding endpoints",
  "updates": [
    {"time": "2026-03-02T10:15:00Z", "body": "Mitigation in progress: traffic routed to standby pool and batchers scaled."}
  ]
}

- Full customer notice (Tier A) — email template (subject + body):

Subject: Ongoing issue: model responses impacted for [customer-name] — update #1

Body:
Hello [Customer First Name],

We are investigating an incident impacting model responses for your account that began at 10:03 UTC. Our engineers have temporarily routed traffic to a compatible model variant and scaled worker capacity. We expect a mitigation window of ~30 minutes to fully stabilize.

What we are doing now:
- Rerouting traffic to compatible model variant
- Scaling worker pools and increasing KV cache retention
- Running targeted evals to ensure quality parity

Next update: in 20 minutes or on change.

If this incident affects features critical to your users, reply and we will prioritize a dedicated mitigation path.

Regards,
Redwood Systems Operations

Operator checklist (post-mitigation):

1) Confirm metrics returned to baseline for 3 consecutive 1-minute windows (p50/p95 latency and error rate)
2) Remove temporary traffic rules and re-enable automated fallback policies
3) Document the incident timeline with exact commands used and owners assigned
4) Open a postmortem issue with the following required artifacts:
   - Timeline (ISO timestamps), affected services, root cause hypothesis, RCA owner, mitigation commands, and a list of follow-up actions with owners and target dates

Postmortem rubric (minimum required sections):

- Summary (2–3 sentences): what happened and customer impact
- Timeline: minute-level events (first alert, detection method, containment steps, mitigation complete)
- Root cause analysis: evidence, why checks failed, and technical causes
- Contributing factors: tooling gaps, on-call fatigue, rollout policy mismatches
- Action items: prioritized, owner-assigned, and target date
- Validation plan: how to verify a fix (synthetic tests, gating in CI, runbook changes)

Game-day exercises (30–90 minute scenarios):

1) 30-minute silent-drift drill
   - Goal: detect quality drift from eval metrics, confirm alerting works end-to-end, and execute targeted rollback
   - Steps:
     a) Inject a synthetic increase in hallucination score into the eval harness (QA team creates simulated regression)
     b) Observe alert in the metrics channel
     c) Execute the runbook: pin to previous model, update status page, and contact affected CS leads
   - Scoring: detection (10 pts), containment (20 pts), customer comms (10 pts)

2) 60-minute multi-region throttle cascade
   - Goal: exercise rate-limiter isolation, status page updates, and cross-team coordination
   - Steps include: simulate noisy tenant, apply tenant-throttle, redirect traffic, escalate to SRE lead, and coordinate rollback of a bad config.

Runbook governance and ownership:

- Owners: "serving-runtime" team owns the action grid and runbook lines for batcher, kernel, and model variant operations. On-call SRE owns the notification cadence and status page payloads.
- Review cadence: quarterly review and after each Sev-2 incident. All runbook changes must be reviewed by at least one serving-runtime engineer and one SRE.

Metrics and success criteria:

- Mean time to acknowledge (MTTA) target: < 90 seconds for Sev-2 incidents.
- Mean time to mitigation (first stabilization action): < 20 minutes for Sev-2.
- Customer communication latency: initial Tier A customer notice within 10 minutes of bridge open.
- Postmortem completion: action items assigned within 72 hours; postmortem published within 7 days.

Appendix A — Quick command cheatsheet:

- Open bridge: /bridge create "model-serving incident" --severity Sev-2
- Scale worker pool: kubectl scale deploy gpu-worker-spot --replicas=8 -n runtime
- Pin model: curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "https://internal-api/serve/pin?service=model-serving&variant=variant-v1"
- Update status page (curl example):
  curl -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '@status-payload.json' https://status.internal/update -H "Authorization: Bearer $STATUS_TOKEN"

Appendix B — Example evidence collection template (to paste into bridge):

- Alerts triggered: [list alert ids]
- Grafana links: [insert links]
- Recent deploys: [commit ids + author]
- Top error snippets (last 15m): [logs]
- Immediate mitigation actions taken: [traffic-shift, scale, restart]

Appendix C — Common templates (copy and paste):

- Internal Slack update (first message):
  :rotating_light: Incident INC-2026-0456: Elevated 5xx rate on model-serving. Bridge: <link>. Primary: @oncall. Mitigation: routing to variant-v1 and scaling gpu-worker-spot.

- Customer SMS (short, Tier A):
  Redwood: We are investigating an issue affecting model responses. We've applied a temporary mitigation and will update within 20 minutes. Reply for high-priority assistance.

Related references:

- Incident lifecycle orchestration templates (link): /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-lifecycle-orchestration-templates-2025
- Triage for degraded latency and SLO regression (link): /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/triage-for-degraded-latency-and-slo-regression-2024

Notes and change log:

- 2026-01-12 (Maya Chen): Initial publish
- 2026-03-02 (Maya Chen): Clarified Tier definitions and added status page JSON examples
