  - Trigger when: latency/throughput regressions visible on status page but mitigated with routing or scaling.
  - Channels: public status page, scheduled emails to subscribed customers, and a short CS summary.
  - Script (short form): "We've detected elevated latencies on embedding endpoints and implemented traffic mitigation. We're continuing work to restore normal latencies. Next update in 30 minutes."

- Tier C — Internal-only incidents / low impact
  - Trigger when: local experimental clusters or developer-facing tools with minimal customer impact.
  - Channels: internal bridge, internal status notes only.

Status page payload examples (copy+paste):

- Minimal public update (Tier B):

{
  "component": "Embedding service",
  "status": "degraded_performance",
  "incident_id": "INC-2026-0456",
  "short_description": "Elevated latency on embedding endpoints",
  "updates": [
    {"time": "2026-03-02T10:15:00Z", "body": "Mitigation in progress: traffic routed to standby pool and batchers scaled."}
  ]
}

- Full customer notice (Tier A) — email template (subject + body):

Subject: Ongoing issue: model responses impacted for [customer-name] — update #1

Body:
Hello [Customer First Name],

We are investigating an incident impacting model responses for your account that began at 10:03 UTC. Our engineers have temporarily routed traffic to a compatible model variant and scaled worker capacity. We expect a mitigation window of ~30 minutes to fully stabilize.

What we are doing now:
- Rerouting traffic to compatible model variant
- Scaling worker pools and increasing KV cache retention
- Running targeted evals to ensure quality parity

Next update: in 20 minutes or on change.

If this incident affects features critical to your users, reply and we will prioritize a dedicated mitigation path.

Regards,
Redwood Systems Operations

Operator checklist (post-mitigation):

1) Confirm metrics returned to baseline for 3 consecutive 1-minute windows (p50/p95 latency and error rate)
2) Remove temporary traffic rules and re-enable automated fallback policies
3) Document the incident timeline with exact commands used and owners assigned
4) Open a postmortem issue with the following required artifacts:
   - Timeline (ISO timestamps), affected services, root cause hypothesis, RCA owner, mitigation commands, and a list of follow-up actions with owners and target dates

Postmortem rubric (minimum required sections):

- Summary (2–3 sentences): what happened and customer impact
- Timeline: minute-level events (first alert, detection method, containment steps, mitigation complete)
- Root cause analysis: evidence, why checks failed, and technical causes
- Contributing factors: tooling gaps, on-call fatigue, rollout policy mismatches
- Action items: prioritized, owner-assigned, and target date
- Validation plan: how to verify a fix (synthetic tests, gating in CI, runbook changes)

Game-day exercises (30–90 minute scenarios):

1) 30-minute silent-drift drill
   - Goal: detect quality drift from eval metrics, confirm alerting works end-to-end, and execute targeted rollback
   - Steps:
     a) Inject a synthetic increase in hallucination score into the eval harness (QA team creates simulated regression)
     b) Observe alert in the metrics channel
     c) Execute the runbook: pin to previous model, update status page, and contact affected CS leads
   - Scoring: detection (10 pts), containment (20 pts), customer comms (10 pts)

2) 60-minute multi-region throttle cascade
   - Goal: exercise rate-limiter isolation, status page updates, and cross-team coordination
   - Steps include: simulate noisy tenant, apply tenant-throttle, redirect traffic, escalate to SRE lead, and coordinate rollback of a bad config.

Runbook governance and ownership:

- Owners: "serving-runtime" team owns the action grid and runbook lines for batcher, kernel, and model variant operations. On-call SRE owns the notification cadence and status page payloads.
- Review cadence: quarterly review and after each Sev-2 incident. All runbook changes must be reviewed by at least one serving-runtime engineer and one SRE.

Metrics and success criteria:

- Mean time to acknowledge (MTTA) target: < 90 seconds for Sev-2 incidents.
- Mean time to mitigation (first stabilization action): < 20 minutes for Sev-2.
- Customer communication latency: initial Tier A customer notice within 10 minutes of bridge open.
- Postmortem completion: action items assigned within 72 hours; postmortem published within 7 days.

Appendix A — Quick command cheatsheet:

- Open bridge: /bridge create "model-serving incident" --severity Sev-2
- Scale worker pool: kubectl scale deploy gpu-worker-spot --replicas=8 -n runtime
- Pin model: curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "https://internal-api/serve/pin?service=model-serving&variant=variant-v1"
- Update status page (curl example):
  curl -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '@status-payload.json' https://status.internal/update -H "Authorization: Bearer $STATUS_TOKEN"

Appendix B — Example evidence collection template (to paste into bridge):

- Alerts triggered: [list alert ids]
- Grafana links: [insert links]
- Recent deploys: [commit ids + author]
- Top error snippets (last 15m): [logs]
- Immediate mitigation actions taken: [traffic-shift, scale, restart]

Appendix C — Common templates (copy and paste):

- Internal Slack update (first message):
  :rotating_light: Incident INC-2026-0456: Elevated 5xx rate on model-serving. Bridge: <link>. Primary: @oncall. Mitigation: routing to variant-v1 and scaling gpu-worker-spot.

- Customer SMS (short, Tier A):
  Redwood: We are investigating an issue affecting model responses. We've applied a temporary mitigation and will update within 20 minutes. Reply for high-priority assistance.

Related references:

- Incident lifecycle orchestration templates (link): /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-lifecycle-orchestration-templates-2025
- Triage for degraded latency and SLO regression (link): /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/triage-for-degraded-latency-and-slo-regression-2024

Notes and change log:

- 2026-01-12 (Maya Chen): Initial publish
- 2026-03-02 (Maya Chen): Clarified Tier definitions and added status page JSON examples
text:
Summary:

This document defines an operator action grid that maps common detection signals to immediate runbook steps, time-to-recovery (TTR) targets, and a staged customer notification strategy. It includes ready-to-use status-page JSON, Tiered customer notice scripts, and short game-day exercises to validate operator speed and communication clarity. Use this as the canonical reference for model-serving degradations and silent/slow degradations where user-visible quality or latency regresses without an obvious outage.

Scope and intent:

- Scope: Redwood inference runtime components (request routing, KV cache, batching layer, model variants), API gateway, and customer-facing rate limiting and quota subsystems.
- Intent: accelerate containment, reduce confusion during multi-service incidents, and provide clear, tiered customer communications that scale from internal note to external status page updates.

Operator Action Grid (quick reference):

| Signal (example) | Severity | Immediate operator actions (first 10 minutes) | TTR target | Notification tier |
|------------------|----------|---------------------------------------------|------------|------------------|
| 5x 5xx spike from model-workers (>=5% of requests) | Sev-2 | 1) Open bridge; 2) Identify failing worker pool (gpu-worker-*); 3) Toggle fallback policy to degrade to lower-cost variant; 4) Add traffic-steering rule | 15–30m | Tier A: internal + affected enterprise customers |
| Sustained 200–500ms p50 latency increase for embedding endpoints | Sev-3 | 1) Check batching backlog and queue length; 2) Inspect KV cache hit ratio; 3) Recycle batcher pods if backlog > 3000; 4) Escalate to serving-runtime team if kernel OOMs | 30–90m | Tier B: internal + status page note + customer success outreach for high-touch customers |
| Silent quality drift detected by evals (BLEU/ROUGE drop, or hallucination rate > threshold) | Sev-2 | 1) Run diagnostic prompt-set evals against pinned model version; 2) Pin to previous model variant if quality delta > configured threshold; 3) Create regression ticket in Tracker with reproduction artifacts | 60m | Tier A + targeted CS outreach to customers with regression-sensitive SLAs |
| KV cache thrash with increased miss rate and cache eviction | Sev-3 | 1) Increase cache retention to temporary mode; 2) Purge known bad prefixes; 3) Restart cache nodes sequentially | 45–120m | Tier B |
| Rate-limit cascade from a noisy tenant (burst > 20x baseline) | Sev-2 | 1) Isolate tenant via tenant-rate-limiter; 2) Notify sales/CS; 3) If abuse, apply temporary quota throttle | 20–60m | Tier A + direct customer notice |

Primary runbook template (use for all Sev-2/Sev-3 incidents):

1) Acknowledge and open bridge (first 2 minutes)
   - Bridge tool: #ops-bridge (Slack) and Zoom link generated via /bridge create
   - Primary on-call: (name) — announce role and read the initial evidence from monitoring (metrics, recent deploys, alerts).
2) Rapid evidence collection (2–8 minutes)
   - Paste: relevant Grafana panels (request rate, p50/p95 latency, error rate), last 3 deploys, and tail of worker logs for implicated services.
   - Commands:
     - kubectl -n runtime logs -l app=gpu-worker --since=15m | grep -i error
     - curl -sS https://internal-metrics/api/now?service=model-serving | jq '.metrics'
3) Containment actions (8–20 minutes)
   - Route traffic to compatible variant: /admin/traffic-shift --service model-serving --from=variant-v2 --to=variant-v1 --percent=100
   - Scale up a healthy worker-pool: kubectl scale deploy gpu-worker-spot --replicas=8 -n runtime
   - If memory pressure: restart the busiest pod with kubectl delete pod <pod-name> -n runtime
4) Communication and customer notice (10–40 minutes, depending on tier)
   - Use the templates in the next section. Update status page payload (example below) and post to #status-updates Slack channel.
5) Root-cause analysis and rollback (40–120 minutes)
   - If deploy is implicated, perform an automated rollback via deployment pipeline and verify metrics for 15 minutes.
   - Create post-incident ticket and assign owners for follow-up actions.

Staged customer notification strategy:

- Tier A — High impact / high-touch customers (SLAs, enterprise contracts)
  - Trigger when: any Sev-1/Sev-2 incident that affects >1% of requests for enterprise customers or quality regressions impacting SLO-sensitive features.
  - Channels: direct email + CS phone call + dedicated Slack channel for customer.
  - Script (short form): "We are investigating an issue impacting model responses for your account. We have applied a temporary traffic reroute and expect stability within 30 minutes. You will receive updates every 20 minutes or on state changes. If this impacts production traffic, please reply to this thread and your CS contact will escalate."

- Tier B — Broad-but-non-critical incidents
  - Trigger when: latency/throughput regressions visible on status page but mitigated with routing or scaling.
  - Channels: public status page, scheduled emails to subscribed customers, and a short CS summary.
  - Script (short form): "We've detected elevated latencies on embedding endpoints and implemented traffic mitigation. We're continuing work to restore normal latencies. Next update in 30 minutes."

- Tier C — Internal-only incidents / low impact
  - Trigger when: local experimental clusters or developer-facing tools with minimal customer impact.
  - Channels: internal bridge, internal status notes only.

Status page payload examples (copy+paste):

- Minimal public update (Tier B):

{
  "component": "Embedding service",
  "status": "degraded_performance",
  "incident_id": "INC-2026-0456",
  "short_description": "Elevated latency on embedding endpoints",
  "updates": [
    {"time": "2026-03-02T10:15:00Z", "body": "Mitigation in progress: traffic routed to standby pool and batchers scaled."}
  ]
}

- Full customer notice (Tier A) — email template (subject + body):

Subject: Ongoing issue: model responses impacted for [customer-name] — update #1

Body:
Hello [Customer First Name],

We are investigating an incident impacting model responses for your account that began at 10:03 UTC. Our engineers have temporarily routed traffic to a compatible model variant and scaled worker capacity. We expect a mitigation window of ~30 minutes to fully stabilize.

What we are doing now:
- Rerouting traffic to compatible model variant
- Scaling worker pools and increasing KV cache retention
- Running targeted evals to ensure quality parity

Next update: in 20 minutes or on change.

If this incident affects features critical to your users, reply and we will prioritize a dedicated mitigation path.

Regards,
Redwood Systems Operations

Operator checklist (post-mitigation):

1) Confirm metrics returned to baseline for 3 consecutive 1-minute windows (p50/p95 latency and error rate)
2) Remove temporary traffic rules and re-enable automated fallback policies
3) Document the incident timeline with exact commands used and owners assigned
4) Open a postmortem issue with the following required artifacts:
   - Timeline (ISO timestamps), affected services, root cause hypothesis, RCA owner, mitigation commands, and a list of follow-up actions with owners and target dates

Postmortem rubric (minimum required sections):

- Summary (2–3 sentences): what happened and customer impact
- Timeline: minute-level events (first alert, detection method, containment steps, mitigation complete)
- Root cause analysis: evidence, why checks failed, and technical causes
- Contributing factors: tooling gaps, on-call fatigue, rollout policy mismatches
- Action items: prioritized, owner-assigned, and target date
- Validation plan: how to verify a fix (synthetic tests, gating in CI, runbook changes)

Game-day exercises (30–90 minute scenarios):

1) 30-minute silent-drift drill
   - Goal: detect quality drift from eval metrics, confirm alerting works end-to-end, and execute targeted rollback
   - Steps:
     a) Inject a synthetic increase in hallucination score into the eval harness (QA team creates simulated regression)
     b) Observe alert in the metrics channel
     c) Execute the runbook: pin to previous model, update status page, and contact affected CS leads
   - Scoring: detection (10 pts), containment (20 pts), customer comms (10 pts)

2) 60-minute multi-region throttle cascade
   - Goal: exercise rate-limiter isolation, status page updates, and cross-team coordination
   - Steps include: simulate noisy tenant, apply tenant-throttle, redirect traffic, escalate to SRE lead, and coordinate rollback of a bad config.

Runbook governance and ownership:

- Owners: "serving-runtime" team owns the action grid and runbook lines for batcher, kernel, and model variant operations. On-call SRE owns the notification cadence and status page payloads.
- Review cadence: quarterly review and after each Sev-2 incident. All runbook changes must be reviewed by at least one serving-runtime engineer and one SRE.

Metrics and success criteria:

- Mean time to acknowledge (MTTA) target: < 90 seconds for Sev-2 incidents.
- Mean time to mitigation (first stabilization action): < 20 minutes for Sev-2.
- Customer communication latency: initial Tier A customer notice within 10 minutes of bridge open.
- Postmortem completion: action items assigned within 72 hours; postmortem published within 7 days.

Appendix A — Quick command cheatsheet:

- Open bridge: /bridge create "model-serving incident" --severity Sev-2
- Scale worker pool: kubectl scale deploy gpu-worker-spot --replicas=8 -n runtime
- Pin model: curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" "https://internal-api/serve/pin?service=model-serving&variant=variant-v1"
- Update status page (curl example):
  curl -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '@status-payload.json' https://status.internal/update -H "Authorization: Bearer $STATUS_TOKEN"

Appendix B — Example evidence collection template (to paste into bridge):

- Alerts triggered: [list alert ids]
- Grafana links: [insert links]
- Recent deploys: [commit ids + author]
- Top error snippets (last 15m): [logs]
- Immediate mitigation actions taken: [traffic-shift, scale, restart]

Appendix C — Common templates (copy and paste):

- Internal Slack update (first message):
  :rotating_light: Incident INC-2026-0456: Elevated 5xx rate on model-serving. Bridge: <link>. Primary: @oncall. Mitigation: routing to variant-v1 and scaling gpu-worker-spot.

- Customer SMS (short, Tier A):
  Redwood: We are investigating an issue affecting model responses. We've applied a temporary mitigation and will update within 20 minutes. Reply for high-priority assistance.

Related references:

- Incident lifecycle orchestration templates (link): /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-lifecycle-orchestration-templates-2025
- Triage for degraded latency and SLO regression (link): /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/triage-for-degraded-latency-and-slo-regression-2024

source.title: P0 Incident Postmortem: Dedicated cluster routing loop causes elevated latency
source.author: Logan Wright
source.body:
## Summary
On 2026-02-20, one dedicated customer cluster experienced a routing loop between the edge router and the regional policy router after a policy update. This caused request amplification, elevated tail latency, and intermittent 503s for ~19 minutes until policy rollback.

## Severity
P0

## Customer impact
- Impacted: 1 dedicated customer environment in us-west (production).
- Symptoms: p99 latency increased from ~1.2s to > 18s; peak 503 rate 11%.

## Timeline (UTC)
- 05:14: Policy update applied (new fallback rule).
- 05:18: Latency alerts fire; on-call engaged.
- 05:25: Suspected loop; rollback initiated.
- 05:33: Metrics normalize.

## Root cause
A misconfigured fallback route allowed traffic to be re-ingested by the edge router with no loop detection. A recent refactor removed the hop-count header from dedicated routing.

## Detection
Detected via dedicated SLO alerting (latency + 5xx).

## Follow-up action items (count these)
1) Re-introduce hop-count header and enforce max hops (drop with clear error) across dedicated routing. Owning team: Eng Platform.
2) Add loop detection metric and alert (route_reingest_rate > 0.1%). Owning team: Eng SRE.
3) Update dedicated policy editor UI to validate fallback routes for potential self-targeting. Owning team: Eng Platform.
4) Add a dedicated routing integration test that simulates fallback chains and asserts no re-ingest. Owning team: Eng Platform.
5) Document dedicated routing headers (including hop-count) in internal routing spec. Owning team: Eng Platform.

## Lessons learned
- Dedicated routing needs stronger guardrails because misconfigurations have outsized effects.

source.title: Dependency Failure Containment and Customer Communication Cookbook
source.author: Maya Ortega
source.content:
Summary:
This cookbook describes standard procedures for containing, mitigating, and communicating incidents caused by external dependencies (third-party APIs, cloud managed services, vendor models, and shared infra components). It focuses on rapid containment patterns, safe degradations, and customer-facing communications. The goal is fast, predictable response with clear ownership and minimal customer surprise.

Scope:
- Applies to incidents where a Redwood customer-visible failure is caused primarily by a non-Redwood dependency (e.g., upstream model host, cloud DB region, vendor auth provider, CDN outage).
- Does NOT replace generic service outage runbooks; it augments them with dependency-specific checks, routing options, and communication templates.
- Intended audience: on-call SREs, service owners, incident commanders, and customer success leads.

TL;DR Playbook (one-page):
1) Detect: confirm alerts and customer signals (pager, dashboards, support queue). Note root-suspect dependency.
2) Contain: apply traffic routing & circuit breakers; switch to cached responses or degraded mode; disable non-critical feature paths.
3) Communicate: publish a short-status page message and notify customer success for high-impact customers. Use the "Partial degraded experience" template (see below).
4) Mitigate: apply vendor workarounds (retry policies, alternate endpoints), escalate to vendor/partner.
5) Restore: re-enable normal routing once metrics recover for min-stability window (see thresholds).
6) Post-incident: capture timeline, evidence (metrics/traces/logs), and owner action items; schedule a dependent-party RCA with vendor if needed.

Detection and Triage:
- Primary signals:
  - Service 5xx spike correlated with a single upstream hostname or SDK (tagged in traces).
  - P95/P99 latency jump on specific call site (example: /models/embeddings endpoint to vendor X).
  - Customer support tickets referencing new errors or timeouts.
- Quick triage checklist (first 10 minutes):
  1. Confirm alert and scope (regions, clusters, customer segments).
  2. Identify upstream hostnames/IPs and SDK version.
  3. Check circuit-breaker metrics and existing retry logs.
  4. Look for vendor status page updates or security advisories.
  5. If high-impact (SLO-blow or large customer swarm), declare an incident and assign IC.

Initial responder checklist (immediate actions):
- Mark affected services and set incident priority.
- Gather key artifacts: one representative failing request/trace, recent deploys, configuration changes.
- Flip read-only toggles if applicable.
- Rate-limit or disable background jobs making heavy upstream calls.
- Enable increased logging around dependency call sites (short-lived).

Containment patterns (choose based on topology):
1) Fail-fast/circuit-breaker: open the circuit for the dependency when error rate > 5% for 2 minutes. Return graceful fallback or cached response.
2) Regional re-routing: if only one cloud region affected, shift traffic to unaffected region (with capacity guardrails).
3) Graceful degradation: turn off non-essential features that rely on the dependency (e.g., re-ranking or non-critical embeddings).
4) Local cache fallback: serve from last-known-good embeddings/search index for up to 30 minutes.
5) Rate limiting & admission control: reduce per-user token budget for generation features to preserve throughput.
6) Alternate vendor endpoint: switch to known-compatible model variant or vendor endpoint if validated during runbook authoring.

Decision matrix (short):
- If >10% customers affected OR SLO breach: containment + status page + CSM notify + vendor escalation.
- If <10% and degraded only for non-critical ops: monitoring and vendor ticket; update customers if it persists >30m.

Customer-facing comms templates
- Status page short (initial):
  "Summary: We are investigating increased error rates for [service-name] that appear related to an external partner (vendorX). Impact: some API requests may return errors or experience increased latency. Mitigation: we've applied temporary traffic controls and fallback paths. Next update: in 15 minutes."
- Status page update (post-containment):
  "Update: We have implemented a failover and reduced feature surface to limit customer-facing errors. Error rate down from 18% to 3% in the last 10 minutes. We continue to monitor and are working with vendorX. ETA for full restoration: unknown; next update in 30 minutes or sooner."
- Customer success email (high-impact customers):
  Subject: Incident affecting [service-name] — current status
  Body: Hello [CustomerName], we are currently investigating an issue affecting [service-name] that may impact [exact features]. We have applied temporary mitigations (failover/degraded mode). Our engineering team is actively working with the upstream provider (vendorX). We will provide a technical timeline and postmortem after resolution. If you require a workaround or status call, reply to this thread and we'll prioritize.
- Internal incident Slack snippet (IC → #oncall):
  "INCIDENT: Dependency degradation on [service-name]. Vendor: vendorX. Impact: ~20% error rate for customers in us-east-1. Containment actions: opened circuit, enabled local cache, reduced model precision. IC: @<name>. Contact: @CSM-Team for VIP customers."

Internal comms and vendor escalation checklist:
1. Collect evidence: representative request IDs, trace IDs, timestamps, SDK versions, and full error payloads.
2. Open vendor ticket: include rate graphs, p95/p99 latency, sample traces, and a clear desired action (e.g., "please confirm whether your control plane reported quota throttles for our API key between T1 and T2").
3. Share incident channel link and SRE contact with vendor escalation engineer.
4. If vendor can't respond within SLA and customer impact is severe, trigger backup plan (alternate vendor or model variant).

SLO / Metrics to record in incident timeline:
- Error rate for affected RPC (1m/5m/15m).
- p95/p99 latency before and after containment.
- Fraction of requests served from cache/fallback.
- Customer seats affected and list of enterprise customers with errors.
- Duration from detection to containment and to full restore.

Restoration criteria and stability window:
- Restoration considered successful when:
  1. Error rate < SLO-threshold for 15 continuous minutes (default: error rate <= 1% for user-facing RPCs) AND
  2. Latency p95 within 20% of baseline for 15 continuous minutes.
- Post-restore steps: gradually remove rate-limits and feature toggles over 30–60 minutes while monitoring.

Post-incident responsibilities (30/60/90 day actions):
- 0–3 days: IC writes timeline and preliminary RCA draft; notify customer success for impacted accounts.
- 3–14 days: schedule vendor RCA call; collect vendor evidence and ticket closure.
- 14–30 days: implement mitigations (circuit-breaker tuning, cache TTL changes, model fallback path testing).
- 30–90 days: schedule a game day to rehearse the same failure mode and validate automation.

Postmortem template (starter fields):
- Title, date, IC and authors.
- Summary (one-paragraph impact).
- Timeline (minute-granular for first hour, 5–15 minute thereafter).
- Root cause hypothesis and evidence.
- Customer impact and severity classification.
- Corrective actions (owners + ETA).
- Preventive actions and verification plan.
- Vendor follow-up and SLA changes (if any).

On-call rotation and ownership notes:
- Primary on-call: owns rapid containment actions and status page updates.
- Secondary: vendor escalations and CSM handoffs.
- Engineering lead (post-incident): leads RCA and remediation prioritization.
- Rationale: keep action items close to the code owners; keep vendor comms centralized through a rotated escalation owner.

Game day scenarios (playbook exercises):
1) Vendor auth service returns 403 for 50% of requests for 30 minutes: practice failover to cached tokens and vendor ticket escalation.
2) Cloud region DB latency spikes causing model store timeouts: practice regional reroute and degraded-mode ranking.
3) Embedding vendor goes into degraded throughput: rehearse switch to low-cost local embedding quantization profile + customer notice cadence.

Example runbook snippets and commands:
- Toggle a circuit breaker (k8s annotation example):
  kubectl annotate deployment service-a rdb-circuit-breaker=open --overwrite
- Re-route traffic (Istio route weight example):
  kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
  apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1alpha3
  kind: VirtualService
  metadata:
    name: service-a-routing
  spec:
    hosts: ["service-a.internal"]
    http:
    - route:
      - destination:
          host: service-a-primary
          subset: v1
        weight: 0
      - destination:
          host: service-a-secondary
          subset: v1
        weight: 100
  EOF
- Sample status page JSON for short update:
  {
    "component": "Model Embeddings API",
    "status": "degraded_performance",
    "message": "We are investigating elevated error rates likely due to vendorX. Temporary mitigations are in place."
  }

Thresholds and configuration table (reference):
- Error-rate-open-circuit: 5% sustained for 2 minutes
- Cache-TTL-fallback: 30 minutes by default
- Min-stability-window-before-restore: 15 minutes continuous
- Customer-notify-threshold: >10% customers impacted OR >5 enterprise accounts affected

Ownership and contact matrix:
- Service owner: @service-a-team (oncall rotation link: https://pager/rosters/service-a)
- SRE escalation: @eng-sre-oncall (primary phone tree)
- CSM lead for VIPs: Customer Success team rotation (contact: csm-rotation@redwood.com)

Guidance for vendor contracts and SLAs:
- For critical dependencies, contractually require: 24/7 escalation path, dedicated escalation engineer for P1s, and uptime SLA with credit terms.
- Track vendor incident frequency and renegotiate if recurring incidents exceed N per quarter.

Appendix: sample escalation message to vendor
Subject: URGENT: Production errors from Redwood account [account-id] — request immediate escalation
Body:
We are experiencing production errors impacting multiple customers. Evidence attached: error logs, trace IDs, timestamps. Impact: 20% of API calls failing in us-east-1. Please escalate to your on-call escalation engineer and confirm whether this is caused by quota throttling, control plane issue, or a degradation in downstream service. Required: callback within 15 minutes and summary within 60 minutes.

Appendix: Game day checklist for exercise facilitators
- Pre-game: pick a target dependency and failure mode; create simulated vendor silence or degraded responses.
- During: measure detection time, containment actions, and status page latency.
- Post: score the run on RTO, communication clarity, and completeness of postmortem artifacts.
- Follow-up: convert findings into 1–3 prioritized action items and assign owners.

Related reading and links:
- Runbook governance and post-incident cadence (policy)
- Incident communication and postmortem framework
- Vendor management playbook and SLA negotiation checklist

title: Dependency Failure Containment and Customer Communication Cookbook
preview:
Summary:
This cookbook describes standard procedures for containing, mitigating, and communicating incidents caused by external dependencies (third-party APIs, cloud managed services, vendor models, and shared infra components). It focuses on rapid containment patterns, safe degradations, and customer-facing communications. The goal is fast, predictable response with clear ownership and minimal customer surprise.

Scope:
- Applies to incidents where a Redwood customer-visible failure is caused primarily by a non-Redwood dependency (e.g., upstream model host, cloud DB region, vendor auth provider, CDN outage).
- Does NOT replace generic service outage runbooks; it augments them with dependency-specific checks, routing options, and communication templates.
- Intended audience: on-call SREs, service owners, incident commanders, and customer success leads.

TL;DR Playbook (one-page):
1) Detect: confirm alerts and customer signals (pager, dashboards, support queue). Note root-suspect dependency.
2) Contain: apply traffic routing & circuit breakers; switch to cached responses or degraded mode; disable non-critical feature paths.
3) Communicate: publish a short-status page message and notify customer success for high-impact customers. Use the "Partial degraded experience" template (see below).
4) Mitigate: apply vendor workarounds (retry policies, alternate endpoints), escalate to vendor/partner.
5) Restore: re-enable normal routing once metrics recover for min-stability window (see thresholds).
6) Post-incident: capture timeline, evidence (metrics/traces/logs), and owner action items; schedule a dependent-party RCA with vendor if needed.

Detection and Triage:
- Primary signals:
  - Service 5xx spike correlated with a single upstream hostname or SDK (tagged in traces).
  - P95/P99 latency jump on specific call site (example: /models/embeddings endpoint to vendor X).
  - Customer support tickets referencing new errors or timeouts.
- Quick triage checklist (first 10 minutes):
  1. Confirm alert and scope (regions, clusters, customer segments).
  2. Identify upstream hostnames/IPs and SDK version.
  3. Check circuit-breaker metrics and existing retry logs.
  4. Look for vendor status page updates or security advisories.
  5. If high-impact (SLO-blow or large customer swarm), declare an incident and assign IC.

Initial responder checklist (immediate actions):
- Mark affected services and set incident priority.
- Gather key artifacts: one representative failing request/trace, recent deploys, configuration changes.
- Flip read-only toggles if applicable.
- Rate-limit or disable background jobs making heavy upstream calls.
- Enable increased logging around dependency call sites (short-lived).

Containment patterns (choose based on topology):
1) Fail-fast/circuit-breaker: open the circuit for the dependency when error rate > 5% for 2 minutes. Return graceful fallback or cached response.
2) Regional re-routing: if only one cloud region affected, shift traffic to unaffected region (with capacity guardrails).
3) Graceful degradation: turn off non-essential features that rely on the dependency (e.g., re-ranking or non-critical embeddings).
4) Local cache fallback: serve from last-known-good embeddings/search index for up to 30 minutes.
5) Rate limiting & admission control: reduce per-user token budget for generation features to preserve throughput.
6) Alternate vendor endpoint: switch to known-compatible model variant or vendor endpoint if validated during runbook authoring.

Decision matrix (short):
- If >10% customers affected OR SLO breach: containment + status page + CSM notify + vendor escalation.
- If <10% and degraded only for non-critical ops: monitoring and vendor ticket; update customers if it persists >30m.

Customer-facing comms templates
- Status page short (initial):
  "Summary: We are investigating increased error rates for [service-name] that appear related to an external partner (vendorX). Impact: some API requests may return errors or experience increased latency. Mitigation: we've applied temporary traffic controls and fallback paths. Next update: in 15 minutes."
- Status page update (post-containment):
  "Update: We have implemented a failover and reduced feature surface to limit customer-facing errors. Error rate down from 18% to 3% in the last 10 minutes. We continue to monitor and are working with vendorX. ETA for full restoration: unknown; next update in 30 minutes or sooner."
- Customer success email (high-impact customers):
  Subject: Incident affecting [service-name] — current status
  Body: Hello [CustomerName], we are currently investigating an issue affecting [service-name] that may impact [exact features]. We have applied temporary mitigations (failover/degraded mode). Our engineering team is actively working with the upstream provider (vendorX). We will provide a technical timeline and postmortem after resolution. If you require a workaround or status call, reply to this thread and we'll prioritize.
- Internal incident Slack snippet (IC → #oncall):
  "INCIDENT: Dependency degradation on [service-name]. Vendor: vendorX. Impact: ~20% error rate for customers in us-east-1. Containment actions: opened circuit, enabled local cache, reduced model precision. IC: @<name>. Contact: @CSM-Team for VIP customers."

Internal comms and vendor escalation checklist:
1. Collect evidence: representative request IDs, trace IDs, timestamps, SDK versions, and full error payloads.
2. Open vendor ticket: include rate graphs, p95/p99 latency, sample traces, and a clear desired action (e.g., "please confirm whether your control plane reported quota throttles for our API key between T1 and T2").
3. Share incident channel link and SRE contact with vendor escalation engineer.
4. If vendor can't respond within SLA and customer impact is severe, trigger backup plan (alternate vendor or model variant).

SLO / Metrics to record in incident timeline:
- Error rate for affected RPC (1m/5m/15m).
- p95/p99 latency before and after containment.
- Fraction of requests served from cache/fallback.
- Customer seats affected and list of enterprise customers with errors.
- Duration from detection to containment and to full restore.

Restoration criteria and stability window:
- Restoration considered successful when:
  1. Error rate < SLO-threshold for 15 continuous minutes (default: error rate <= 1% for user-facing RPCs) AND
  2. Latency p95 within 20% of baseline for 15 continuous minutes.
- Post-restore steps: gradually remove rate-limits and feature toggles over 30–60 minutes while monitoring.

Post-incident responsibilities (30/60/90 day actions):
- 0–3 days: IC writes timeline and preliminary RCA draft; notify customer success for impacted accounts.
- 3–14 days: schedule vendor RCA call; collect vendor evidence and ticket closure.
- 14–30 days: implement mitigations (circuit-breaker tuning, cache TTL changes, model fallback path testing).
- 30–90 days: schedule a game day to rehearse the same failure mode and validate automation.

Postmortem template (starter fields):
- Title, date, IC and authors.
- Summary (one-paragraph impact).
- Timeline (minute-granular for first hour, 5–15 minute thereafter).
- Root cause hypothesis and evidence.
- Customer impact and severity classification.
- Corrective actions (owners + ETA).
- Preventive actions and verification plan.
- Vendor follow-up and SLA changes (if any).

On-call rotation and ownership notes:
- Primary on-call: owns rapid containment actions and status page updates.
- Secondary: vendor escalations and CSM handoffs.
- Engineering lead (post-incident): leads RCA and remediation prioritization.
- Rationale: keep action items close to the code owners; keep vendor comms centralized through a rotated escalation owner.

Game day scenarios (playbook exercises):
1) Vendor auth service returns 403 for 50% of requests for 30 minutes: practice failover to cached tokens and vendor ticket escalation.
2) Cloud region DB latency spikes causing model store timeouts: practice regional reroute and degraded-mode ranking.
3) Embedding vendor goes into degraded throughput: rehearse switch to low-cost local embedding quantization profile + customer notice cadence.

Example runbook snippets and commands:
- Toggle a circuit breaker (k8s annotation example):
  kubectl annotate deployment service-a rdb-circuit-breaker=open --overwrite
- Re-route traffic (Istio route weight example):
  kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
  apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1alpha3
  kind: VirtualService
  metadata:
    name: service-a-routing
  spec:
    hosts: ["service-a.internal"]
    http:
    - route:
      - destination:
          host: service-a-primary
          subset: v1
        weight: 0
      - destination:
          host: service-a-secondary
          subset: v1
        weight: 100
  EOF
- Sample status page JSON for short update:
  {
    "component": "Model Embeddings API",
    "status": "degraded_performance",
    "message": "We are investigating elevated error rates likely due to vendorX. Temporary mitigations are in place."
  }

Thresholds and configuration table (reference):
- Error-rate-open-circuit: 5% sustained for 2 minutes
- Cache-TTL-fallback: 30 minutes by default
- Min-stability-window-before-restore: 15 minutes continuous
- Customer-notify-threshold: >10% customers impacted OR >5 enterprise accounts affected

Ownership and contact matrix:
- Service owner: @service-a-team (oncall rotation link: https://pager/rosters/service-a)
- SRE escalation: @eng-sre-oncall (primary phone tree)
- CSM lead for VIPs: Customer Success team rotation (contact: csm-rotation@redwood.com)

Guidance for vendor contracts and SLAs:
- For critical dependencies, contractually require: 24/7 escalation path, dedicated escalation engineer for P1s, and uptime SLA with credit terms.
- Track vendor incident frequency and renegotiate if recurring incidents exceed N per quarter.

Appendix: sample escalation message to vendor
Subject: URGENT: Production errors from Redwood account [account-id] — request immediate escalation
Body:
We are experiencing production errors impacting multiple customers. Evidence attached: error logs, trace IDs, timestamps. Impact: 20% of API calls failing in us-east-1. Please escalate to your on-call escalation engineer and confirm whether this is caused by quota throttling, control plane issue, or a degradation in downstream service. Required: callback within 15 minutes and summary within 60 minutes.

Appendix: Game day checklist for exercise facilitators
- Pre-game: pick a target dependency and failure mode; create simulated vendor silence or degraded responses.
- During: measure detection time, containment actions, and status page latency.
- Post: score the run on RTO, communication clarity, and completeness of postmortem artifacts.
- Follow-up: convert findings into 1–3 prioritized action items and assign owners.

Related reading and links:
- Runbook governance and post-incident cadence (policy)
- Incident communication and postmortem framework
- Vendor management playbook and SLA negotiation checklist

source.title: Postmortem: SEV-2 Console rollout UI bug triggered rollback loop (2026-02-22)
source.author: Ava Chen
source.customer_impact: Operators could not reliably pause or promote canary rollouts via Console. Some actions displayed as successful but were not applied, leading to confusion and delayed mitigations for unrelated rollouts.
source.summary: A Console UI change caused repeated client-side retries on a non-idempotent rollback endpoint, putting rollouts-service into a rollback loop for a subset of deployments.
source.timeline:
19:05 UTC: Internal user reported Console rollout controls unresponsive.
19:10 UTC: Eng on-call saw elevated 5xx and increased write QPS to rollouts-service.
19:20 UTC: Isolated to Console release 2026.02.22.2 with a new retry wrapper.
19:28 UTC: Disabled feature flag for retry wrapper in prod.
19:40 UTC: Rollouts-service write QPS returned to normal; rollback loops stopped.
19:58 UTC: Incident resolved.
source.root_cause: The Console’s new retry wrapper treated 409 conflict responses as retryable and retried POST /rollback automatically. The endpoint was not idempotent and could re-trigger rollback actions.
source.detection: Internal report and rollouts-service error/QPS alerts.
source.mitigation: Disabled the retry wrapper via feature flag and added a server-side idempotency key requirement for rollback actions.
source.what_went_well: Feature flag allowed immediate mitigation. Service-level alerts were actionable.
source.what_went_wrong: The UI change was not tested against the real rollouts-service conflict behavior; endpoint idempotency was assumed but not documented.
follow_ups: Document idempotency requirements for rollouts-service endpoints (Platform Engineering; due 2026-03-10).
Add integration tests in Console CI that simulate 409 and ensure no retries on non-idempotent endpoints (Console & Tooling; due 2026-03-15).
Introduce a standard client retry policy library with an allowlist of safe endpoints (DevEx; due 2026-04-01).

source.title: Status timeline automation spec and template library
source.author: Ava Chen
source.body:
Overview

This page is the canonical library for: (A) structured incident status timelines used by Redwood Inference responder teams and (B) the automation specification for generating and publishing those timelines to the status page, internal Slack bridge, and customer notification channels. The goal is fast, consistent, auditable customer communications that map to incident phases and operator actions.

Who should use this

- On-call SREs and service owners who publish incident updates
- Incident bridge operators and communications owners
- Automation engineers who implement status page webhooks and templates
- Customer Success for pre-approved messaging when high-impact incidents occur

Principles

1) Single source of truth: the status timeline generator (STG) must accept a single incident payload (incident_id) and emit consistent updates to all channels.
2) Phase-first messaging: timeline entries follow discrete phases (Detect, Ack, Mitigate, Customer Notice, Resolution, Retro).
3) Minimal friction: template variables are short and human-editable; automated drafts must be editable before publish.
4) Auditability: every published update must include evidence fields (owner, time, changelog, source).

Incident phases and expected cadence

- Detection (T0): short summary posted internally within 2 minutes of pager. Fields: incident_id, primary symptom, detected_by, initial_severity.
- Acknowledgement (T0+<5m): on-call acknowledges; bridge created and initial internal note placed.
- Mitigation (T0+5–30m): actions in-progress (scopes, mitigations, traffic fencing). Public-facing message: "We are investigating" with impacted features and preliminary mitigation steps.
- Customer Notice (T0+15–90m): first public update if customers are impacted; subsequent updates every 30–60 minutes while active.
- Resolution (when SLO restored): summary update with resolution steps, time to restore, and next steps for customers.
- Retro (postmortem week): consolidated timeline + action items shared with customers if the incident affected SLAs or sensitive data.

Templates (placeholders are double-curly variables)

1) Internal brief (Slack Bridge)

- Subject: [INCIDENT] {{incident_id}} — {{short_title}}
- Body:
  • Severity: {{severity}}
  • Detected: {{detected_at}} by {{detected_by}}
  • Impact: {{impact_summary}}
  • Current action: {{current_action}}
  • Owners: {{primary_owner}} / {{oncall_backup}}
  • Next update: {{next_update_in}}

2) Public status page short update

- Purpose: single-sentence high-level update for the status page header.
- Template: "{{service_list}}: We are investigating an issue affecting {{user_impact}}. Engineers are actively working on a fix. Next update at {{next_update_at}}."

3) Public status page detailed update

- Purpose: for the status page body with a timeline.
- Template: "Summary: {{short_title}}\nImpact: {{detailed_impact}}\nWhat we're doing: {{mitigation_steps}}\nWhen it started: {{detected_at}}\nWho’s working on it: {{primary_owner}}\nNext update: {{next_update_at}}\n"

4) Customer email/SMS template (for customers subscribed to incident alerts)

- Subject: Redwood Inference incident {{incident_id}} — {{short_title}}
- Body: "Hello,\n\nWe're contacting you because some of your requests to Redwood Inference ({{service_list}}) may be delayed or failing starting at {{detected_at}}. We are actively investigating.\n\nImpact to you: {{customer_specific_impact}}\nTemporary mitigation: {{customer_guidance}}\nExpected next update: {{next_update_at}}\n\nWe apologize for the disruption. If you require immediate assistance, reply to this message or contact your Customer Success rep: {{cs_contact}}.\n\n—The Redwood Inference Ops Team"

Automation specification (STG v1.2)

Inputs (incident payload schema) — must be provided to the generator as JSON via webhook or internal API:

| field | type | example | notes |
|-------|------|---------|-------|
| incident_id | string | INF-2026-1234 | unique id 
| start_time | iso8601 | 2026-02-14T09:12:03Z | detection time 
| detected_by | string | heartbeat/synthetic/pager | origin of signal 
| severity | enum | critical/high/medium/low | maps to page visibility 
| services | list[string] | [inference-proxy, batching-router] | service slugs 
| impact_summary | string | latency spike > 10s for 10% of requests | human brief 
| customer_impacted | boolean | true | feature gating for emails
| primary_owner | string | eng-sre-inference | oncall handle/team
| mitigation_actions | list[string] | [scale-out pool, disable-optimizer] | ops actions in-flight

Processing rules (server-side)

1) Visibility rules: severity=critical -> publish both status page header and detailed body; severity=high -> header + on-demand body; medium/low -> internal only unless customer_impacted=true.
2) Rate limiting: automated publishes limited to 1 per 10 minutes unless human overrides.
3) Freeze window: non-critical scheduled maintenance windows must not trigger public notices from STG.
4) Editable draft: pipeline creates a draft with filled variables in /status-drafts channel; a human must confirm publish for critical incidents.

Delivery targets and channels

- Status page API (primary): /api/v1/incidents/publish — accepts the resolved template body and evidence fields.
- Slack bridge (internal): /hooks/status-bridge — posts internal brief.
- Email/SMS provider: SendGrid/Twilio integration via provider adapter.
- PagerDuty: correlation is created via incident_id tag for cross-references.

Security and compliance constraints

- No sensitive customer PII in public updates. STG must strip any payload fields flagged as pii=true before template rendering.
- Audit log retention: publish events stored for 18 months with operator id and revision history.

Operational runbook (quick steps to configuring STG for a new service)

1) Add service slug to services catalog (git: infra/status/services.yaml). Required fields: slug, display_name, default_impact_text, cs_contact. Example YAML: 

```yaml
- slug: inference-proxy
  display_name: Inference Proxy
  default_impact_text: "Requests may be delayed or fail"
  cs_contact: cs-inference@redwood.com
```

2) Add templating override if service needs different phrasing: infra/status/templates/inference-proxy.md
3) Configure PagerDuty integration: attach the incident_id tag mapping in pd-rules.json and enable correlation=true
4) Set the publish policy: infra/status/policies/{{service_slug}}.json (fields: auto_publish_allowed, publish_rate_limit_mins, human_confirm_required)

Best-practice examples (realistic scenarios)

- Multi-region failover (partial impact): publish header: "We’re routing traffic to healthy regions; customers may experience brief increases in latency." Include a mitigation step that lists the region and expected time to failover.
- Tokenization service regression (quality-only): internal-only notification first; public notice only after impact degrades >5% of inference calls.
- Cache-eviction storm (thundering): publish immediately with guidance for customers to stagger requests and enable local caching.

Governance and ownership

- Primary owner for STG: eng-platform/status-team (pager: #status-ops).
- Publishing ownership: the on-call rotational engineer for the affected service is responsible for sign-off; Customer Success must review high-impact customer notices for enterprise tenants.

FAQ

Q: Who can edit templates?
A: Only members of eng-platform or triaged service owners. Edits require an approved PR and a short release note.

Q: What if automation fails to publish?
A: STG has a fallback: write draft to internal S3 bucket and ping the #status-ops channel. Human bridge operator must copy/paste to the status page manually.

Appendix: canned status snippets (copy-to-edit)

- Investigating header: "We’re investigating reports of degraded {{service_list}} performance. This may affect some customers using {{feature_list}}. Next update: {{next_update_at}}."
- Mitigating body: "Action in progress: {{mitigation_steps}}. We have isolated the problem to {{fault_domain}} and are applying mitigations to restore normal traffic. Impacted customers: {{customer_segment}}."
- Resolved body: "Root cause: {{root_cause_summary}}. What we did: {{remediation_summary}}. Time to restore: {{duration}}. Action items: {{postmortem_actions}}. If you continue to see issues, contact {{cs_contact}}."

Change log

- 2025-11-05: created by Ava Chen (initial STG design)
- 2026-02-18: added rate-limiting and draft flow (reviewed by Diego Martinez)
- 2026-03-01: updated mail/SMS templates and retention policy

Related artifacts and implementation links

- infra repo: git@github.com:redwood/infra (status subsystem folder)
- STG service: stg.redwood-inference.internal (api docs at /docs/swagger)
- Status page provider: status.redwood.com (api key stored in vault path: secret/status-page)
source.short_description: Template library and automation spec for incident status timelines, with templates for internal bridge, status page, and customer notifications, plus rules for automation and governance.

title: Status timeline automation spec and template library
preview:
Overview

This page is the canonical library for: (A) structured incident status timelines used by Redwood Inference responder teams and (B) the automation specification for generating and publishing those timelines to the status page, internal Slack bridge, and customer notification channels. The goal is fast, consistent, auditable customer communications that map to incident phases and operator actions.

Who should use this

- On-call SREs and service owners who publish incident updates
- Incident bridge operators and communications owners
- Automation engineers who implement status page webhooks and templates
- Customer Success for pre-approved messaging when high-impact incidents occur

Principles

1) Single source of truth: the status timeline generator (STG) must accept a single incident payload (incident_id) and emit consistent updates to all channels.
2) Phase-first messaging: timeline entries follow discrete phases (Detect, Ack, Mitigate, Customer Notice, Resolution, Retro).
3) Minimal friction: template variables are short and human-editable; automated drafts must be editable before publish.
4) Auditability: every published update must include evidence fields (owner, time, changelog, source).

Incident phases and expected cadence

- Detection (T0): short summary posted internally within 2 minutes of pager. Fields: incident_id, primary symptom, detected_by, initial_severity.
- Acknowledgement (T0+<5m): on-call acknowledges; bridge created and initial internal note placed.
- Mitigation (T0+5–30m): actions in-progress (scopes, mitigations, traffic fencing). Public-facing message: "We are investigating" with impacted features and preliminary mitigation steps.
- Customer Notice (T0+15–90m): first public update if customers are impacted; subsequent updates every 30–60 minutes while active.
- Resolution (when SLO restored): summary update with resolution steps, time to restore, and next steps for customers.
- Retro (postmortem week): consolidated timeline + action items shared with customers if the incident affected SLAs or sensitive data.

Templates (placeholders are double-curly variables)

1) Internal brief (Slack Bridge)

- Subject: [INCIDENT] {{incident_id}} — {{short_title}}
- Body:
  • Severity: {{severity}}
  • Detected: {{detected_at}} by {{detected_by}}
  • Impact: {{impact_summary}}
  • Current action: {{current_action}}
  • Owners: {{primary_owner}} / {{oncall_backup}}
  • Next update: {{next_update_in}}

2) Public status page short update

- Purpose: single-sentence high-level update for the status page header.
- Template: "{{service_list}}: We are investigating an issue affecting {{user_impact}}. Engineers are actively working on a fix. Next update at {{next_update_at}}."

3) Public status page detailed update

- Purpose: for the status page body with a timeline.
- Template: "Summary: {{short_title}}
Impact: {{detailed_impact}}
What we're doing: {{mitigation_steps}}
When it started: {{detected_at}}
Who’s working on it: {{primary_owner}}
Next update: {{next_update_at}}
"

4) Customer email/SMS template (for customers subscribed to incident alerts)

- Subject: Redwood Inference incident {{incident_id}} — {{short_title}}
- Body: "Hello,

We're contacting you because some of your requests to Redwood Inference ({{service_list}}) may be delayed or failing starting at {{detected_at}}. We are actively investigating.

Impact to you: {{customer_specific_impact}}
Temporary mitigation: {{customer_guidance}}
Expected next update: {{next_update_at}}

We apologize for the disruption. If you require immediate assistance, reply to this message or contact your Customer Success rep: {{cs_contact}}.

—The Redwood Inference Ops Team"

Automation specification (STG v1.2)

Inputs (incident payload schema) — must be provided to the generator as JSON via webhook or internal API:

| field | type | example | notes |
|-------|------|---------|-------|
| incident_id | string | INF-2026-1234 | unique id 
| start_time | iso8601 | 2026-02-14T09:12:03Z | detection time 
| detected_by | string | heartbeat/synthetic/pager | origin of signal 
| severity | enum | critical/high/medium/low | maps to page visibility 
| services | list[string] | [inference-proxy, batching-router] | service slugs 
| impact_summary | string | latency spike > 10s for 10% of requests | human brief 
| customer_impacted | boolean | true | feature gating for emails
| primary_owner | string | eng-sre-inference | oncall handle/team
| mitigation_actions | list[string] | [scale-out pool, disable-optimizer] | ops actions in-flight

Processing rules (server-side)

1) Visibility rules: severity=critical -> publish both status page header and detailed body; severity=high -> header + on-demand body; medium/low -> internal only unless customer_impacted=true.
2) Rate limiting: automated publishes limited to 1 per 10 minutes unless human overrides.
3) Freeze window: non-critical scheduled maintenance windows must not trigger public notices from STG.
4) Editable draft: pipeline creates a draft with filled variables in /status-drafts channel; a human must confirm publish for critical incidents.

Delivery targets and channels

- Status page API (primary): /api/v1/incidents/publish — accepts the resolved template body and evidence fields.
- Slack bridge (internal): /hooks/status-bridge — posts internal brief.
- Email/SMS provider: SendGrid/Twilio integration via provider adapter.
- PagerDuty: correlation is created via incident_id tag for cross-references.

Security and compliance constraints

- No sensitive customer PII in public updates. STG must strip any payload fields flagged as pii=true before template rendering.
- Audit log retention: publish events stored for 18 months with operator id and revision history.

Operational runbook (quick steps to configuring STG for a new service)

1) Add service slug to services catalog (git: infra/status/services.yaml). Required fields: slug, display_name, default_impact_text, cs_contact. Example YAML: 

```yaml
- slug: inference-proxy
  display_name: Inference Proxy
  default_impact_text: "Requests may be delayed or fail"
  cs_contact: cs-inference@redwood.com
```

2) Add templating override if service needs different phrasing: infra/status/templates/inference-proxy.md
3) Configure PagerDuty integration: attach the incident_id tag mapping in pd-rules.json and enable correlation=true
4) Set the publish policy: infra/status/policies/{{service_slug}}.json (fields: auto_publish_allowed, publish_rate_limit_mins, human_confirm_required)

Best-practice examples (realistic scenarios)

- Multi-region failover (partial impact): publish header: "We’re routing traffic to healthy regions; customers may experience brief increases in latency." Include a mitigation step that lists the region and expected time to failover.
- Tokenization service regression (quality-only): internal-only notification first; public notice only after impact degrades >5% of inference calls.
- Cache-eviction storm (thundering): publish immediately with guidance for customers to stagger requests and enable local caching.

Governance and ownership

- Primary owner for STG: eng-platform/status-team (pager: #status-ops).
- Publishing ownership: the on-call rotational engineer for the affected service is responsible for sign-off; Customer Success must review high-impact customer notices for enterprise tenants.

FAQ

Q: Who can edit templates?
A: Only members of eng-platform or triaged service owners. Edits require an approved PR and a short release note.

Q: What if automation fails to publish?
A: STG has a fallback: write draft to internal S3 bucket and ping the #status-ops channel. Human bridge operator must copy/paste to the status page manually.

Appendix: canned status snippets (copy-to-edit)

- Investigating header: "We’re investigating reports of degraded {{service_list}} performance. This may affect some customers using {{feature_list}}. Next update: {{next_update_at}}."
- Mitigating body: "Action in progress: {{mitigation_steps}}. We have isolated the problem to {{fault_domain}} and are applying mitigations to restore normal traffic. Impacted customers: {{customer_segment}}."
- Resolved body: "Root cause: {{root_cause_summary}}. What we did: {{remediation_summary}}. Time to restore: {{duration}}. Action items: {{postmortem_actions}}. If you continue to see issues, contact {{cs_contact}}."

Change log

- 2025-11-05: created by Ava Chen (initial STG design)
- 2026-02-18: added rate-limiting and draft flow (reviewed by Diego Martinez)
- 2026-03-01: updated mail/SMS templates and retention policy

Related artifacts and implementation links

- infra repo: git@github.com:redwood/infra (status subsystem folder)
- STG service: stg.redwood-inference.internal (api docs at /docs/swagger)
- Status page provider: status.redwood.com (api key stored in vault path: secret/status-page)
