- **SEV-3**: every **120 minutes** or when new information is available.

### Resolution notice
Post a final “Resolved” update when:
- Metrics return to normal ranges AND
- Mitigation is stable (no active rollback risk) AND
- IC confirms monitoring period (typically 15–30 minutes for SEV-1/2).

---
## Tier-specific wording rules
### Always include (required fields)
- **Tier(s) impacted**: Hosted, Dedicated, or both.
- **Regions**: e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-1.
- **Endpoints**: chat/completions, embeddings, streaming.
- **Symptoms**: elevated 5xx, increased p95 latency, streaming disconnects/timeouts.
- **Start time** (UTC) and **current status** (Investigating / Identified / Monitoring / Resolved).
- **Mitigation** in plain language.
- **Next update time**.

### Avoid (public status page)
- Customer names, account IDs, dedicated cluster names.
- Exact capacity numbers (GPU counts), unless explicitly approved by VP Eng Platform or VP Infra & Reliability.
- “SLO breach” declarations in real time. Use “degraded performance” or “elevated error rates.”

### Dedicated-only messages may include
- Account-scoped details (customer-approved channels only): dedicated pool name, specific model deployments, known request IDs, customer-specific routing changes.
- Clarification on whether the incident is expected to affect their contractual SLA calculations (only if coordinated with PM Dedicated + Legal guidance; otherwise say: “We’ll follow up with a reliability report after resolution.”)

---
## Streaming-specific guidance
Streaming incidents are frequently reported as “disconnects.” Use consistent taxonomy:
- **Server-side disconnects / stream errors**: count as service impact; message as “stream interruptions” or “streaming errors.”
- **Client aborts**: do not message as an outage unless there is evidence of server-induced aborts.

When streaming is impacted but non-streaming requests are healthy, be explicit:
- “Streaming responses may terminate early; non-streaming responses are unaffected.”

---
## Templates
### 1) Public status page: Hosted-only degradation (SEV-2)
**Title:** Degraded performance for Hosted API in <REGION>

**Body:**
Investigating: We are investigating elevated latency and error rates affecting the **Hosted API** in <REGION>. Impacted endpoints include <ENDPOINTS>. Dedicated capacity is not currently showing degradation.

Mitigation: <WHAT WE ARE DOING IN PLAIN LANGUAGE>.

Next update: <TIME UTC>.

### 2) Public status page: Both Hosted + some Dedicated impact (SEV-1)
**Title:** Service disruption affecting Hosted API and some Dedicated deployments in <REGION/AREAS>

**Body:**
Identified: We are responding to a service disruption causing elevated error rates and/or timeouts. The **Hosted API** is impacted in <REGIONS>. We are also seeing impact on **some Dedicated deployments** in <REGIONS>.

Mitigation: <ROLLBACK / CAPACITY ACTION / ROUTING CHANGE>.

We will provide another update by <TIME UTC>.

### 3) Dedicated-only email (account-scoped)
**Subject:** [Redwood Dedicated] Service degradation for your deployment in <REGION> (Investigating)

**Message:**
We are investigating an issue impacting your **Dedicated** deployment in <REGION>. Symptoms observed: <SYMBOLS: elevated 5xx / increased p95 latency / streaming interruptions> beginning at <TIME UTC>.

What we’re doing: <MITIGATION STEPS>.

What you may see: <EXPECTED CUSTOMER IMPACT AND WORKAROUNDS IF ANY>.

Next update by: <TIME UTC>.

If you have example request IDs/timestamps, please reply with:
- time range (UTC)
- region
- endpoint and model
- request IDs (if available)

### 4) Public status page: Investigating (when scope is unknown)
**Title:** Investigating increased errors and latency

**Body:**
Investigating: We are investigating reports of increased errors and latency. We are currently assessing which regions and tiers are impacted.

Next update: <TIME UTC>.

### 5) Resolution template (public)
**Title:** Resolved: <SUMMARY>

**Body:**
Resolved: This issue has been mitigated and service has returned to normal levels. The incident started at <START UTC> and was resolved at <END UTC>.

If you continue to see issues, contact Support with timestamps, region, and request IDs.

---
## What to say about fallbacks and graceful degradation
Routing/fallback can preserve availability while altering behavior. Communicate clearly when relevant:
- If we temporarily route to a compatible model variant: “We applied a mitigation that may change model selection for some requests while we restore capacity.”
- If overload shedding is enabled for Hosted: “Some requests may be rate-limited or rejected to protect overall service reliability.”

Do not promise that output quality is unchanged unless validated by the owning team.

---
## Escalation and coordination with Support
- If Support has an active surge of tickets, Comms should include a short “customer actions” line (what data to provide, where to look) and link internal incident ID in the Support macro.
- CS/SE should route Dedicated escalations using the intake template and include:
  - affected region(s)
  - endpoint(s)
  - model(s)
  - whether streaming is used
  - request IDs/timestamps

---
## Approval and exception process
### Default approvals
- **Public status page**: IC + Incident Comms.
- **Dedicated-only comms**: IC + CS/SE owner for the account.

### Exceptions (require additional approval)
- Mentioning specific infrastructure vendors, security events, or data exposure: requires Security & Compliance review.
- Sharing capacity numbers, detailed architecture diagrams, or internal component failures: requires VP Eng Platform or VP Infra & Reliability.

---
## Quality checklist (before posting)
- Tier(s) impacted stated explicitly.
- Region(s) stated.
- Endpoint(s) stated.
- Symptom stated with plain language.
- Mitigation and next update time included.
- No customer-identifying or sensitive operational details included.
- Message matches current incident status (Investigating/Identified/Monitoring/Resolved).

---
## FAQ
**Q: Should we publish a status page update for a single Dedicated customer?**
A: No, not by default. Use Dedicated-only comms unless impact is widespread or cannot be scoped quickly.

**Q: Can we say “SLO breach” during an incident?**
A: Avoid real-time “breach” language. Use “degraded”/“elevated errors.” SLO reporting is handled post-incident and over defined windows.

**Q: If Hosted is fine but a routing change might affect Hosted, do we post publicly?**
A: If the change is expected to be user-visible (e.g., model fallback, elevated rate-limits), publish a brief advisory on the status page.

---
## Change log
- 2026-02-20: Added streaming-specific guidance and clarified Dedicated-only publication criteria.
- 2026-02-12: Initial publication aligned to tiered SLO/error budget rollout.

source.title: Escalation cadence and playbook for blitz recovery
source.author: Maya Chen
source.content:
Overview

Purpose: This document defines the escalation cadence, a standardized playbook for high-severity (blitz) incidents affecting customer-facing inference throughput or correctness, and the templates we use for runbooks, status page updates, and customer communications. It is intended to reduce time-to-restoration, clarify responsibilities, and ensure consistent post-incident learning.

Scope: Applies to the Redwood inference control plane, hosted API endpoints, Dedicated gateway agents, and cache/kv subsystems. Do NOT use this playbook for purely billing/metadata issues or non-production sandbox incidents.

Quick links
- Incident tracking channel: #prod-incidents (Slack)
- PagerDuty: Redwood Prod Escalation
- Status page template: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/status-page-templates

Definitions and severity matrix

We use three operational severities for production incidents in this playbook:

1) Sev-1 (Blitz): system-wide customer impact (>=10% customers seeing timeouts / >30% token throttling / systemic model-serving failures). Requires immediate escalation.
2) Sev-2 (Partial): degraded latency or errors for subset of customers or models (intermittent 5–30%): elevated oncall + engineering followup.
3) Sev-3 (Minor): single-customer or non-blocking issues, scheduled remediation.

Runbook ownership and roles

- Primary responder (On-call engineer): initial triage, gather signals, begin mitigation steps (PagerDuty responder).
- Incident commander (IC): coordinates multi-team work, sets priority, approves customer comms. Role rotates to senior SRE or platform engineer depending on time-of-day.
- Escalation engineer(s): domain experts (runtime, routing, infra) called as needed.
- Customer success liaison: drafts external comms and collects impacted customer list.
- Postmortem author: typically the primary responder or IC.

Escalation matrix (summary)

| Trigger | Primary | Escalate to (after 15 min) | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|
| API errors > 5% across region | On-call | Runtime lead + SRE manager | Open firebreak if error spike persists 10+ min |
| Latency p50 > 500ms and p95 > 2s | On-call | Backend service owner | Check batching and queue metrics |
| Consistent OOMs on GPU nodes | On-call | Infra/GPU fleet oncall + Eng manager | Consider draining node pool, fallback models |
| Data exfil/compromise suspicion | On-call | Security oncall + Legal | Follow security incident process; move to restricted docs |

Incident lifecycle (playbook steps for a Blitz / Sev-1)

1) Detection and acknowledgment (0–5 min)
   - On-call engineer acknowledges PagerDuty and posts initial message in #prod-incidents: short problem statement, ``impact`` and ``first mitigation step``.
   - Run the quick health checks:
     - curl -sS https://inference-gw.prod.redwood/api/v1/health | jq .status
     - kubectl -n inference get pods -l app=inference-gateway -o wide
     - check streaming queue depth: curl -s http://metrics.internal:9090/api/v1/query?query=queue_length{service=\"gateway\"}

2) Containment & immediate mitigation (5–15 min)
   - If model-serving hot path is overloaded: switch routing policy to fallback model variant with lower resource needs (example): POST /admin/routing with JSON body (see admin templates).
   - If GPU OOMs: reduce inference batch size with the endpoint config and restart pods in controlled rolling restart: ``kubectl rollout restart deploy/inference-gateway --namespace=inference --selector=pool=dedicated``
   - If unknown error source: enable request tracing and increase sampling to 100% for 10 minutes: set tracing.sampling=1.0 via configmap.

3) Stabilize and monitor (15–45 min)
   - Confirm error rates return to baseline for at least two consecutive 5-minute windows.
   - Open temporary incident dashboard with these panels: 1) request rate, 2) 4xx/5xx rate, 3) p50/p95 latency, 4) GPU memory pressure, 5) KV cache hit ratio.
   - If stabilization not achieved at 30 min, IC authorizes widening the escalation and customer comms.

4) Customer notification (30–60 min)
   - Use the status page template below; publish a brief incident banner and mark affected regions/services. Notify top impacted customers via CSMs (see email template).
   - Keep messages focused on: impact, mitigation in progress, expected next update time (30–60 min cadence).

5) Recovery and rollback (60–180 min)
   - Run controlled rollback if a recent deploy is implicated: ``kubectl rollout undo deploy/inference-gateway --to-revision=<n>`` and monitor.
   - If capacity shortage: activate reserved capacity pool or scale up Dedicated pools as per capacity playbook (runbook name: dedicated-scaling-and-burst-activation).

6) Post-incident (within 24–72 hrs)
   - IC assigns a postmortem author; postmortem must include timeline, root cause analysis, impact metrics, and action items with owners and target dates.
   - Hold a blameless review within 72 hours; capture lessons learned and update runbooks.

Runbook templates and checklists

Primary triage checklist (first 15 minutes)
- [ ] Acknowledge alert and post in #prod-incidents
- [ ] Attach initial metrics (error rate, latency, request rate) and top-3 affected endpoints
- [ ] Confirm if rollback or routing fallback is safe
- [ ] Identify a single IC and declare incident level

Status page and customer comms templates

Status page brief (example)
- Title: Ongoing service degradation for text-generation API (region: us-west-2)
- Impact: Requests may experience errors or increased latency. Some customers may see timeouts.
- Mitigation: We have diverted traffic to a fallback model and are scaling capacity.
- Next update: 2026-02-18T14:30Z

Customer email template (concise)
Subject: Incident affecting Redwood inference in us-west-2 (Feb 18)
Hello <CustomerName>,
We are investigating an incident affecting inference requests in us-west-2. Impact: increased errors and latency for some requests. Mitigation in progress: we have switched to a fallback model and are scaling capacity. We will provide a further update by <time>. We apologize for the disruption and are prioritizing restore.
Regards,
Redwood Operations

Operational thresholds and alerting examples
- 5xx rate > 1% for 5m => P1 alert to on-call (PagerDuty)
- p95 latency > 2s for 5m => P1 alert
- Queue depth > 1000 (sustained 3m) => P1 alert
- KV cache hit ratio < 60% for 10m and request rate unchanged => P2

Playbook snippets (commands and quick actions)
- Check pod logs for inference-gateway (last 500 lines): ``kubectl -n inference logs -l app=inference-gateway --tail=500``
- Restart service gracefully: ``kubectl -n inference scale deployment inference-gateway --replicas=0 && sleep 10 && kubectl -n inference scale deployment inference-gateway --replicas=3``
- Toggle routing to fallback model (admin API):
  curl -X POST -H \"Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN\" \
    https://admin.prod.redwood/api/v1/routing/override \
    -d '{"service":"text-generation","policy":"fallback","duration_minutes":60}'

On-call rotation, handoffs, and leave coverage

- Rotation length: 7 days for primary on-call, 14 days for secondary SRE.
- Handoff window: 30 minutes overlap recommended for critical teams; incoming engineer verifies alerts and outstanding incidents.
- Leave policy: request coverage in the oncall calendar and notify SRE manager at least 72 hours in advance for planned absences. For urgent leave, declare interim coverage in #prod-incidents.

Handoff checklist (incoming)
- [ ] Confirm no active Sev-1 incidents
- [ ] Review outstanding Sev-2/3 tickets assigned to you
- [ ] Verify your PagerDuty contact and Slack status
- [ ] Run the preflight health check script: /scripts/oncall-preflight.sh

Game day plan (90-minute scenario for blitz readiness)

Objective: exercise cross-team escalation, role assignments, and status page publishing under a simulated GPU capacity exhaustion event.
Participants: 2 SREs (primary/secondary), 1 runtime engineer, 1 infra engineer, 1 CSM, 1 product lead.
Sequence:
1. Inject simulated traffic to inference-gateway to raise queue depth to 2x normal.
2. Trigger a synthetic OOM on a non-production GPU node to practice draining process.
3. Require IC to publish status page update and send customer email within 30 minutes.
4. Validate rollback flow and rolling restart.

Metrics to measure during game day: mean time to acknowledge (target < 2 min), mean time to mitigate (target < 20 min), quality of comms (CSM score).

Postmortem template (required fields)
- Title
- Summary (1–2 lines)
- Severity and impact (requests affected, regions, customer count)
- Timeline (minute-resolution for first 2 hours)
- Root cause analysis (why change occurred)
- Corrective actions (owner + due date)
- Preventative actions and follow-ups

Governance, runbook maintenance and continuous improvement

- After every confirmed Sev-1, the associated runbooks and escalation matrix must be reviewed and updated within 10 business days.
- Quarterly game days are mandatory for the SRE and runtime teams; participation is tracked and reported to Eng leadership.
- Runbook owners: each service must have a named owner listed in service registry; owners must confirm runbook accuracy twice yearly.

Appendix: useful query snippets and dashboards
- Prometheus query: ``sum(rate(http_requests_total{job=\"gateway\",status=~\"5..\"}[5m])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total{job=\"gateway\"}[5m]))``
- Dashboard panels to create: error ratio by model, latency by customer tier, model-memory vs GPU utilization.

Contact & escalation directory (primary contacts)
- On-call rotation: see calendar: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/oncall-rotations-calendar
- SRE manager: Diego Morales
- Runtime lead: Priya Natarajan
- Infra/GPU lead: Ethan Brooks

Revision history
- 2025-09-12: created by Maya Chen (initial draft)
- 2025-11-03: added game-day plan and customer email template (reviewers: Priya N.)
- 2026-02-18: formalized escalation matrix and updated thresholds (published)

   - If unknown error source: enable request tracing and increase sampling to 100% for 10 minutes: set tracing.sampling=1.0 via configmap.

3) Stabilize and monitor (15–45 min)
   - Confirm error rates return to baseline for at least two consecutive 5-minute windows.
   - Open temporary incident dashboard with these panels: 1) request rate, 2) 4xx/5xx rate, 3) p50/p95 latency, 4) GPU memory pressure, 5) KV cache hit ratio.
   - If stabilization not achieved at 30 min, IC authorizes widening the escalation and customer comms.

4) Customer notification (30–60 min)
   - Use the status page template below; publish a brief incident banner and mark affected regions/services. Notify top impacted customers via CSMs (see email template).
   - Keep messages focused on: impact, mitigation in progress, expected next update time (30–60 min cadence).

5) Recovery and rollback (60–180 min)
   - Run controlled rollback if a recent deploy is implicated: ``kubectl rollout undo deploy/inference-gateway --to-revision=<n>`` and monitor.
   - If capacity shortage: activate reserved capacity pool or scale up Dedicated pools as per capacity playbook (runbook name: dedicated-scaling-and-burst-activation).

6) Post-incident (within 24–72 hrs)
   - IC assigns a postmortem author; postmortem must include timeline, root cause analysis, impact metrics, and action items with owners and target dates.
   - Hold a blameless review within 72 hours; capture lessons learned and update runbooks.

Runbook templates and checklists

Primary triage checklist (first 15 minutes)
- [ ] Acknowledge alert and post in #prod-incidents
- [ ] Attach initial metrics (error rate, latency, request rate) and top-3 affected endpoints
- [ ] Confirm if rollback or routing fallback is safe
- [ ] Identify a single IC and declare incident level

Status page and customer comms templates

Status page brief (example)
- Title: Ongoing service degradation for text-generation API (region: us-west-2)
- Impact: Requests may experience errors or increased latency. Some customers may see timeouts.
- Mitigation: We have diverted traffic to a fallback model and are scaling capacity.
- Next update: 2026-02-18T14:30Z

Customer email template (concise)
Subject: Incident affecting Redwood inference in us-west-2 (Feb 18)
Hello <CustomerName>,
We are investigating an incident affecting inference requests in us-west-2. Impact: increased errors and latency for some requests. Mitigation in progress: we have switched to a fallback model and are scaling capacity. We will provide a further update by <time>. We apologize for the disruption and are prioritizing restore.
Regards,
Redwood Operations

Operational thresholds and alerting examples
- 5xx rate > 1% for 5m => P1 alert to on-call (PagerDuty)
- p95 latency > 2s for 5m => P1 alert
- Queue depth > 1000 (sustained 3m) => P1 alert
- KV cache hit ratio < 60% for 10m and request rate unchanged => P2

Playbook snippets (commands and quick actions)
- Check pod logs for inference-gateway (last 500 lines): ``kubectl -n inference logs -l app=inference-gateway --tail=500``
- Restart service gracefully: ``kubectl -n inference scale deployment inference-gateway --replicas=0 && sleep 10 && kubectl -n inference scale deployment inference-gateway --replicas=3``
- Toggle routing to fallback model (admin API):
  curl -X POST -H \"Authorization: Bearer $ADMIN_TOKEN\" \
    https://admin.prod.redwood/api/v1/routing/override \
    -d '{"service":"text-generation","policy":"fallback","duration_minutes":60}'

On-call rotation, handoffs, and leave coverage

- Rotation length: 7 days for primary on-call, 14 days for secondary SRE.
- Handoff window: 30 minutes overlap recommended for critical teams; incoming engineer verifies alerts and outstanding incidents.
- Leave policy: request coverage in the oncall calendar and notify SRE manager at least 72 hours in advance for planned absences. For urgent leave, declare interim coverage in #prod-incidents.

Handoff checklist (incoming)
- [ ] Confirm no active Sev-1 incidents
- [ ] Review outstanding Sev-2/3 tickets assigned to you
- [ ] Verify your PagerDuty contact and Slack status
- [ ] Run the preflight health check script: /scripts/oncall-preflight.sh

Game day plan (90-minute scenario for blitz readiness)

Objective: exercise cross-team escalation, role assignments, and status page publishing under a simulated GPU capacity exhaustion event.
Participants: 2 SREs (primary/secondary), 1 runtime engineer, 1 infra engineer, 1 CSM, 1 product lead.
Sequence:
1. Inject simulated traffic to inference-gateway to raise queue depth to 2x normal.
2. Trigger a synthetic OOM on a non-production GPU node to practice draining process.
3. Require IC to publish status page update and send customer email within 30 minutes.
4. Validate rollback flow and rolling restart.

Metrics to measure during game day: mean time to acknowledge (target < 2 min), mean time to mitigate (target < 20 min), quality of comms (CSM score).

Postmortem template (required fields)
- Title
- Summary (1–2 lines)
- Severity and impact (requests affected, regions, customer count)
- Timeline (minute-resolution for first 2 hours)
- Root cause analysis (why change occurred)
- Corrective actions (owner + due date)
- Preventative actions and follow-ups

Governance, runbook maintenance and continuous improvement

- After every confirmed Sev-1, the associated runbooks and escalation matrix must be reviewed and updated within 10 business days.
- Quarterly game days are mandatory for the SRE and runtime teams; participation is tracked and reported to Eng leadership.
- Runbook owners: each service must have a named owner listed in service registry; owners must confirm runbook accuracy twice yearly.

Appendix: useful query snippets and dashboards
- Prometheus query: ``sum(rate(http_requests_total{job=\"gateway\",status=~\"5..\"}[5m])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total{job=\"gateway\"}[5m]))``
- Dashboard panels to create: error ratio by model, latency by customer tier, model-memory vs GPU utilization.

Contact & escalation directory (primary contacts)
- On-call rotation: see calendar: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/oncall-rotations-calendar
- SRE manager: Diego Morales
- Runtime lead: Priya Natarajan
- Infra/GPU lead: Ethan Brooks

Revision history
- 2025-09-12: created by Maya Chen (initial draft)
- 2025-11-03: added game-day plan and customer email template (reviewers: Priya N.)
- 2026-02-18: formalized escalation matrix and updated thresholds (published)

source.title: Customer Incident Comms Alignment (Support ↔ Slack Bridge ↔ Status Page)
source.author: Allison Grant
source.body:
## Purpose
This page defines how Redwood Inference communicates during customer-impacting incidents across:
- **Support customer comms** (email + ticket updates)
- **Slack bridges** (#support bridge channels and #incidents)
- **Status page updates** (public and/or customer-facing status events)

It provides:
1) A shared definition of what qualifies for a **status page incident**
2) A single **approval flow** so customers receive consistent, accurate updates
3) A standard **message structure** so Support, SRE, and Platform are aligned on impact, mitigations, and timelines

This is written to reduce rework, avoid conflicting statements, and keep our comms aligned with on-call practices and customer contract expectations.

---

## Scope and non-goals
### In scope
- Customer-impacting incidents and degradations across Hosted API, Dedicated, and Private (when Redwood is responsible for a shared control plane component).
- Communication alignment and escalation mechanics (who updates what, where, and when).
- When to use:
  - status page incident
  - docs-site banner / help-center banner
  - direct customer comms only

### Out of scope
- Product roadmap or long-term remediation plans (those belong in postmortems/RCAs and engineering planning).
- Customer-specific details that should remain in restricted channels/tickets.

---

## Guiding principles
1) **One source of truth for “current state.”** During an incident, the Incident Commander (IC) owns the canonical status. Support mirrors that status to customers.
2) **Be explicit about confidence.** If the cause is unknown, say so; do not speculate.
3) **Separate impact from cause.** Customers need to understand symptoms, scope, and mitigations even when root cause is still under investigation.
4) **Do not promise ETAs unless approved by IC.** If an ETA is needed, it must be framed as an estimate and tied to a specific action (e.g., rollout completion).
5) **Minimize customer-sensitive detail.** Use request IDs, regions, and model names carefully. Do not include customer identifiers on the status page.
6) **Cadence beats completeness.** Regular updates reduce inbound pressure and improve trust. Updates can be short if there is no change.

---

## Definitions
- **Slack bridge:** A dedicated Slack channel created by Support or on-call to coordinate triage and mitigation for a customer-impacting issue. (See Slack bridge conventions page.)
- **#incidents channel:** The company-wide incident channel used when the issue is broadly customer-impacting or meets incident criteria.
- **Status page incident:** A customer-facing incident entry (public status page or authenticated customer status surface) used for broad impact or contractual expectations.
- **Comms lead:** The person responsible for drafting and posting updates. Often Support lead for customer emails and IC/SRE for status page.

---

## Decision framework: When do we post a status page incident?
Use the criteria below. If any “YES” condition is met, open a status incident unless explicitly waived by IC + CS leadership.

### A) Immediate “YES” criteria
Create a status page incident when any of the following are true:
- **Multi-customer impact** (two or more customers) with confirmed platform-level symptoms (e.g., region-level latency/timeouts, auth errors, widespread 429/5xx).
- **Critical tier impact** (Dedicated/Private/Enterprise) where we expect SLA-related concerns, even if the blast radius is limited.
- **Customer-facing outage/degradation** in a region or product area that is likely to be noticed externally (availability/latency materially degraded).
- **Security or data access incident** that requires coordinated disclosures (follow Security process; status page language must be reviewed by Security/Legal).

### B) “Likely YES” criteria (use judgment)
Usually create a status page incident if:
- Impact persists beyond **15 minutes** with no quick mitigation.
- We are actively performing **traffic shifts/fallbacks/rollbacks** that may change behavior for customers.
- Support is receiving escalating inbound volume (multiple duplicate tickets) indicating broader impact.

### C) “NO” criteria (status page usually not needed)
Do not create a status page incident for:
- A single-customer configuration issue or client-side bug (unless it reveals a platform defect with broader risk).
- Known issue with a documented workaround and no measurable platform degradation.
- Private/on-prem incidents strictly inside the customer’s environment (unless Redwood-managed components are failing and multiple customers are affected).

### D) If not status page: when to use a docs/help-center banner
Use a docs/help-center banner when:
- There is a **known issue** with a validated workaround, and
- Customers are likely to hit it repeatedly, and
- It is not a real-time incident (or it’s resolved but needs proactive guidance).

Owner: Docs/DevEx with Support input. Approval: Support lead + relevant Eng owner.

---

## Severity alignment (comms expectations)
We align comms behavior to Support priority and Engineering incident severity.

- **Support P0 / Engineering Sev0–Sev1:**
  - Status page incident expected.
  - Update cadence: follow enterprise expectations; do not exceed what the incident team can sustain (see SLA policy page).
  - Comms must be synchronized across Support + IC.

- **Support P1 / Engineering Sev1–Sev2:**
  - Status page incident recommended if impact is multi-customer or tier-critical.
  - Updates should include mitigation progress and next check-in.

- **Support P2+ / Engineering Sev2–Sev3:**
  - Status page generally not required unless there is broad impact.
  - Prefer KB article and support macros for repeatability.

Note: Severity classification is owned by the IC/on-call process; Support priority is owned by Support triage. They must be mapped consistently in Jira (see severity matrix and Jira field guidelines).

---

## Roles and responsibilities
### Incident Commander (IC) (on-call)
- Owns incident severity, mitigation decisions, and canonical platform status.
- Approves status page updates and any customer-facing ETA statements.

### Support Lead / Escalation Manager (Support on duty)
- Owns customer ticket prioritization and direct customer comms.
- Ensures Support messaging matches the status page and IC’s current assessment.
- Opens/maintains the Support Slack bridge, ensures the bridge includes required context.

### Comms Lead (may be IC or delegated)
- Drafts the status page updates and ensures timestamps and scope are correct.
- Keeps language consistent with customer emails.

### SRE/Platform subject matter owners
- Provide verified technical summaries and mitigation steps.
- Review any statements about cause, blast radius, and expected time-to-mitigation.

### Security/Legal (as needed)
- Review language for security, privacy, compliance, or contractual constraints.

---

## Standard approval flow
### 1) Start: create a bridge and establish canonical context
When customer impact is suspected:
1. Support starts a Slack bridge per conventions.
2. First bridge message must include:
   - Symptoms (what customer observes)
   - Region(s)
   - Model(s)/endpoint(s)
   - Time window (UTC)
   - Example request IDs (customer-safe)
   - Current customer tier(s) impacted (Hosted/Dedicated/Private/Enterprise)
   - Links: Jira SUP ticket(s), relevant dashboards/runbooks
3. If criteria indicates broad impact, move coordination to **#incidents** or create an incident channel.

### 2) Decide: status page incident vs direct comms
- IC + Support Lead decide within **10 minutes** of confirmation.
- If uncertain, default to **opening a status incident** with cautious language (“Investigating”).

### 3) Draft and approve first public update
- Status page initial post must be approved by:
  - IC (required)
  - Security/Legal (only if security/compliance related)
- Support email can be sent after IC approves the core wording OR it can reference the status page entry once live.

### 4) Ongoing updates
- Status page updates are posted by Comms Lead with IC approval.
- Support mirrors updates to:
  - affected customer tickets
  - enterprise email threads as needed
- If there is divergence (Support needs customer-specific guidance), keep details in direct comms; do not add customer-specific details to status page.

### 5) Resolution and follow-up
- Status page: “Monitoring” → “Resolved” transitions must match actual platform behavior.
- Support resolution email/ticket update must include:
  - what changed (mitigation)
  - customer action required (if any)
  - how to confirm recovery
  - pointer to RCA timeline if/when available

---

## Message structure (use consistently)
Use this structure for both status page updates and customer emails (tone adjusted by channel).

### Required fields
- **What’s happening (symptom):** e.g., elevated timeouts / streaming disconnects
- **Who is affected (scope):** region(s), endpoint(s), model families (no customer names)
- **When it started (time):** include timezone (prefer UTC)
- **What we’re doing:** investigation, mitigation, rollback, traffic shift
- **What customers can do now:** workaround guidance if safe
- **Next update time:** a specific timestamp OR “within X minutes”

### Language guidelines
- Use “We are investigating” when root cause is not confirmed.
- Use “We believe” only when confidence is moderate and state the evidence.
- Avoid:
  - exact internal system names not meaningful to customers
  - speculation about third-party providers unless confirmed
  - detailed infrastructure topology

---

## Templates
### Status page: Initial update (Investigating)
**Title:** [Region/Product] Degraded performance for [endpoint]

**Body:**
We are investigating an issue causing [symptom] for [scope: region(s), endpoint(s), model(s) if relevant] starting at approximately [time UTC].

Current impact: [brief, measurable if possible: elevated error rates / increased latency].

We will provide another update by [time UTC] or sooner as we learn more.

### Status page: Mitigation in progress
We have identified [probable/confirmed] cause related to [high-level cause]. We are implementing mitigation by [action: rollback/traffic shift/capacity increase].

Impact remains [describe]. Next update by [time UTC].

### Status page: Resolved
The issue has been mitigated and service has returned to normal levels as of [time UTC]. We will continue to monitor.

---

## Keeping Support emails aligned with the status page
### Rules of thumb
- If a status page incident exists, **link it** in customer comms and avoid re-paraphrasing details differently.
- Support comms may add:
  - customer-specific confirmation steps
  - customer-specific workarounds or config changes
  - account-level investigations (quota, routing policy)
- Support comms must not add:
  - unapproved ETAs
  - unverified root cause
  - statements that contradict the status page scope (e.g., claiming “only you” while a status incident is open)

### Handling “Waiting on Customer” without SLA confusion
If customer action is required:
- Explicitly list the action and what we need (request IDs, timeframe, repro steps).
- State that we will continue monitoring platform health in parallel.
- Do not imply the incident is resolved if the platform issue is still being monitored.

---

## Linking and traceability requirements
To avoid losing context after an incident:
- Every incident bridge must pin:
  - the primary SUP Jira ticket(s)
  - the status page incident link (if created)
  - the current customer-impact summary
- Every SUP ticket must include:
  - Slack bridge link
  - status page link (if any)
  - summary of customer comms sent (timestamps + key promises)

This ensures post-incident review can reconcile:
- what was known when
- what customers were told
- whether update cadence matched policy

---

## Common scenarios
### Scenario 1: Region degradation (Hosted API)
- Create #incidents channel
- Open status page incident within 10 minutes of confirmation
- Support: send initial acknowledgement referencing the status page and collect request IDs

### Scenario 2: Dedicated customer sees latency spikes (single tenant)
- Start Support bridge
- If isolated to a single tenant and mitigated via config/capacity adjustment: no status page
- If similar symptoms appear across multiple Dedicated tenants or shared infrastructure: status page

### Scenario 3: Known issue causing repeated tickets (not real-time)
- No status page
- Publish/refresh KB article and add docs banner if needed
- Update Support macros to link workaround and “when to escalate” criteria

---

## Escalation for comms conflicts
If Support and IC disagree on what to say (scope/ETA/impact):
1) Pause outbound updates (unless required by SLA cadence—then send a “no material change” update).
2) Resolve in bridge with IC + Support Lead.
3) If still blocked for >10 minutes, escalate to:
- VP CS/Support sponsor (Jasmine Liu or Rachel Kim)
- SRE on-call lead (Sean Gallagher or Rafael Mendes)

---

## Compliance and sensitivity
- Do not include customer names, contract terms, or credit decisions on the status page.
- Do not paste customer logs into broadly visible Slack channels. Use approved secure sharing paths and reference request IDs.
- If there is any security/compliance concern, loop in Security/Legal before posting cause statements.

---

## Change log
- 2026-02-16: Clarified criteria for Dedicated/Enterprise status page usage and standardized required fields for initial updates.
- 2026-02-10: Added explicit traceability requirements for pinning SUP tickets and status links in bridge channels.
- 2026-01-22: Initial publication.

  - status page link (if any)
  - summary of customer comms sent (timestamps + key promises)

This ensures post-incident review can reconcile:
- what was known when
- what customers were told
- whether update cadence matched policy

---

## Common scenarios
### Scenario 1: Region degradation (Hosted API)
- Create #incidents channel
- Open status page incident within 10 minutes of confirmation
- Support: send initial acknowledgement referencing the status page and collect request IDs

### Scenario 2: Dedicated customer sees latency spikes (single tenant)
- Start Support bridge
- If isolated to a single tenant and mitigated via config/capacity adjustment: no status page
- If similar symptoms appear across multiple Dedicated tenants or shared infrastructure: status page

### Scenario 3: Known issue causing repeated tickets (not real-time)
- No status page
- Publish/refresh KB article and add docs banner if needed
- Update Support macros to link workaround and “when to escalate” criteria

---

## Escalation for comms conflicts
If Support and IC disagree on what to say (scope/ETA/impact):
1) Pause outbound updates (unless required by SLA cadence—then send a “no material change” update).
2) Resolve in bridge with IC + Support Lead.
3) If still blocked for >10 minutes, escalate to:
- VP CS/Support sponsor (Jasmine Liu or Rachel Kim)
- SRE on-call lead (Sean Gallagher or Rafael Mendes)

---

## Compliance and sensitivity
- Do not include customer names, contract terms, or credit decisions on the status page.
- Do not paste customer logs into broadly visible Slack channels. Use approved secure sharing paths and reference request IDs.
- If there is any security/compliance concern, loop in Security/Legal before posting cause statements.

---

## Change log
- 2026-02-16: Clarified criteria for Dedicated/Enterprise status page usage and standardized required fields for initial updates.
- 2026-02-10: Added explicit traceability requirements for pinning SUP tickets and status links in bridge channels.
- 2026-01-22: Initial publication.
text:
## Purpose
This page defines how Redwood Inference communicates during customer-impacting incidents across:
- **Support customer comms** (email + ticket updates)
- **Slack bridges** (#support bridge channels and #incidents)
- **Status page updates** (public and/or customer-facing status events)

It provides:
1) A shared definition of what qualifies for a **status page incident**
2) A single **approval flow** so customers receive consistent, accurate updates
3) A standard **message structure** so Support, SRE, and Platform are aligned on impact, mitigations, and timelines

This is written to reduce rework, avoid conflicting statements, and keep our comms aligned with on-call practices and customer contract expectations.

---

## Scope and non-goals
### In scope
- Customer-impacting incidents and degradations across Hosted API, Dedicated, and Private (when Redwood is responsible for a shared control plane component).
- Communication alignment and escalation mechanics (who updates what, where, and when).
- When to use:
  - status page incident
  - docs-site banner / help-center banner
  - direct customer comms only

### Out of scope
- Product roadmap or long-term remediation plans (those belong in postmortems/RCAs and engineering planning).
- Customer-specific details that should remain in restricted channels/tickets.

---

## Guiding principles
1) **One source of truth for “current state.”** During an incident, the Incident Commander (IC) owns the canonical status. Support mirrors that status to customers.
2) **Be explicit about confidence.** If the cause is unknown, say so; do not speculate.
3) **Separate impact from cause.** Customers need to understand symptoms, scope, and mitigations even when root cause is still under investigation.
4) **Do not promise ETAs unless approved by IC.** If an ETA is needed, it must be framed as an estimate and tied to a specific action (e.g., rollout completion).
5) **Minimize customer-sensitive detail.** Use request IDs, regions, and model names carefully. Do not include customer identifiers on the status page.
6) **Cadence beats completeness.** Regular updates reduce inbound pressure and improve trust. Updates can be short if there is no change.

---

## Definitions
- **Slack bridge:** A dedicated Slack channel created by Support or on-call to coordinate triage and mitigation for a customer-impacting issue. (See Slack bridge conventions page.)
- **#incidents channel:** The company-wide incident channel used when the issue is broadly customer-impacting or meets incident criteria.
- **Status page incident:** A customer-facing incident entry (public status page or authenticated customer status surface) used for broad impact or contractual expectations.
- **Comms lead:** The person responsible for drafting and posting updates. Often Support lead for customer emails and IC/SRE for status page.

---

## Decision framework: When do we post a status page incident?
Use the criteria below. If any “YES” condition is met, open a status incident unless explicitly waived by IC + CS leadership.

### A) Immediate “YES” criteria
Create a status page incident when any of the following are true:
- **Multi-customer impact** (two or more customers) with confirmed platform-level symptoms (e.g., region-level latency/timeouts, auth errors, widespread 429/5xx).
- **Critical tier impact** (Dedicated/Private/Enterprise) where we expect SLA-related concerns, even if the blast radius is limited.
- **Customer-facing outage/degradation** in a region or product area that is likely to be noticed externally (availability/latency materially degraded).
- **Security or data access incident** that requires coordinated disclosures (follow Security process; status page language must be reviewed by Security/Legal).

### B) “Likely YES” criteria (use judgment)
Usually create a status page incident if:
- Impact persists beyond **15 minutes** with no quick mitigation.
- We are actively performing **traffic shifts/fallbacks/rollbacks** that may change behavior for customers.
- Support is receiving escalating inbound volume (multiple duplicate tickets) indicating broader impact.

### C) “NO” criteria (status page usually not needed)
Do not create a status page incident for:
- A single-customer configuration issue or client-side bug (unless it reveals a platform defect with broader risk).
- Known issue with a documented workaround and no measurable platform degradation.
- Private/on-prem incidents strictly inside the customer’s environment (unless Redwood-managed components are failing and multiple customers are affected).

### D) If not status page: when to use a docs/help-center banner
Use a docs/help-center banner when:
- There is a **known issue** with a validated workaround, and
- Customers are likely to hit it repeatedly, and
- It is not a real-time incident (or it’s resolved but needs proactive guidance).

Owner: Docs/DevEx with Support input. Approval: Support lead + relevant Eng owner.

---

## Severity alignment (comms expectations)
We align comms behavior to Support priority and Engineering incident severity.

- **Support P0 / Engineering Sev0–Sev1:**
  - Status page incident expected.
  - Update cadence: follow enterprise expectations; do not exceed what the incident team can sustain (see SLA policy page).
  - Comms must be synchronized across Support + IC.

- **Support P1 / Engineering Sev1–Sev2:**
  - Status page incident recommended if impact is multi-customer or tier-critical.
  - Updates should include mitigation progress and next check-in.

- **Support P2+ / Engineering Sev2–Sev3:**
  - Status page generally not required unless there is broad impact.
  - Prefer KB article and support macros for repeatability.

Note: Severity classification is owned by the IC/on-call process; Support priority is owned by Support triage. They must be mapped consistently in Jira (see severity matrix and Jira field guidelines).

---

## Roles and responsibilities
### Incident Commander (IC) (on-call)
- Owns incident severity, mitigation decisions, and canonical platform status.
- Approves status page updates and any customer-facing ETA statements.

### Support Lead / Escalation Manager (Support on duty)
- Owns customer ticket prioritization and direct customer comms.
- Ensures Support messaging matches the status page and IC’s current assessment.
- Opens/maintains the Support Slack bridge, ensures the bridge includes required context.

### Comms Lead (may be IC or delegated)
- Drafts the status page updates and ensures timestamps and scope are correct.
- Keeps language consistent with customer emails.

### SRE/Platform subject matter owners
- Provide verified technical summaries and mitigation steps.
- Review any statements about cause, blast radius, and expected time-to-mitigation.

### Security/Legal (as needed)
- Review language for security, privacy, compliance, or contractual constraints.

---

## Standard approval flow
### 1) Start: create a bridge and establish canonical context
When customer impact is suspected:
1. Support starts a Slack bridge per conventions.
2. First bridge message must include:
   - Symptoms (what customer observes)
   - Region(s)
   - Model(s)/endpoint(s)
   - Time window (UTC)
   - Example request IDs (customer-safe)
   - Current customer tier(s) impacted (Hosted/Dedicated/Private/Enterprise)
   - Links: Jira SUP ticket(s), relevant dashboards/runbooks
3. If criteria indicates broad impact, move coordination to **#incidents** or create an incident channel.

### 2) Decide: status page incident vs direct comms
- IC + Support Lead decide within **10 minutes** of confirmation.
- If uncertain, default to **opening a status incident** with cautious language (“Investigating”).

### 3) Draft and approve first public update
- Status page initial post must be approved by:
  - IC (required)
  - Security/Legal (only if security/compliance related)
- Support email can be sent after IC approves the core wording OR it can reference the status page entry once live.

### 4) Ongoing updates
- Status page updates are posted by Comms Lead with IC approval.
- Support mirrors updates to:
  - affected customer tickets
  - enterprise email threads as needed
- If there is divergence (Support needs customer-specific guidance), keep details in direct comms; do not add customer-specific details to status page.

### 5) Resolution and follow-up
- Status page: “Monitoring” → “Resolved” transitions must match actual platform behavior.
- Support resolution email/ticket update must include:
  - what changed (mitigation)
  - customer action required (if any)
  - how to confirm recovery
  - pointer to RCA timeline if/when available

---

## Message structure (use consistently)
Use this structure for both status page updates and customer emails (tone adjusted by channel).

### Required fields
- **What’s happening (symptom):** e.g., elevated timeouts / streaming disconnects
- **Who is affected (scope):** region(s), endpoint(s), model families (no customer names)
- **When it started (time):** include timezone (prefer UTC)
- **What we’re doing:** investigation, mitigation, rollback, traffic shift
- **What customers can do now:** workaround guidance if safe
- **Next update time:** a specific timestamp OR “within X minutes”

### Language guidelines
- Use “We are investigating” when root cause is not confirmed.
- Use “We believe” only when confidence is moderate and state the evidence.
- Avoid:
  - exact internal system names not meaningful to customers
  - speculation about third-party providers unless confirmed
  - detailed infrastructure topology

---

## Templates
### Status page: Initial update (Investigating)
**Title:** [Region/Product] Degraded performance for [endpoint]

**Body:**
We are investigating an issue causing [symptom] for [scope: region(s), endpoint(s), model(s) if relevant] starting at approximately [time UTC].

Current impact: [brief, measurable if possible: elevated error rates / increased latency].

We will provide another update by [time UTC] or sooner as we learn more.

### Status page: Mitigation in progress
We have identified [probable/confirmed] cause related to [high-level cause]. We are implementing mitigation by [action: rollback/traffic shift/capacity increase].

Impact remains [describe]. Next update by [time UTC].

### Status page: Resolved
The issue has been mitigated and service has returned to normal levels as of [time UTC]. We will continue to monitor.

---

## Keeping Support emails aligned with the status page
### Rules of thumb
- If a status page incident exists, **link it** in customer comms and avoid re-paraphrasing details differently.
- Support comms may add:
  - customer-specific confirmation steps
  - customer-specific workarounds or config changes
  - account-level investigations (quota, routing policy)
- Support comms must not add:
  - unapproved ETAs
  - unverified root cause
  - statements that contradict the status page scope (e.g., claiming “only you” while a status incident is open)

### Handling “Waiting on Customer” without SLA confusion
If customer action is required:
- Explicitly list the action and what we need (request IDs, timeframe, repro steps).
- State that we will continue monitoring platform health in parallel.
- Do not imply the incident is resolved if the platform issue is still being monitored.

---

## Linking and traceability requirements
To avoid losing context after an incident:
- Every incident bridge must pin:
  - the primary SUP Jira ticket(s)
  - the status page incident link (if created)
  - the current customer-impact summary
- Every SUP ticket must include:
  - Slack bridge link
  - status page link (if any)
  - summary of customer comms sent (timestamps + key promises)

This ensures post-incident review can reconcile:
- what was known when
- what customers were told
- whether update cadence matched policy

---

## Common scenarios
### Scenario 1: Region degradation (Hosted API)
- Create #incidents channel
- Open status page incident within 10 minutes of confirmation
- Support: send initial acknowledgement referencing the status page and collect request IDs

### Scenario 2: Dedicated customer sees latency spikes (single tenant)
- Start Support bridge
- If isolated to a single tenant and mitigated via config/capacity adjustment: no status page
- If similar symptoms appear across multiple Dedicated tenants or shared infrastructure: status page

### Scenario 3: Known issue causing repeated tickets (not real-time)
- No status page
- Publish/refresh KB article and add docs banner if needed
- Update Support macros to link workaround and “when to escalate” criteria

---

## Escalation for comms conflicts
If Support and IC disagree on what to say (scope/ETA/impact):
1) Pause outbound updates (unless required by SLA cadence—then send a “no material change” update).
2) Resolve in bridge with IC + Support Lead.
3) If still blocked for >10 minutes, escalate to:
- VP CS/Support sponsor (Jasmine Liu or Rachel Kim)
- SRE on-call lead (Sean Gallagher or Rafael Mendes)

---

## Compliance and sensitivity
- Do not include customer names, contract terms, or credit decisions on the status page.
- Do not paste customer logs into broadly visible Slack channels. Use approved secure sharing paths and reference request IDs.
- If there is any security/compliance concern, loop in Security/Legal before posting cause statements.

---

## Change log
- 2026-02-16: Clarified criteria for Dedicated/Enterprise status page usage and standardized required fields for initial updates.
- 2026-02-10: Added explicit traceability requirements for pinning SUP tickets and status links in bridge channels.
- 2026-01-22: Initial publication.

source.title: P0 Incident Postmortem: Control plane API outage during config rollout
source.author: Aisha Rahman
source.body:
## Summary
On 2026-02-07, the control plane API returned elevated 5xx and became unavailable for ~14 minutes. The data plane continued serving most requests, but customers could not create keys, change routing policies, or update deployments.

## Severity
P0

## Customer impact
- Control plane unavailable for 14 minutes (peak error rate 82%).
- Console actions failed; CI/CD integrations relying on control plane were blocked.

## Timeline (UTC)
- 09:42: Automated rollout begins (config schema update).
- 09:47: 5xx spikes; autoscaler adds pods but errors persist.
- 09:52: Rollout paused; manual rollback attempted.
- 09:56: Traffic restored after reverting schema + clearing stale caches.

## Root cause
A new config schema field triggered an unbounded cache key cardinality in the policy evaluator service. Under normal read/write load, memory pressure caused repeated OOM kills and cascading restarts.

## Detection
Detected via alert: control-plane 5xx > 5% for 2 minutes.

## Follow-up action items (count these)
1) Add a hard cap on policy evaluator cache size and evict by LRU. Owning team: Eng Platform.
2) Add integration test coverage for config schema changes with realistic payload sizes. Owning team: Eng Platform.
3) Add memory limit guardrails and pre-rollout canary with automatic halt on OOM restart rate. Owning team: Eng SRE.
4) Create runbook for fast rollback of control plane schema migrations. Owning team: Eng SRE.
5) Add static analysis to detect cache key explosions for new schema fields. Owning team: Eng Platform.
6) Update change-management checklist to require performance review for policy evaluator changes. Owning team: Eng SRE.

## What went well
- Automated alerting and on-call response were fast.

## What didn’t go well
- Rollout gates did not include memory pressure indicators.

## References
- Service: policy-evaluator
- Related Confluence: change-management standards

source.title: Holiday & Maintenance Window On-call Orchestration and Customer Communication Toolkit
source.author: Ava Chen
source.body:
Overview

Purpose: This document standardizes how Redwood operates scheduled maintenance and holiday windows when staffing is reduced. It covers on-call rotation adaptations, a concise operational runbook for scheduled degradations, pre- and post-maintenance game-day exercises, and customer-facing status templates. The goal is to maintain SLOs where possible, minimize customer surprise, and ensure a clear, timeboxed recovery path if regressions occur.

Scope
- Services in-scope: redwood-serving, routing-proxy, kv-cache, console-api, billing-queue, telemetry-ingest.
- Applies to: company-approved maintenance (deploys, infra upgrades, region maintenance) and planned holiday staffing reductions (global public holidays, end-of-year window).

Key principles
- Prioritize availability-critical routes: routing-proxy and redwood-serving production routes must be treated with highest priority.
- Timebox actions: every remediation step must have a max timeout (see runbook).
- Proactive comms: notify customers early with clear impact tiers and expected windows.
- Escalation guardrails: rely on compensating automation (feature flags, auto-fallbacks) before escalations when on-call headcount is limited.

SLOs and thresholds to drive action
- Availability SLO: 99.95% (monthly) for core generation endpoints (redwood-serving).
- Latency SLO: p95 < 120ms, p99 < 500ms (production read-only endpoints).
- Error budget policy during maintenance: allow up to 0.5% extra budget consumption during approved maintenance windows; any breach > 1.5x planned consumption triggers immediate escalation.

Pre-maintenance checklist (72-24 hours)
1. Notify internal stakeholders: product, sales engineering, customer-success, security.
2. Publish customer advisory: use template A (see templates section) at T-minus 72h and T-minus 24h.
3. Verify auto-fallback and read-only modes for console-api and billing-queue.
4. Confirm backup snapshots: kv-cache snapshot and model-serving checkpoint saved to GCS: gs://redwood-backups/<date>.
5. Identify test prompts and smoke-checks (see quick smoke list).
6. Assign holiday on-call roster: ensure primary + 1 backup are reachable.

Staffing matrix example (holiday window)
| Timezone coverage | Primary on-call | Backup | Bridge operator | Customer comms lead | Notes |
| UTC-8 (Americas) | eng-oncall-a | eng-oncall-b | platform-bridge | cs-duty-a | Focus peak PST hours |
| UTC+0 (EMEA) | eng-oncall-c | eng-oncall-d | platform-bridge | cs-duty-b | Overlap with EMEA customers |
| UTC+8 (APAC) | eng-oncall-e (on-call light) | eng-oncall-f | platform-bridge | cs-duty-c | Night watch; escalation path to Americas |

On-call rotation adjustments for holiday periods
- Shorten primary shift length to 6 hours for holiday storm windows to keep cognitive load low.
- Each primary must designate a reachable backup who can take full command in the primary's absence.
- Implement an "escalation blackout" on non-critical noise (low-severity alerts aggregated); critical SLO breaches still page immediately.

Runbook: Scheduled Maintenance with Reduced Staffing
(Use this for planned deploys or infra upgrades occurring in holiday windows)
1. Pre-window (T-24h): confirm checklist done and bridge created, publish advisory.
2. Start window (T0): announce on internal #ops-bridge and post abbreviated status page message (Template B).
3. Smoke tests (T+5m): run 3 core probes: healthcheck /health for redwood-serving, 3 synthetic token generations for a known prompt set, and billing-queue enqueue/dequeue check.
   - Commands:
     - kubectl rollout status deployment/redwood-serving -n prod --timeout=5m
     - curl -sS https://health.prod.redwood.ai/health | jq .status
     - ./tools/smoke-test --suite holiday-smoke --region us-west1
4. If smoke tests pass, continue with stepwise rollout (canary 5%, 25%, 100%) with 15-minute observation windows.
5. If p99 latency increases > 2x baseline or errors spike > 0.5% absolute: trigger canary pause and rollback to last known good.
6. Rollback command (example): kubectl rollout undo deployment/redwood-serving -n prod --to-revision=<rev>
7. If rollback fails or anomalies persist > 30m: escalate to on-call lead and open the incident bridge. Bridge operator follows the bridge script below.
8. Post-window: publish completion note, run extended health checks for 4 hours, and kickoff postmortem if any Service Level Objective was violated.

Bridge operator script (timeboxed actions)
- Within first 10 minutes: collect evidence links (Grafana dashboard, Kibana query, deployment history, GCS backup path) and pin in bridge chat. Evidence quick list:
  - Grafana: dashboards/serving/latency-overview (p95/p99)
  - Kibana query: index=prod-logs service=redwood-serving errors
  - Deployment history from CI: ci/builds/<commit-id>
- 10-30 minutes: run mitigation list: temporary route shift to warm pool, enable model-fallback to smaller quantized variant, increase autoscale target.
- 30-60 minutes: if mitigation unsuccessful, invoke cross-region failover (pre-approved only if customer impact > 1% or major SLO breach).

Escalation matrix (holiday simplified)
- Pager -> primary on-call (15 min) -> backup on-call (10 min) -> on-call lead (5 min) -> SRE manager -> product/CS notified if impact > P2 for > 30 min.
- For security incidents, route directly to security@redwood and the on-call security engineer.

Customer communication templates
- Template A (72h advisory):
Subject: Scheduled maintenance on <YYYY-MM-DD> — limited staffing window
Body: We plan routine maintenance on <service> on <date> between <start> and <end> (UTC). We expect minimal disruption. If you use dedicated/private deployments, your account team will contact you directly. For hosted customers: the platform will remain available; fallback routes may produce slightly higher latency for up to 30 minutes. Contact support@redwood.ai for concerns.

- Template B (In-window status page short message):
Status: Partial degradation — performing scheduled maintenance
Details: We are performing scheduled maintenance affecting generation latency in <region>. We are observing elevated latency; teams are following the maintenance runbook. Expected resolution: T+30–90 minutes.

- Template C (Customer-facing incident update for SLA-impact):
Subject: Incident update — degraded generation on <YYYY-MM-DD>
Body: We identified an issue during scheduled maintenance that impacted generation endpoints for some customers in <region>. Mitigations were applied and service was restored at <timestamp> UTC. We are investigating root cause and will share a detailed postmortem within 72 hours.

Internal Slack/Bridge announcement samples
- Start: "[MAINT] Starting scheduled maintenance for redwood-serving in us-west1. Bridge: <link>. Primary: @eng-oncall-a, Backup: @eng-oncall-b. Smoke tests running T+5m."
- Issue detected: "[ALERT] p99 latency spike to 1.2s for redwood-serving-canary. Pausing rollout. Running rollback (cmd). Bridge: <link>."

Game-day scenarios (recommended exercises)
1. Holiday-light staffing drill (2h exercise): simulate a canary failing during a midnight deploy with only primary + backup available. Objectives: test bridge script, customer comm cadence, and cross-region failover.
2. Fail-and-rollback drill (1h): intentionally introduce a faulty model artifact in a test cluster and practice rollback and postmortem execution under holiday staffing constraints.

Postmortem and evidence checklist (if SLO breached or customer impact > P2)
- Postmortem must be posted within 72 hours and include: timeline (UTC), impact summary (customers affected, routes), root cause hypothesis, evidence list (dashboards, logs, deployment SHAs), mitigations taken, action items with owner and ETA, and follow-up verification plan.
- Evidence items to attach: Grafana panels (PNG link), Kibana query exports (ndjson), deployment manifest (yaml), GCS backup path, smoke-test outputs.

Postmortem template (publish as confluence page)
1. Title: Postmortem — <short description> — <YYYY-MM-DD>
2. Impact summary (bullet points)
3. Timeline (table with UTC timestamps and actor)
4. Root cause analysis (clear causal chain)
5. Immediate mitigations and timeline
6. Long-term fixes and owners (action, owner, due date)
7. Verification plan (how we will measure success)
8. Lessons learned and follow-ups

Operational automation snippets
- Autoscale bump (example):
  gcloud compute --project=redwood-infra autoscaler update ... (internal scripted tool)
- Canary rollback helper (internal CLI):
  rhubarb rollback --service redwood-serving --env prod --to-revision <rev>

Metrics and reporting post-window
- Run 24-hour snapshot of SLO metrics and compare to baseline. If error budget consumed > planned threshold, schedule a dedicated follow-up review with SRE and product.

Governance and approvals
- Any cross-region failover during holiday windows must be approved by SRE manager and on-call lead.
- Emergency exceptions (e.g., critical data loss) allow on-call lead to act immediately and notify execs within 30 minutes.

Appendix: smoke-check quick list
- Generation sanity: 3 synthetic prompts across small/large models, verify tokens and latency < p95 baseline*2.
- KV cache consistency: read a known key pre/post-deploy.
- Billing queue round-trip: enqueue/test consumer ack.

Notes and references
- Related runbooks: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbook-retention-and-responder-onboarding-handbook-2026
- Deployment policy: /confluence/eng-infra/ci-cd-and-build/deployment-policy

Contact and ownership
- Document owner: Ava Chen (oncall coordinator)
- Primary contacts: eng-oncall-rotations@redwood.ai, platform-bridge@redwood.ai, customer-success@redwood.ai

This toolkit is intended to be a compact operational reference for holiday and reduced-staff maintenance windows. Update this page after each holiday window runbook exercise with concrete lessons and revised templates.

title: Holiday & Maintenance Window On-call Orchestration and Customer Communication Toolkit
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This document standardizes how Redwood operates scheduled maintenance and holiday windows when staffing is reduced. It covers on-call rotation adaptations, a concise operational runbook for scheduled degradations, pre- and post-maintenance game-day exercises, and customer-facing status templates. The goal is to maintain SLOs where possible, minimize customer surprise, and ensure a clear, timeboxed recovery path if regressions occur.

Scope
- Services in-scope: redwood-serving, routing-proxy, kv-cache, console-api, billing-queue, telemetry-ingest.
- Applies to: company-approved maintenance (deploys, infra upgrades, region maintenance) and planned holiday staffing reductions (global public holidays, end-of-year window).

Key principles
- Prioritize availability-critical routes: routing-proxy and redwood-serving production routes must be treated with highest priority.
- Timebox actions: every remediation step must have a max timeout (see runbook).
- Proactive comms: notify customers early with clear impact tiers and expected windows.
- Escalation guardrails: rely on compensating automation (feature flags, auto-fallbacks) before escalations when on-call headcount is limited.

SLOs and thresholds to drive action
- Availability SLO: 99.95% (monthly) for core generation endpoints (redwood-serving).
- Latency SLO: p95 < 120ms, p99 < 500ms (production read-only endpoints).
- Error budget policy during maintenance: allow up to 0.5% extra budget consumption during approved maintenance windows; any breach > 1.5x planned consumption triggers immediate escalation.

Pre-maintenance checklist (72-24 hours)
1. Notify internal stakeholders: product, sales engineering, customer-success, security.
2. Publish customer advisory: use template A (see templates section) at T-minus 72h and T-minus 24h.
3. Verify auto-fallback and read-only modes for console-api and billing-queue.
4. Confirm backup snapshots: kv-cache snapshot and model-serving checkpoint saved to GCS: gs://redwood-backups/<date>.
5. Identify test prompts and smoke-checks (see quick smoke list).
6. Assign holiday on-call roster: ensure primary + 1 backup are reachable.

Staffing matrix example (holiday window)
| Timezone coverage | Primary on-call | Backup | Bridge operator | Customer comms lead | Notes |
| UTC-8 (Americas) | eng-oncall-a | eng-oncall-b | platform-bridge | cs-duty-a | Focus peak PST hours |
| UTC+0 (EMEA) | eng-oncall-c | eng-oncall-d | platform-bridge | cs-duty-b | Overlap with EMEA customers |
| UTC+8 (APAC) | eng-oncall-e (on-call light) | eng-oncall-f | platform-bridge | cs-duty-c | Night watch; escalation path to Americas |

On-call rotation adjustments for holiday periods
- Shorten primary shift length to 6 hours for holiday storm windows to keep cognitive load low.
- Each primary must designate a reachable backup who can take full command in the primary's absence.
- Implement an "escalation blackout" on non-critical noise (low-severity alerts aggregated); critical SLO breaches still page immediately.

Runbook: Scheduled Maintenance with Reduced Staffing
(Use this for planned deploys or infra upgrades occurring in holiday windows)
1. Pre-window (T-24h): confirm checklist done and bridge created, publish advisory.
2. Start window (T0): announce on internal #ops-bridge and post abbreviated status page message (Template B).
3. Smoke tests (T+5m): run 3 core probes: healthcheck /health for redwood-serving, 3 synthetic token generations for a known prompt set, and billing-queue enqueue/dequeue check.
   - Commands:
     - kubectl rollout status deployment/redwood-serving -n prod --timeout=5m
     - curl -sS https://health.prod.redwood.ai/health | jq .status
     - ./tools/smoke-test --suite holiday-smoke --region us-west1
4. If smoke tests pass, continue with stepwise rollout (canary 5%, 25%, 100%) with 15-minute observation windows.
5. If p99 latency increases > 2x baseline or errors spike > 0.5% absolute: trigger canary pause and rollback to last known good.
6. Rollback command (example): kubectl rollout undo deployment/redwood-serving -n prod --to-revision=<rev>
7. If rollback fails or anomalies persist > 30m: escalate to on-call lead and open the incident bridge. Bridge operator follows the bridge script below.
8. Post-window: publish completion note, run extended health checks for 4 hours, and kickoff postmortem if any Service Level Objective was violated.

Bridge operator script (timeboxed actions)
- Within first 10 minutes: collect evidence links (Grafana dashboard, Kibana query, deployment history, GCS backup path) and pin in bridge chat. Evidence quick list:
  - Grafana: dashboards/serving/latency-overview (p95/p99)
  - Kibana query: index=prod-logs service=redwood-serving errors
  - Deployment history from CI: ci/builds/<commit-id>
- 10-30 minutes: run mitigation list: temporary route shift to warm pool, enable model-fallback to smaller quantized variant, increase autoscale target.
- 30-60 minutes: if mitigation unsuccessful, invoke cross-region failover (pre-approved only if customer impact > 1% or major SLO breach).

Escalation matrix (holiday simplified)
- Pager -> primary on-call (15 min) -> backup on-call (10 min) -> on-call lead (5 min) -> SRE manager -> product/CS notified if impact > P2 for > 30 min.
- For security incidents, route directly to security@redwood and the on-call security engineer.

Customer communication templates
- Template A (72h advisory):
Subject: Scheduled maintenance on <YYYY-MM-DD> — limited staffing window
Body: We plan routine maintenance on <service> on <date> between <start> and <end> (UTC). We expect minimal disruption. If you use dedicated/private deployments, your account team will contact you directly. For hosted customers: the platform will remain available; fallback routes may produce slightly higher latency for up to 30 minutes. Contact support@redwood.ai for concerns.

- Template B (In-window status page short message):
Status: Partial degradation — performing scheduled maintenance
Details: We are performing scheduled maintenance affecting generation latency in <region>. We are observing elevated latency; teams are following the maintenance runbook. Expected resolution: T+30–90 minutes.

- Template C (Customer-facing incident update for SLA-impact):
Subject: Incident update — degraded generation on <YYYY-MM-DD>
Body: We identified an issue during scheduled maintenance that impacted generation endpoints for some customers in <region>. Mitigations were applied and service was restored at <timestamp> UTC. We are investigating root cause and will share a detailed postmortem within 72 hours.

Internal Slack/Bridge announcement samples
- Start: "[MAINT] Starting scheduled maintenance for redwood-serving in us-west1. Bridge: <link>. Primary: @eng-oncall-a, Backup: @eng-oncall-b. Smoke tests running T+5m."
- Issue detected: "[ALERT] p99 latency spike to 1.2s for redwood-serving-canary. Pausing rollout. Running rollback (cmd). Bridge: <link>."

Game-day scenarios (recommended exercises)
1. Holiday-light staffing drill (2h exercise): simulate a canary failing during a midnight deploy with only primary + backup available. Objectives: test bridge script, customer comm cadence, and cross-region failover.
2. Fail-and-rollback drill (1h): intentionally introduce a faulty model artifact in a test cluster and practice rollback and postmortem execution under holiday staffing constraints.

Postmortem and evidence checklist (if SLO breached or customer impact > P2)
- Postmortem must be posted within 72 hours and include: timeline (UTC), impact summary (customers affected, routes), root cause hypothesis, evidence list (dashboards, logs, deployment SHAs), mitigations taken, action items with owner and ETA, and follow-up verification plan.
- Evidence items to attach: Grafana panels (PNG link), Kibana query exports (ndjson), deployment manifest (yaml), GCS backup path, smoke-test outputs.

Postmortem template (publish as confluence page)
1. Title: Postmortem — <short description> — <YYYY-MM-DD>
2. Impact summary (bullet points)
3. Timeline (table with UTC timestamps and actor)
4. Root cause analysis (clear causal chain)
5. Immediate mitigations and timeline
6. Long-term fixes and owners (action, owner, due date)
7. Verification plan (how we will measure success)
8. Lessons learned and follow-ups

Operational automation snippets
- Autoscale bump (example):
  gcloud compute --project=redwood-infra autoscaler update ... (internal scripted tool)
- Canary rollback helper (internal CLI):
  rhubarb rollback --service redwood-serving --env prod --to-revision <rev>

Metrics and reporting post-window
- Run 24-hour snapshot of SLO metrics and compare to baseline. If error budget consumed > planned threshold, schedule a dedicated follow-up review with SRE and product.

Governance and approvals
- Any cross-region failover during holiday windows must be approved by SRE manager and on-call lead.
- Emergency exceptions (e.g., critical data loss) allow on-call lead to act immediately and notify execs within 30 minutes.

Appendix: smoke-check quick list
- Generation sanity: 3 synthetic prompts across small/large models, verify tokens and latency < p95 baseline*2.
- KV cache consistency: read a known key pre/post-deploy.
- Billing queue round-trip: enqueue/test consumer ack.

Notes and references
- Related runbooks: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbook-retention-and-responder-onboarding-handbook-2026
- Deployment policy: /confluence/eng-infra/ci-cd-and-build/deployment-policy

Contact and ownership
- Document owner: Ava Chen (oncall coordinator)
- Primary contacts: eng-oncall-rotations@redwood.ai, platform-bridge@redwood.ai, customer-success@redwood.ai

This toolkit is intended to be a compact operational reference for holiday and reduced-staff maintenance windows. Update this page after each holiday window runbook exercise with concrete lessons and revised templates.
