  4. Verify access: observability dashboard, status page editor, control plane CLI, and runbook edit permissions.
  5. Shadow at least one full shift with the outgoing rotation member.

- First shift objectives:
  1. Acknowledge a synthetic alert and perform the test mitigation (documented in this page).
  2. Post one status-page draft to the staging status editor and get approval from comms liaison.
  3. Run a simulated triage for a degraded KV cache scenario using the playbook.

- Ongoing expectations for rotation members:
  - Keep the runbook index updated with any runbook edits made during incident response.
  - Add short incident notes to the rotation handoff doc before shift end.
  - Attend the monthly rotation retrospective and share one improvement action.

5) Game day playbook (3-hour template)

Objective: validate bypass and failover steps for KV cache and ensure comms cadence and status page flow works.

Schedule:

- 0:00–0:15: Kickoff and objectives, assign roles (IC, primary, secondary, comms, observer).
- 0:15–0:45: Inject alert: KV cache hit ratio dropped to 45%. Trigger PagerDuty to primary.
- 0:45–1:30: Teams perform triage and apply mitigation (bypass KV cache). Observers fill checklist.
- 1:30–2:00: Execute controlled restarts of two cache nodes with stagger.
- 2:00–2:45: Validate recovery, re-enable cache, measure hit ratio and latencies.
- 2:45–3:00: Debrief and capture action items.

Game day deliverables:

- Edited runbook reflecting discovered gaps.
- Communication artifact: status page timeline and comms templates used.
- At least one concrete follow-up with owner and due date.

6) Post-incident learning loop and postmortem template

Timeline and expectations:

- Draft postmortem: publish within 72 hours. Use the lightweight template below.
- Blameless review meeting: within 7 business days.
- Action items: assigned with owners and target dates; tracked in the incident audit board.

Minimal postmortem template (copy into confluence page):

- Title: [date] - [summary]
- Severity: Sev-1/2/3
- Impact summary: customers, regions, duration
- Timeline: concise event timeline with timestamps
- Root cause: summary + links to logs/profiles
- Mitigations taken: immediate and long-term
- Follow-up actions: list with owner and due date
- Lessons learned and prevention plan

7) Operational SLOs and thresholds (extract)

- API generation latency: P95 < 300ms for baseline workloads.
- API availability (success rate): 99.9% monthly.
- Paging threshold: alert when 5-minute error-rate >1% or P95 latency >500ms for 5 minutes.
- Mean time to acknowledge (MTTA) target: < 5 minutes.
- Mean time to mitigation (MTTM) target: < 30 minutes for Sev-1 and Sev-2.

8) Ownership and escalation matrix (roles)

- Primary responder: owns first-line triage and initial mitigation. (Role: oncall-engineer)
- Secondary/backup: provides technical support and takes over if primary is blocked. (Role: oncall-senior)
- Incident Commander (IC): runs coordination, customer comms, and exec updates. (Role: rotation-lead)
- Comms liaison: drafts status and customer messages and coordinates with CS. (Role: support-ops)
- Platform lead: responsible for long-term fixes and action items from postmortem. (Role: platform-engineering)

Escalation ordering (automated via PagerDuty): primary -> secondary (10 minutes) -> rotation-lead (15 more minutes) -> oncall-manager (30 more minutes or at IC discretion).

9) Runbook hygiene and governance

- Every runbook must have an owner and be reviewed quarterly. Add a one-line summary at top with expected MTTR.
- Changes made during incidents: document the change in the runbook change log and notify rotation-lead.
- Runbook retirement: if a runbook has not been exercised or edited for 12 months, mark for review.

Appendix: sample CLI commands and queries

- Check KV cache hit ratio (example):

  ```bash
  curl -sS https://observability.internal/api/query?expr='sum(rate(kv_cache_hits_total[5m]))/sum(rate(kv_cache_lookups_total[5m]))' | jq .data
  ```

- Bypass KV cache (control plane):

  ```bash
  redwood ctl routing update --policy inference-routing --set bypass_kv_cache=true --reason "Incident work"
  ```

- Post a draft status (status page editor CLI):

  ```bash
  statusctl post --title "Investigating increased inference latency" --impact "Partial" --body-file ./status-draft.md
  ```

Document owner and maintenance:

- Owner: Maya Thompson (SRE)
- Maintenance cadence: quarterly review by SRE oncall guild; updates pushed to runbook index.

If you have feedback or want to add a new contingency entry, file a PR against this page or ping the oncall guild channel.

source.title: Swift Response Taskforce Playbook and CSR Briefing Protocol
source.author: Aisha Patel
source.body:
Summary:
This playbook describes the activation, composition, and operating procedures for Redwood’s Swift Response Taskforce (SRT). It standardizes rapid stabilization steps, evidence collection, CSR (customer success representative) brief scripts, and post-incident handoffs for incidents that risk violating customer-facing SLOs or require immediate customer outreach. Use this document when an incident meets Severity 1 or a high-severity multi-tenant degradation (see "Severity and Activation Matrix").

Purpose and scope:
- Provide a lightweight, reproducible pattern for assembling a cross-functional rapid-response team within the first 15 minutes of detection.
- Ensure consistent, brand-safe customer messaging from first detection through postmortem.
- Define measurable stabilization objectives, evidence artifacts, and closure criteria.

When to use this playbook (activation criteria):
1) Any alert that indicates SLO breach for 1+ customer(s) for more than 5 minutes.
2) Multi-region outage, e.g. >30% increased p99 latency across regions A/B/C.
3) Persistent KV cache thrash or sustained GPU OOMs affecting dedicated pools.
4) Repeated 500s from inference-api for >1% of requests in 10m window.

Severity and Activation Matrix:
| Severity | Criteria | Initial Response SLA | Action |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| Sev-1 (Critical) | Complete service outage or SLO breach impacting many customers | 5 min | Activate SRT, open bridge, exec notify |
| Sev-2 (High) | Significant degradation (p95/p99 latency or increased error rate) affecting tiered customers | 15 min | Pager escalation, partial taskforce |
| Sev-3 (Medium) | Single-customer impact, degraded feature | 1 hour | Owner-level triage, targeted comms |

Roles and responsibilities (SRT roster template):
- Incident Commander (IC): coordinates triage and decisions, owns incident timeline.
- Bridge Operator: maintains bridge, records timestamps, documents commands.
- Primary Responder: investigates root cause signals (logs, profiler, kernel traces).
- Secondary Responder: executes mitigations (rollbacks, traffic diversion, capacity bump).
- Comms Lead (CSR liaison): crafts customer messages, posts status updates, liaises with CS/Support.
- QA/Regression Owner: validates behavior after mitigation and runs smoke tests.
- Postmortem Owner: assigned during closure to track investigation and actions.

Activation checklist (first 15 minutes):
1. Acknowledge alert and create incident ticket (prefix: SRT-YYYYMMDD-###).
2. IC opens bridge and invites primary participants: IC, Bridge, Primary, Secondary, Comms Lead.
3. Set shared document (incident timeline) and start live notes.
4. Bridge Operator captures: initial alert times, impacted services (inference-api, prefix-cache, routing-service), affected regions, and top metrics.
5. Comms Lead prepares initial customer-safe status message (template below) and gets IC sign-off.
6. Primary responder runs quick health checks:
   - curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{time_total}" https://inference-api.internal/health || true
   - grafana query: error_rate("inference-api", "10m")
   - check kv-cache hit ratio: redis-cli info | grep hit_ratio
7. If mitigation is fast (traffic reroute, capacity bump), execute with Secondary Responder and record exact commands and times.

Stabilization objectives (measurable):
- Objective A: Reduce 5xx error rate to <0.1% of baseline within 20 minutes.
- Objective B: Restore p95 latency to within 1.5x baseline for affected tenants within 30 minutes.
- Objective C: Re-attach evicted KV cache segments or rewarm prefixes to achieve >70% hit rate for top-100 prefixes within 45 minutes.

Evidence capture (must-have artifacts):
- Bridge recording and transcript (link to recording).
- Snapshot of SLO dashboards (p50/p95/p99 latency; 5xx/error-rate; token throughput) at T0 and at stabilization.
- Relevant logs and stack traces (log ranges by time and service).
- Kernel-level profiler output if kernel scheduling suspected (perf traces).
- Deployment/rollout timestamps and git commit ids for any hotfix/rollback.

Command snippets and useful queries (examples):
- List busy pods (k8s): kubectl get pods -n inference -o wide --sort-by=.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount
- Check GPU utilization (nvidia-smi): nvidia-smi --query-gpu=utilization.gpu,memory.used --format=csv
- Retrieve recent 500s in logs (elk): POST /api/logs/_search {"query":{"bool":{"must":[{"match":{"http.status_code":"500"}},{"range":{"@timestamp":{"gte":"now-15m"}}}]}}}
- Force routing to fallback model (CLI): rwood route set inference-api --fallback-model redwood-small-v2 --region us-west-2

Mitigation patterns (approved, order-of-operations):
1. Traffic routing fallback to secondary model variant (low-risk).
2. Prefix cache evictions and rewarming for cold-start storms.
3. Scale out dedicated pools (if capacity bound): increase workers by +25% with autoscale override.
4. Emergency rollback to previous model revision when recent rollout correlates with onset.
5. Short-lived throttling (graceful 429) to protect control plane while restoring caches.

Customer messaging templates (CSR-safe — use placeholders and avoid internal details):
- Initial — acknowledged:
  Subject: [Redwood] We are investigating degraded inference for customers in {region}
  Body: We detected an increase in errors/latency impacting some requests to our inference API in {region}. Our on-call team has been activated and we are actively investigating. We will post an update within 30 minutes or sooner. We apologize for the impact.

- Ongoing — mitigation underway:
  Subject: [Redwood] Update: mitigation in progress for inference degradation
  Body: We’ve identified the likely root cause and are applying mitigations that may include traffic routing and limited throttling to protect stability. You may experience intermittent 5xx responses or slightly higher latency. We expect an improvement within {eta_minutes} minutes.

- Resolved:
  Subject: [Redwood] Resolved: inference service restored in {region}
  Body: The issue impacting inference in {region} is resolved. All customer traffic is restored to normal. We will follow up with a post-incident summary within 72 hours. If you continue to see problems, please contact support@redwood.ai and reference incident {incident_id}.

- Postmortem notice (short):
  Subject: [Redwood] Post-incident summary for {incident_id}
  Body: We published a post-incident summary that includes root cause, timeline, and corrective actions: {postmortem_link}

Game day and simulation guidance (quarterly cadence):
- Frequency: quarterly cross-functional tabletop + 1 full live simulator per year.
- Simulation targets: KV cache storms, multi-tenant p99 latency spike, model rollout regressions.
- Pre-reqs: test cluster snapshot, stubbed customer traffic generator, documented blast-radius limiters.
- Scorecard metrics: time-to-detect, time-to-bridge, time-to-first-mitigation, time-to-stabilize.

On-call rotation and handoffs (practical rules):
- Primary oncall window is 9am-9am local timezone handover. Shadow handover for first 2 weeks after joining rotation.
- Handoff checklist (must be filled in on shift change): active incidents, outstanding actions with owners, current alerts suppressed, contact list, and quiet-window restrictions.
- Escalation ladder: Primary -> Secondary -> IC on-call -> Eng Director. Each step has a 10-minute ack SLA.

Post-incident process and deliverables:
1. Stabilize and close incident.
2. Postmortem draft due within 72 hours. Use template: summary, timeline, impact, root cause, corrective actions (owner + due date), detection & mitigation notes, and follow-ups.
3. Triage meeting within 7 calendar days to prioritize action items.
4. Track actions in tracker: label with "SRT-action" and include owner + SLA.

Closure criteria (hard):
- All stabilization objectives met.
- Postmortem drafted and assigned.
- Customer notice queued and linked.
- Action items created and triaged.

Metrics and SLOs referenced in playbook (examples):
- inference-api: availability SLO = 99.95% monthly (measured by successful token responses not 5xx).
- p95 latency SLO = 250ms for hosted tier in us-west-2.
- KV cache hit-rate baseline = 78% for top-1k prefixes.

Runbook governance and continuous improvement:
- After each SRT activation, the Postmortem Owner must add a short "Playbook feedback" section describing what worked and what should change.
- Quarterly playbook review by eng-sre + product to refresh thresholds and approved mitigation patterns.

Appendix A — Example incident timeline (short):
T+00:00 Detection: Alert fires for increased 5xx rate in us-west-2 (alert id: A-20432)
T+00:02 SRT created, bridge opened, IC assigned (Aisha P.)
T+00:06 Comms Lead posts initial customer message (template used).
T+00:12 Primary responder identifies runaway prefix-eviction pattern in prefix-cache service.
T+00:20 Secondary reroutes a fraction of traffic to fallback model; p95 drops 35%.
T+00:35 Stabilization objectives met; SRT moves to recovery mode.

Appendix B — Templates and quick links:
- Incident ticket template: https://jira.redwood.ai/browse/SRT-{{id}}
- Incident timeline doc template: https://docs.redwood.ai/templates/incident-timeline
- Recording link placeholder: https://meetings.redwood.ai/recordings/{bridge_id}

Appendix C — Escalation contacts (example roles):
- Eng Director (oncall): liam.chen@redwood.ai
- Customer Success escalation: marisol.gomez@redwood.ai
- Security on-call: sec-oncall@redwood.ai (only if data exfiltration suspected)

Notes and change log:
- 2025-09-12 (Aisha Patel) created initial playbook.
- 2026-02-10 (Aisha Patel) updated stabilization objectives and CSR templates after Q4 game day learnings.

End of document.

  Body: The issue impacting inference in {region} is resolved. All customer traffic is restored to normal. We will follow up with a post-incident summary within 72 hours. If you continue to see problems, please contact support@redwood.ai and reference incident {incident_id}.

- Postmortem notice (short):
  Subject: [Redwood] Post-incident summary for {incident_id}
  Body: We published a post-incident summary that includes root cause, timeline, and corrective actions: {postmortem_link}

Game day and simulation guidance (quarterly cadence):
- Frequency: quarterly cross-functional tabletop + 1 full live simulator per year.
- Simulation targets: KV cache storms, multi-tenant p99 latency spike, model rollout regressions.
- Pre-reqs: test cluster snapshot, stubbed customer traffic generator, documented blast-radius limiters.
- Scorecard metrics: time-to-detect, time-to-bridge, time-to-first-mitigation, time-to-stabilize.

On-call rotation and handoffs (practical rules):
- Primary oncall window is 9am-9am local timezone handover. Shadow handover for first 2 weeks after joining rotation.
- Handoff checklist (must be filled in on shift change): active incidents, outstanding actions with owners, current alerts suppressed, contact list, and quiet-window restrictions.
- Escalation ladder: Primary -> Secondary -> IC on-call -> Eng Director. Each step has a 10-minute ack SLA.

Post-incident process and deliverables:
1. Stabilize and close incident.
2. Postmortem draft due within 72 hours. Use template: summary, timeline, impact, root cause, corrective actions (owner + due date), detection & mitigation notes, and follow-ups.
3. Triage meeting within 7 calendar days to prioritize action items.
4. Track actions in tracker: label with \"SRT-action\" and include owner + SLA.

Closure criteria (hard):
- All stabilization objectives met.
- Postmortem drafted and assigned.
- Customer notice queued and linked.
- Action items created and triaged.

Metrics and SLOs referenced in playbook (examples):
- inference-api: availability SLO = 99.95% monthly (measured by successful token responses not 5xx).
- p95 latency SLO = 250ms for hosted tier in us-west-2.
- KV cache hit-rate baseline = 78% for top-1k prefixes.

Runbook governance and continuous improvement:
- After each SRT activation, the Postmortem Owner must add a short \"Playbook feedback\" section describing what worked and what should change.
- Quarterly playbook review by eng-sre + product to refresh thresholds and approved mitigation patterns.

Appendix A — Example incident timeline (short):
T+00:00 Detection: Alert fires for increased 5xx rate in us-west-2 (alert id: A-20432)
T+00:02 SRT created, bridge opened, IC assigned (Aisha P.)
T+00:06 Comms Lead posts initial customer message (template used).
T+00:12 Primary responder identifies runaway prefix-eviction pattern in prefix-cache service.
T+00:20 Secondary reroutes a fraction of traffic to fallback model; p95 drops 35%.
T+00:35 Stabilization objectives met; SRT moves to recovery mode.

Appendix B — Templates and quick links:
- Incident ticket template: https://jira.redwood.ai/browse/SRT-{{id}}
- Incident timeline doc template: https://docs.redwood.ai/templates/incident-timeline
- Recording link placeholder: https://meetings.redwood.ai/recordings/{bridge_id}

Appendix C — Escalation contacts (example roles):
- Eng Director (oncall): liam.chen@redwood.ai
- Customer Success escalation: marisol.gomez@redwood.ai
- Security on-call: sec-oncall@redwood.ai (only if data exfiltration suspected)

Notes and change log:
- 2025-09-12 (Aisha Patel) created initial playbook.
- 2026-02-10 (Aisha Patel) updated stabilization objectives and CSR templates after Q4 game day learnings.

End of document.

source.title: Responder wellbeing and orchestration guide
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
Overview

Purpose: This guide unifies three practical priorities for Redwood responders: (1) a compact incident lifecycle focused on fast containment and humane workload, (2) repeatable runbook patterns and postmortem scaffolding, and (3) on-call rotation and game-day design that preserves responder wellbeing while meeting SLO obligations.

Scope: applies to all teams that operate inference services (api-frontend, inference-router, kv-cache, model-host). This is an operational reference — for team-specific runbooks link to the service page.

Owner and escalation

- Primary owner: Eng SRE (pager rotation managed in OnCall roster).
- Secondary owner: team that owns the impacted service (see escalation matrix below).
- Pager escalation: primary -> secondary -> SRE lead -> CTO on extended incidents (>4 hours).

Escalation matrix (quick view)

| Symptom | Primary | Secondary | Escalate after |
|---|---:|---|---:|
| Elevated error rate (>2x baseline) | api-frontend | inference-router | 30m |
| P95 latency > 500ms for 15m | inference-router | kv-cache | 20m |
| Model mis-scores / quality regression | model-host | Applied ML | 60m |
| Large-scale capacity shortage | SRE capacity | Eng infra | 15m |

Incident lifecycle (recommended lightweight flow)

1) Detect & acknowledge (0-5 min)
   - Pager owner: acknowledge within 5 minutes in PagerDuty.
   - Create incident channel in Slack: #inc-<service>-<shortid>.
2) Triage & classify (5-20 min)
   - Identify customer impact (partial outage / degraded performance / no impact).
   - Record evidence: metric snapshots, logs, recent deploys, config changes.
3) Contain & mitigate (20-90 min)
   - Apply a targeted mitigation (traffic routing, scale-up, circuit-breaker, rollback).
   - Activate fallback model variant if model-host latency or quality is impacted.
4) Restore & verify (90-240 min)
   - Confirm SLO recovery and run smoke tests (see verification checklist).
   - If incident exceeds 4 hours, notify executives and prepare a customer-facing message.
5) Close & learn (post-incident)
   - Run postmortem within 72 hours; include action items with owners and target dates.

Runbook templates (copy and adapt per service)

High latency (p95 > 500ms) runbook

- Preconditions: p95 latency alert triggered; 3x baseline CPU or queue growth observed.
- Immediate checks (do not skip):
  1. Confirm alert: metrics.redwood.com > inference-router > p95 (last 15m).
  2. Check pods: `kubectl -n inference get pods -l app=inference-router`
  3. Inspect node metrics: `gcloud compute instances list --filter="labels:redwood-gpu"` or cloud equivalent.
- Triage steps:
  - Is KV cache miss-rate elevated? `promql: sum(increase(kv_cache_miss_total[15m]))`
  - Any recent deploys? `git log --oneline --decorate -n 5` in the service repo.
- Mitigation options (ordered):
  a) Increase inference-router replicas (temporary): `kubectl scale deployment inference-router --replicas=6`.
  b) Promote cached model shard to hot-cache (see kv-cache ops doc).
  c) Route a subset of traffic to a cheaper, lower-latency model variant via routing policy: set route "latency-fallback" to true in Routing Console.
- Verification:
  - p95 below 300ms sustained for 10 minutes.
  - Synthetic smoke test: `curl -sS https://smoke-redwood.local/health?service=inference-router` returns 200.
- Post-incident note: add observation to the incident timeline and assign postmortem owner.

Postmortem template (required fields)

- Title: concise description + date.
- Severity: Sev2 / Sev3 / Informational.
- Incident lead: name and rotation week.
- Timeline: bullet list of timestamps and key actions (short, 1–2 sentences each).
- Root cause analysis: brief summary and evidence.
- Contributing factors: list of 2–5 items.
- Customer impact: who/what SLA/regions affected; approximate % of requests.
- Action items: table with owner, action, ETA, verification plan.
- Follow-ups: link to monitoring/dashboard changes or new runbook attachments.

On-call rotations and humane shift design

Goals: reduce burnout, make handoffs frictionless, and keep coverage predictable. Recommended model for Redwood (evidence-based):

- Primary weekday rotation: 09:00–17:00 local (8-hour shifts) for teams with high daytime traffic.
- Night/weekend coverage: two-week night-sprint model — one engineer on a 24/7 follow-the-sun primary for 2 weeks, then 4 weeks off from night duty.
- Max continuous pager span: 2 hours per duty window; allow one 30-minute deep-focus block per 8-hour shift.
- Maximum consecutive weeks on-call (night): 2 weeks.

Handoff checklist (required at end of shift)

1. Confirm there are no active incidents assigned.
2. Post a short handoff note in #oncall-handoffs including:
   - Summary of ongoing issues and mitigation state.
   - Any temporary config toggles and how to revert.
   - Expected QPS / capacity considerations.
3. Tag the next responder and wait for verbal acknowledgement.
4. Mark the shift as completed in OnCall roster tool and update the oncall-diary slack thread.

Game day design and objectives

Purpose: verify runbooks, validate routing fallbacks, and rehearse communication cadence. Run once per quarter per team.

Example 3-hour game day agenda:
- 00:00–00:20: kickoff, roles (Incident Lead, Bridge, Communications, SRE buddy, Observability).
- 00:20–00:60: simulated degraded latency event — follow runbook, test routing fallback.
- 00:60–01:20: simulated model quality regression — promote fallback model and run quality smoke tests.
- 01:20–02:00: mass-failure simulation (50% of nodes in us-west) — execute capacity failover and customer rate-limits.
- 02:00–02:30: status page exercise — compose and publish messages (see templates below).
- 02:30–03:00: retro and action capture.

Scoring (simple): containment time (goal < 30m), correctness of messaging, and checklist completion. Include at least one cross-team observer to evaluate comms.

Status page & customer comm templates

Principles for public comms: be accurate, empathetic, and set expectations for remediation and follow-up. Use short subject lines and a visible update cadence (every 30–60 minutes for active incidents).

Template: "Degraded performance affecting inference API (partial outage)"

Headline: "We are experiencing degraded performance for the Redwood inference API in us-west."
Impact: "Customers may see increased latency or timeouts for text generation requests. This affects ~12% of requests routed to us-west."
What we are doing: "Our engineering teams have identified a routing pressure point and are performing a staged traffic reroute to healthy regions. We will provide updates every 30 minutes." 

Template: "Partial service interruption — model quality regression"

Headline: "We have detected a quality regression in the default embeddings model for some queries."
Impact: "A subset of embeddings produced for short-form inputs may be lower quality; downstream reranking may be affected for some customers."
What we are doing: "We have rolled traffic to the previous model version and are running regression checks. If you rely on embeddings quality, you can pin to model 'embed-v2' in the API until we finish verification."

Internal quick message (Slack) when escalating to execs:

"Inc <ID>: severity Sev2, service inference-router, impact: partial outage affecting 12% of requests. Mitigation: traffic reroute in progress. Current ETA to restore: 45m. Owner: Ava Chen (SRE). Next update: +30m."

Verification and evidence collection checklist

- Save metric snapshots (p95, p99, error-rate) for 1 hour before and during incident.
- Export logs for impacted pods (last 60 minutes).
- Record all runbook commands used and config changes.
- Capture status page messages and timestamps.

Responder wellbeing guidance (practical rules)

- Use the 2+2 rule for paging intensity: if an engineer receives >2 pages in 2 hours, discuss on duty to reduce load (scale, automation, or temporary handoff).
- Mandatory cool-down: after an incident rated Sev1 or extended >4 hours the responder gets 12 hours protected time (no noncritical meetings).
- Post-incident check-in: incident lead schedules a 30-minute wellness check with the responder within 48 hours for any Sev1/Sev2 incidents. This is not a performance review.

Appendix: sample commands and snippets

- List inference router pods: `kubectl -n inference get pods -l app=inference-router -o wide`
- Restart a misbehaving pod safely: `kubectl -n inference rollout restart deployment/inference-router --timeout=2m`
- Promote fallback model via API (example):
```
POST https://routing.redwood.internal/v1/route
Body: { "service": "inference", "policy": "latency-fallback", "enabled": true }
```
- Quick smoke test (synthetic): `python tools/smoke_test.py --service inference-router --assert-p95 300`

Appendix: retention & follow-up norms

- Postmortem actions: tracked as issues in the team backlog; owners must set an ETA and verification PR.
- Runbook pruning cadence: every 6 months runbook owners must re-validate steps in game day or during a change window.
- Incident metrics retention: timeline and evidence for Sev2+ retained 2 years.

Contact list (short):
- SRE lead: Ravi Patel (ravi@redwood.com)
- Observability: Lena Morris (lena@redwood.com)
- Applied ML on-call: Priya Singh (priya@redwood.com)

References and links

- SLO decomposition: /confluence/eng-sre/capacity-planning/slo-decomposition-2025
- Runbook library: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbook-retention-and-responder-onboarding-handbook-2026
- Routing console (internal): https://routing-console.internal.redwood/

This guide is intentionally concise. Teams should copy relevant sections into their service-specific runbooks and adopt the wellbeing rules as a minimum standard.

What we are doing: "Our engineering teams have identified a routing pressure point and are performing a staged traffic reroute to healthy regions. We will provide updates every 30 minutes." 

Template: "Partial service interruption — model quality regression"

Headline: "We have detected a quality regression in the default embeddings model for some queries."
Impact: "A subset of embeddings produced for short-form inputs may be lower quality; downstream reranking may be affected for some customers."
What we are doing: "We have rolled traffic to the previous model version and are running regression checks. If you rely on embeddings quality, you can pin to model 'embed-v2' in the API until we finish verification."

Internal quick message (Slack) when escalating to execs:

"Inc <ID>: severity Sev2, service inference-router, impact: partial outage affecting 12% of requests. Mitigation: traffic reroute in progress. Current ETA to restore: 45m. Owner: Ava Chen (SRE). Next update: +30m."

Verification and evidence collection checklist

- Save metric snapshots (p95, p99, error-rate) for 1 hour before and during incident.
- Export logs for impacted pods (last 60 minutes).
- Record all runbook commands used and config changes.
- Capture status page messages and timestamps.

Responder wellbeing guidance (practical rules)

- Use the 2+2 rule for paging intensity: if an engineer receives >2 pages in 2 hours, discuss on duty to reduce load (scale, automation, or temporary handoff).
- Mandatory cool-down: after an incident rated Sev1 or extended >4 hours the responder gets 12 hours protected time (no noncritical meetings).
- Post-incident check-in: incident lead schedules a 30-minute wellness check with the responder within 48 hours for any Sev1/Sev2 incidents. This is not a performance review.

Appendix: sample commands and snippets

- List inference router pods: `kubectl -n inference get pods -l app=inference-router -o wide`
- Restart a misbehaving pod safely: `kubectl -n inference rollout restart deployment/inference-router --timeout=2m`
- Promote fallback model via API (example):
```
POST https://routing.redwood.internal/v1/route
Body: { "service": "inference", "policy": "latency-fallback", "enabled": true }
```
- Quick smoke test (synthetic): `python tools/smoke_test.py --service inference-router --assert-p95 300`

Appendix: retention & follow-up norms

- Postmortem actions: tracked as issues in the team backlog; owners must set an ETA and verification PR.
- Runbook pruning cadence: every 6 months runbook owners must re-validate steps in game day or during a change window.
- Incident metrics retention: timeline and evidence for Sev2+ retained 2 years.

Contact list (short):
- SRE lead: Ravi Patel (ravi@redwood.com)
- Observability: Lena Morris (lena@redwood.com)
- Applied ML on-call: Priya Singh (priya@redwood.com)

References and links

- SLO decomposition: /confluence/eng-sre/capacity-planning/slo-decomposition-2025
- Runbook library: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbook-retention-and-responder-onboarding-handbook-2026
- Routing console (internal): https://routing-console.internal.redwood/

This guide is intentionally concise. Teams should copy relevant sections into their service-specific runbooks and adopt the wellbeing rules as a minimum standard.

source.title: Incident Classification Policy (incident_type + owner required)
source.author: Rafael Mendes
source.content:
## Purpose
This policy defines when and how incidents must be classified using the **Incident Taxonomy v1** (incident_type + subtype), who is responsible for setting/updating classification during an incident, how to handle “unknown” classifications, and the enforcement rules that prevent closing incidents without required fields.

Classification is required so we can:
- Route mitigation work to the correct owning team quickly.
- Track incident volume and impact by type/owner.
- Measure remediation SLA compliance by severity and customer tier.
- Reduce recurring incidents through clear accountability.

## Scope
This policy applies to all incidents created via incident-bot and tracked in our incident tooling, including:
- Customer-impacting incidents (Hosted API, Dedicated, Private).
- High-severity internal incidents that threaten reliability (even if impact is not yet confirmed).

This policy does **not** replace root-cause analysis; detailed causal analysis remains in postmortems.

## Definitions
- **incident_type / subtype:** Required taxonomy fields selected from the v1 taxonomy list.
- **Owner (primary):** The single team accountable for mitigation coordination and driving the fix plan for the incident’s primary type.
- **Severity:** Sev0–Sev3 as defined in the standard incident severity rubric.
- **Tier:** Hosted / Dedicated / Private (choose the tier primarily impacted; if multiple, use the highest commitment tier impacted and note the others).
- **IC:** Incident Commander.
- **Scribe:** Person responsible for timeline and artifacts.

## Policy summary (requirements)
1) **Every incident must have incident_type + subtype set**.
2) **Every incident must have a primary owner team set**.
3) For **Sev0/Sev1**, classification must be set early (see timing below) and revisited during the incident.
4) Incidents **cannot be closed** (resolved/closed state in incident-bot) without:
   - incident_type
   - subtype
   - primary owner
   - tier
   - (Sev0/Sev1) a postmortem link or placeholder follow-up ticket

## When classification is required
### Incidents requiring immediate classification
Classification is required for **all severities**, but with different time expectations:
- **Sev0 / Sev1:** classification required within **10 minutes** of incident declaration (or incident-bot creation time, whichever is earlier).
- **Sev2:** classification required within **30 minutes**.
- **Sev3:** classification required before marking the incident resolved.

### Incidents created from alerts (pre-incident)
If an alert creates an incident automatically (or an on-call opens one preemptively):
- Set a best-effort incident_type/subtype immediately.
- If impact is unconfirmed, use the closest likely type and add a note in the incident channel: “Impact unconfirmed; classification provisional.”

## Ownership and responsibility (who sets/updates classification)
### RACI (practical)
- **Incident Commander (IC)**
  - Accountable for ensuring classification is set and accurate.
  - May delegate the act of setting fields, but not the outcome.

- **Scribe (or delegated responder)**
  - Responsible for entering/updating incident_type/subtype and confirming the owner in incident-bot.
  - Responsible for documenting any uncertainty and when the classification will be revisited.

- **Primary owner on-call**
  - Consulted on correctness of type/subtype and assists with refining classification.
  - Responsible for ensuring follow-up fix ownership is correctly assigned once mitigation is underway.

- **SRE on-call / SRE Duty Manager**
  - Consulted for edge cases and multi-cause incidents.
  - Responsible for arbitration if ownership/classification is disputed.

## How to classify during a live incident
### Step-by-step procedure
1) **Pick the primary symptom category** using the decision tree in the taxonomy doc (e.g., availability/5xx, latency regression, streaming disconnects, routing/fallback misbehavior, capacity shortage, control plane outage, auth/RBAC, telemetry blind spot, private connectivity/config).
2) **Choose a subtype** that best matches current evidence.
3) **Confirm tier** (Hosted / Dedicated / Private). If multiple tiers are impacted, select the tier with highest operational commitment impacted and note others in the incident channel.
4) **Confirm primary owner** via the ownership mapping matrix (incident-bot should auto-resolve this once type/subtype is selected).
5) **Announce in the incident channel** (one-line):
   - “Classification: <type>/<subtype> | Owner: <team> | Tier: <tier> | Provisional: yes/no”

### Updating classification
Classification is expected to evolve as evidence improves.
- Update incident_type/subtype when new evidence indicates the initial choice is wrong.
- When changing classification, post a short note in the incident channel:
  - “Reclassified from <old> to <new> based on <signal/logs/customer reports>.”
- If reclassification changes the primary owner, the IC must ensure the new owner on-call is engaged immediately.

## Unknown / ambiguous classification policy
“Unknown” is allowed, but it is not a final state.

### When to use unknown
Use **incident_type = unknown** (and **subtype = unknown**) only when:
- There is active impact (or credible risk of impact), and
- Evidence is insufficient to choose among two or more top-level types without guessing.

### Requirements when using unknown
If set to unknown, you must:
- Assign **primary owner = SRE (Reliability)** temporarily.
- Post a note in the incident channel with:
  - what is known (symptoms, impacted endpoints/regions/tenants)
  - what is unknown (missing signals)
  - the next 1–3 steps to disambiguate
  - the time you will revisit classification

### Revisit cadence for unknown
- **Sev0/Sev1:** revisit at **T+30 min** and **T+60 min** from incident start.
- **Sev2:** revisit at **T+60 min**.
- If still unknown at the second checkpoint, IC must explicitly ask for SRE Duty Manager help to arbitrate and pick the least-wrong category for reporting/routing.

### Prohibited use of unknown
Do not use unknown to avoid choosing between teams or to delay paging.

## Multi-cause incidents (capacity + routing, etc.)
Many incidents involve multiple contributing factors. For v1 reporting consistency:
- Choose **one primary incident_type/subtype** based on the **initial customer-visible failure mode** (what customers experienced first / what drove the page).
- Capture additional systems in:
  - incident channel notes (“Contributing: <system/team>”) and
  - the postmortem “Contributing systems/factors” fields.

If there is a genuine split-brain scenario (two independently failing systems with separate mitigations), the IC may open a second incident and link them, but this should be rare.

## Enforcement in tooling
### Closure gating
Effective immediately for new incidents:
- incident-bot will block transitions to **Resolved/Closed** unless required fields are present:
  - incident_type
  - subtype
  - primary owner
  - tier
  - (Sev0/Sev1) postmortem link or placeholder ticket

### Owner resolution
- The primary owner is determined by a versioned mapping (type/subtype → owner team) maintained in the control plane.
- If incident-bot’s resolved owner appears wrong:
  1) confirm the selected subtype is correct
  2) check the ownership mapping matrix
  3) escalate to #eng-platform-oncall or SRE if the mapping is incorrect (file a tooling issue)

### Override policy
Overrides are permitted only when tooling is malfunctioning or during force majeure events:
- Only **SRE Duty Manager** (or delegated SRE incident tooling admin) may approve an override to close without required fields.
- Overrides must include a reason in the incident record.

## Audit and compliance
SRE will track and review:
- % of incidents classified within target time (by severity)
- % closed with required fields
- reclassification rate (signal for taxonomy ambiguity)
- unknown usage rate and time-to-resolution from unknown → final type

Repeat non-compliance will be addressed through on-call training and, if needed, escalation to engineering management.

## Examples
### Example 1: 5xx spike due to overload
- Symptom: elevated 5xx + saturation signals
- Classification: Availability/Overload (subtype per taxonomy)
- Owner: Fleet/Capacity or Serving Gateway (per mapping)
- Note: If routing is failing to shed load correctly, keep primary as overload if customers see errors first; note routing as contributing.

### Example 2: Latency regression during rollout
- Symptom: p95 latency step change after model version rollout
- Classification: Latency/rollout regression
- Owner: Serving Runtime (if runtime change) or Control Plane (if policy/config), per subtype mapping

### Example 3: Private deployment connectivity after upgrade
- Symptom: DNS/TLS handshake failures in customer VPC/on-prem
- Classification: Private connectivity/config
- Owner: Private Deployments / Networking (per subtype mapping)

## Where to find the allowed values
- Taxonomy definitions + decision tree: Incident Taxonomy v1 Definitions
- Owner mapping + remediation SLA matrix: Incident Ownership Mapping and Remediation SLA Matrix

## Change log
- 2026-02-05: Clarified unknown revisit cadence and temporary owner = SRE requirement; added explicit reclassification announcement guidance.
- 2026-01-10: Initial publish aligned to ADR-0142 and incident-bot enforcement rollout.

source.title: Postmortem: SEV-2 Serving runtime KV-cache eviction storm (2026-01-28)
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.customer_impact: p95 latency increased from ~680ms to ~1.9s for chat workloads. Approximately 4% of streaming responses experienced stalls >5s. No data loss.
source.summary: A change to the runtime memory watermark defaults caused aggressive KV-cache eviction under bursty workloads, leading to repeated recomputation and elevated latency.
source.timeline:
17:06 UTC: SLO alert for p95 latency on chat route fired (us-east).
17:10 UTC: On-call observed GPU memory oscillations and high cache evictions.
17:18 UTC: Identified runtime config change in 2026.01.28.1 setting low watermark from 0.85 to 0.70.
17:24 UTC: Rolled back runtime config for affected pool; evictions decreased.
17:40 UTC: p95 latency returned to baseline; monitored for stability.
18:02 UTC: Incident resolved.
source.root_cause: The serving runtime’s KV-cache manager used the low watermark to trigger eviction. The new default (0.70) was tuned for long-context batch workloads but was applied globally, causing churn for short, bursty chat traffic.
source.detection: Latency SLO alerts and GPU memory telemetry dashboards.
source.mitigation: Rolled back runtime config and pinned chat pools to the previous watermark settings. Disabled the automated config propagation job until validation was added.
source.what_went_well: Clear dashboards made the symptom obvious. Rollback procedure was well documented.
source.what_went_wrong: No workload-specific validation for memory watermark changes; config was promoted without canary on representative chat traffic.
follow_ups: Add perf-canary scenario for chat-bursty workload that fails on elevated KV-cache evictions (Serving Runtime; due 2026-02-20).
Split runtime watermark defaults by pool type (chat vs batch) and document in standards (Serving Runtime; due 2026-03-01).
Add change-management checklist item for runtime config promotions impacting memory behavior (SRE; due 2026-02-15).

source.title: Restoration Orchestra Playbook for LLM Serving Epsilon Events
source.author: Priya Patel
source.body:
Summary
This playbook covers fast, repeatable response patterns for small-to-medium ("epsilon") degradations of Redwood's LLM serving surface caused by short-lived traffic shifts, KV cache entropy, noisy neighbors, or routing oscillations. These events typically cause transient P99 latency spikes, elevated tail error rates, and benign customer impact if left unhandled. The goal: contain impact in 10-30 minutes and restore steady-state without a full rollback.

Scope and when to use this playbook
- Use for incidents classified Severity 2 (degraded performance with partial customer impact) and high-priority S3 transient patterns that show clear signal but no data loss.
- Do NOT use for full regional outages, sustained model quality regressions, or security incidents; follow the outage and security runbooks instead.

Key concepts and roles
- Conductor (oncall engineer): coordinates the recovery, authorizes routing changes.
- Section Lead - Traffic (networking/infra): evaluates routing, load balancer, rate limiter settings.
- Section Lead - Serving (runtime): checks model process health, KV cache metrics, batching queues.
- Section Lead - Storage/Caching: inspects Redis/ElastiCache, eviction rates, TTL distributions.
- Patch/Backfill owner: executes quick config patches, cache warms, or model pin actions.
- Communications owner (pager/bridge): posts status updates and customer-facing messages.

Immediate detection signals (examples)
- Alert: p99.response_time > 800ms for > 3 minutes across > 5% of requests to a model route.
- Alert: error_rate (%) > 2% OR model-fallback rate increased by 1.5x baseline.
- Alert: KV cache hit rate drops by > 30% vs 30m rolling baseline and eviction rate spikes.
- Observability tip: correlate tail latency spikes with traffic origin (region, customer-id, route).

First 10 minutes: quick stabilizers (Conductor)
1) Acknowledge and open a bridged incident channel (Zoom/Slack/Bridge). Post initial message with: trigger, scope, owner list, next check-in ETA (5m). Use status page template (see Communications section).
2) Snapshot key signals (run these commands and paste output into the bridge):
   - Metrics query (Prometheus):
     curl -s -G "https://prometheus.internal/api/v1/query" --data-urlencode 'query=histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket{route="/v1/generate"}[2m])) by (le, model))'
   - KV cache metrics (Redis/Cloud):
     redis-cli -h redis-prod-01 INFO stats | egrep "keyspace_hits|keyspace_misses|evicted_keys|used_memory_human"
   - Serving pool health (k8s):
     kubectl -n serving get pods -l app=redwood-runtime -o wide
3) Apply a non-invasive throttling or short-circuit for noisy tenants: add a per-tenant rate limit (if tenant_id provided) to reduce noisy traffic by 50% for 5 minutes. Example (feature-flag toggle):
   fwctl toggle-rate-limit --tenant $TENANT_ID --limit 20rps --duration 5m
4) If p99 is localized to one region, shift regional traffic using routing policy to healthy region with 5-10% traffic-steering increments (Conductor must approve).

Investigation (10-30 minutes)
- Check for model process OOM, GPU pre-emption, or kernel scheduling anomalies: inspect /var/log/nvidia-installer or runtime logs on the affected hosts.
- Look for sudden KV cache TTL crowding: query key TTL distribution for hot prefixes.
   redis-cli -h redis-prod-01 --scan --pattern 'kv:prefix:*' | xargs -n100 redis-cli -h redis-prod-01 ttl | awk '{print int($1/60)"m"}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -20
- Inspect batching queue latency and in-flight request counts via runtime admin API:
   curl -s https://runtime-admin.internal/metrics?service=batching | jq .
- Validate that fallback policies did not trigger to a slower model: check the fallback_count metric by model version.

Containment actions (pick 1-2 low-risk steps first)
- Warm caches: prepopulate prefix cache for top 50 affected routes using replay of sampled requests:
   python tools/warm_cache.py --route /v1/generate --sample-file /tmp/sample-requests.json --concurrency 8
- Reduce batching window or temporarily disable large-batch optimizations for the affected model to lower tail latency: use feature-flag rollback:
   ffctl set serving.batch_window_ms --model-id $MODEL --value 8 --duration 15m --author $ONCALL
- Isolate noisy tenants: put tenant on limited-capacity queue (see throttling command earlier).
- Scale up dedicated serving pool by +1 GPU node (only when autoscaling slower than needed):
   kscale serving-pool-$POOL --replicas +1 --reason "epsilon spike: ${INCIDENT_ID}"

When to apply a targeted rollback
- Apply rollback if a config change deployed in the last 30 minutes correlates with metric regression and quick revert test reduces p99 by >20% in 5 minutes. Follow standard rollback ADR and tag the roll-back commit.

Validation and close (30-60 minutes)
- Validate: p99 back to baseline ±10%, error_rate at normal levels, cache hit rate recovered.
- Run synthetic sanity tests against impacted routes (1000 requests at baseline traffic shaping) and confirm successful responses and throughput.
- Draft incident summary with timeline and actions in the bridge; assign a post-incident owner if follow-ups are required. Close bridge when stability maintained for 30 minutes.

Post-incident follow-up (owner: Conductor)
- Create a short postmortem in the postmortem template if customer impact exceeded 0.5% of active customers or if follow-up tasks required. Use the compact postmortem rubric (include timeline, root cause hypothesis, actions, owners, and SLO impact).
- For recurring epsilon events, schedule a mini-blameless RCA focused on cache hygiene, tenant isolation policies, and traffic shaping.

SLOs and thresholds relevant to this playbook
- p99 end-to-end latency SLO: 300ms (target), 800ms (alert threshold).
- Error rate SLO: 0.5% (target), 2% (alert threshold).
- Cache hit rate target: 88% (alert < 60%).

Runbook decision matrix (plain text table)
| Symptom | First action | Escalate to | Quick fix candidate | When to roll back |
|---------|--------------|-------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| p99 spike + cache miss | Warm cache + check evictions | Storage/Caching | Pre-warm top prefixes | If config deploy within 30m |
| High fallback rate | Check model rollouts | Serving | Pin to previous model version | If pin reduces fallback >50% |
| Localized region latency | Traffic-shift 10% increments | Traffic Lead | Shift regional traffic | If shift shows immediate benefit |

Communications: status page and customer message templates
- Status page short update (initial):
  "We are investigating increased response latency for text-generation API in select regions. Some requests may experience delays; we are actively mitigating and will post updates every 10 minutes. Impacted routes: /v1/generate."
- Status page update (containment):
  "Mitigation in place: we have applied short-term throttling and targeted cache warming to reduce tail latency. Customers using high-volume burst traffic may see reduced throughput for 5-15 minutes while systems stabilize."
- Customer email (if > 5% of customers affected for > 15 minutes):
  Subject: "Service update: temporary latency increase for Redwood Inference API"
  Body: "We detected and mitigated a temporary increase in response times affecting the text-generation endpoint. Our team applied targeted throttles and cache warming which restored service to normal. No data loss was observed. We are performing a follow-up analysis and will share findings in the post-incident summary. If you continue to experience issues, reply to this email or open a support ticket."

Game day exercises and scoring (quarterly)
- Scenario: simulated noisy tenant that triggers KV cache eviction cascade and p99 spike.
- Goals: Conductor opens bridge in <2 minutes, throttle noisy tenant in <8 minutes, restore p99 to <500ms in <20 minutes.
- Metrics: time-to-bridge, time-to-first-mitigation, time-to-restore p99, number of wrong escalations.
- Post-game tasks: update sampled routes for cache-warm tool, review per-tenant limit defaults, publish after-action notes.

Prevention and hardening suggestions (longer-term tasks)
- Implement adaptive tenant isolation: auto-detect bursty tenants and automatically place them in limited queues until traffic normalizes.
- Improve sampling for cache-warm tool and retain 24h of representative request samples per route.
- Add a canary in the routing plane to shift 1% of traffic to fallback-safe variants before full rollouts.

Operational checklist (copyable)
- [ ] Acknowledge alert and open bridge
- [ ] Snapshot metrics and paste into bridge
- [ ] Identify noisy tenant(s) and throttle if needed
- [ ] Warm top prefixes for affected routes
- [ ] Try small routing shifts if localized
- [ ] Validate p99 and error rates for 30 minutes
- [ ] Close bridge and create follow-up tickets

Appendix: useful commands and runbook links
- Prometheus quick query (p99 for route):
  curl -s -G "https://prometheus.internal/api/v1/query" --data-urlencode 'query=histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket{route="/v1/generate",job="runtime"}[2m])) by (le))' | jq .
- Model pin rollback (runtime API):
  curl -X POST https://runtime-admin.internal/models/pin -H "Authorization: Bearer ${ADMIN_TOKEN}" -d '{"route":"/v1/generate","model":"redwood-1.4.2","duration_minutes":30}'
- Cache warm helper: tools/warm_cache.py --help
- Runbook links:
  - /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-execution-guide-runbooks-and-communication-templates-2026
  - /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/cache-layer-guidelines

Owner and contact matrix
- Playbook author: Priya Patel (PRIYA@redwood.com)
- Oncall escalation lead: oncall@redwood.com (alias)
- Storage/Caching team: caching-team@redwood.com

Document history
- 2026-01-12: Created by Priya Patel
- 2026-02-03: Incorporated reviewer feedback, added customer messaging templates and game day scoring

- Add a canary in the routing plane to shift 1% of traffic to fallback-safe variants before full rollouts.

Operational checklist (copyable)
- [ ] Acknowledge alert and open bridge
- [ ] Snapshot metrics and paste into bridge
- [ ] Identify noisy tenant(s) and throttle if needed
- [ ] Warm top prefixes for affected routes
- [ ] Try small routing shifts if localized
- [ ] Validate p99 and error rates for 30 minutes
- [ ] Close bridge and create follow-up tickets

Appendix: useful commands and runbook links
- Prometheus quick query (p99 for route):
  curl -s -G "https://prometheus.internal/api/v1/query" --data-urlencode 'query=histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket{route="/v1/generate",job="runtime"}[2m])) by (le))' | jq .
- Model pin rollback (runtime API):
  curl -X POST https://runtime-admin.internal/models/pin -H "Authorization: Bearer ${ADMIN_TOKEN}" -d '{"route":"/v1/generate","model":"redwood-1.4.2","duration_minutes":30}'
- Cache warm helper: tools/warm_cache.py --help
- Runbook links:
  - /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-execution-guide-runbooks-and-communication-templates-2026
  - /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/cache-layer-guidelines

Owner and contact matrix
- Playbook author: Priya Patel (PRIYA@redwood.com)
- Oncall escalation lead: oncall@redwood.com (alias)
- Storage/Caching team: caching-team@redwood.com

Document history
- 2026-01-12: Created by Priya Patel
- 2026-02-03: Incorporated reviewer feedback, added customer messaging templates and game day scoring

source.title: Ops conductor patterns and scenario worksheets
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
Summary:

This document defines a set of repeatable incident-command patterns (the Ops Conductor patterns) and scenario worksheets for on-call teams owning LLM inference services. It complements line-item runbooks by codifying the orchestration roles, step sequences, and customer communication templates for common and high‑impact events (capacity spikes, KV cache thrashing, model quality regressions, and regional failovers).

Scope:
- Applies to all on-call responders for inference services: inference-gateway, route-router, kv-cache, model-hostpool, console-api, and billing-proxy.
- Intended for use during live incidents, game-day rehearsals, and postmortem evidence gathering.

Intent and benefits:
- Reduce cognitive load on first responders by prescribing clear conductor/bridge patterns.
- Shorten mean time to acknowledgement (MTA) and mean time to repair (MTTR) via pre-baked decision ladders.
- Ensure consistent, legally-safe customer/status messaging.

1) Roles & responsibilities (Ops Conductor pattern)

Primary roles (one person may hold multiple roles during low-severity incidents):
- Conductor (incident commander): owns the incident timeline, decisions, and external status messages. Typically a senior SRE or service owner.
- Bridge operator: runs the incident bridge, collects logs, and posts interim updates.
- Remediation lead: drives hands-on mitigation (scaling, rollbacks).
- Quality lead: assesses model/regression signals and runs prompt-set checks.
- Customer liaison (CSR/Success): crafts customer-facing messages and triages priority accounts.

Role matrix (minimum staffing by severity):
- Sev3: Conductor + Bridge operator (shared)
- Sev2: Conductor + Bridge operator + Remediation lead
- Sev1: Full cadre (Conductor, Bridge, Remediation, Quality, Customer liaison, Exec liaison)

2) Incident classification (quick reference)
- Latency degradation: 95th percentile p95 response time > 300 ms above baseline for > 5 minutes on a single region; impact: p95 API latency SLO 99.9%.
- Availability: error rate (5xx) > 2% sustained for 3 minutes or customer-visible rate > 0.5% across top-10 tenants.
- Cost/Capacity spike: GPU utilization > 90% on a Dedicated pool for 10+ minutes triggering queueing/backpressure.
- Model-quality regression: automated eval-set BLEU/EM drop > 3% or user-reported semantic regressions affecting CSAT.
- Data-leak or security incident: any verified exfiltration or policy breach -> follow security incident policy and escalate to Security.

3) Conductor play patterns (decision ladders)

Pattern A — Rapid containment (for cascading backend errors):
1. Conductor declares containment window (10 min).
2. Bridge operator collects top-5 error traces (service, trace ID, time window).
3. Remediation lead toggles read-only or route to backup model variant (route-router failover).
4. Conductor posts status: "Containment in progress; routing to fallback model variants."
5. If containment fails in 15 minutes, escalate to region failover plan.

Pattern B — Graceful degradation (for sustained capacity pressure):
1. Conductor activates throttling policy for non‑critical routes (billing-proxy + inference-gateway).
2. Remediation lead increases batch sizing suggestions (redwood-optimize hints) and spins up burst pool if allowed.
3. Customer liaison posts staged notice with guidance for retry/backoff windows.

Pattern C — Quality regression investigation:
1. Quality lead runs the automated prompt-set eval (eval-harness) and sanity checks against canary model.
2. If regression confirmed, initiate immediate rollback to previous version and pin model-hostpool.
3. Conductor coordinates a post-rollback validation window (30 minutes).

4) Scenario worksheet (example): KV cache thrash on europe-pool

- Situation snapshot:
  - Time detected: 2026-01-21T10:03Z
  - Service: kv-cache (europe-pool)
  - Trigger: sudden increase in miss rate (cache miss rate rising from 20% -> 78%) and p95 latency increase on inference-gateway.
  - Initial impact: Multi-tenant latency spikes, p95 increased by 760 ms, error rate steady at 0.4%.

- Immediate actions (first 10 minutes):
  1. Conductor: Acknowledge incident and open bridge (link: internal-bridge://incident-xxxx).
  2. Bridge operator: Execute diagnostics:
     - kubectl -n prod logs deploy/kv-cache --since=15m | head -n 200
     - curl -s "https://metrics.internal/prom/api/v1/query?query=rate(kv_cache_miss_total[5m])" | jq .
  3. Remediation lead: Temporarily set kv_cache.eviction_policy=LRU-aggressive in config and roll the daemonset to europe nodes.

- Containment goal (target):
  - Reduce miss rate to < 30% and p95 add-on latency < 200 ms within 30 minutes.

- Escalation ladder:
  - If miss rate > 60% after 20 minutes -> enable per-tenant cache quotas and route-heavy tenants to ephemeral model-hostpool.
  - If miss rate remains > 60% after 40 minutes -> initiate regional read-only for europe and trigger Dedicated pool burst allocation.

5) Runbook snippet: rapid rollback + staging validation (commands)

- Rollback command (kubectl example):
  - kubectl -n prod set image deployment/model-hostpool model-hostpool=registry.redwood.ai/model-hostpool:v2025-11-04
- Post-rollback validation:
  1. Run 100 synthetic chat requests through canary route: curl -X POST https://canary.inference.internal/v1/chat -d '{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello"}] }' -H 'Authorization: Bearer $TEST_KEY'
  2. Measure p95: curl "https://metrics.internal/prom/api/v1/query?query=histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{route=\"canary\"}[5m])) by (le))"

6) Status page and customer comms templates

- Short status line (for status.redwood.ai):
  - Title: "Serving degradation in EU region — mitigation in progress"
  - Body: "We are investigating elevated latency and partial service degradation affecting some EU tenants. Mitigations have been applied and engineered teams are actively restoring normal service. Next update in 30 minutes or earlier if the status changes."

- Customer email / CSR template (for impacted customers):
  - Subject: "Update: Inference service degraded in EU region — actions and recommended mitigations"
  - Body: "Hi <CustomerName>,

We detected a service degradation impacting inference latency in the EU region affecting some requests. Our engineers have applied cache tuning and routing mitigations; we recommend throttling non-essential batch jobs and applying exponential retry with jitter for transient failures. We will send another update within 30 minutes or as we have new information. For urgent account-level concerns, please reply to this email or open a support case with priority flag.

Sincerely,
Redwood Support"

- Exec summary blurb (for execs and legal):
  - "Root cause suspected to be cascading cache miss amplification due to recent prefix routing change. Containment measures are active; full restoration expected within 2 hours. No customer data exposure is suspected at this time."

7) Post-incident evidence checklist (minimum required for Sev1/Sev2)
- Timestamps and timeline (UTC) with actor IDs and decision points.
- Top-10 traces and span IDs for the incident window.
- Metric graphs: p95 latency, error rate, cache miss rate, GPU util by pool.
- Configuration diffs (git commit IDs) for any immediate changes.
- Customer impact list (tenant IDs, endpoints affected) and any credits issued.

8) Postmortem rubric (what a complete incident write-up must include)
- Author and contributors; incident timeline; impact statement (customers, internal SLOs, financial/CSAT impact).
- Root cause analysis with reproducible hypothesis and evidence.
- Action items: owner, due date, and verification steps.
- Retro notes: what worked, what didn't, and a prioritized remediation backlog.

9) Game-day scenarios and scoring worksheet

Recommended quarterly game days by theme:
- Cache failure day: simulate kv-cache thrash with synthetic miss flood; evaluate MTTR and correctness of throttling policies.
- Model rollback day: validate rollback tooling and rollback-to-canary validation windows.
- Cross-region failover: bring down europe control-plane and observe routing fallback and customer notice cadence.

Scoring rubric (pass/fail thresholds):
- Acknowledge within 5 min (pass if <= 5m).
- Containment action chosen and implemented within 20 min.
- Status update posted within 10 min of bridge opening.
- Postmortem draft published within 72 hours.

10) Operational hygiene and runbook ownership
- Each runbook or incident scenario worksheet must have an owner (service steward) with a quarterly verification sign-off.
- Runbook repository: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbook-index (link)
- Retirement cadence: any runbook not exercised in 18 months must be reviewed and either updated or archived.

Appendices
A. Example mitigation CLI snippets
- Enable burst pool via CLI (internal):
  - redwood infra pool allocate --pool europe-burst --gpu-count 8 --reason "incident: kv-cache-thrash"
- Set per-tenant cache quota (templated JSON):
  - curl -X POST https://config.internal/api/v1/tenants/123/cache-quota -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"quota_mb": 256, "enforce": true}'

B. Quick link map (internal links)
- Incident runbook index: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbook-index
- KV cache observability dashboard: https://dashboards.internal/d/kv-cache
- Eval harness and prompt-set runner: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness

C. Diagram (text):
- The orchestration diagram for the Conductor pattern: "Conductor" sits at the top coordinating three horizontal lanes: Observability (metrics, traces), Remediation (scaling, rollback, routing), and Communications (status page, csr). Each lane has owned actors (Bridge, Remediation lead, Customer liaison) and arrows describing decision flows (contains -> escalate -> failover).

Contact and ownership
- Document owner: Ava Chen (SRE)
- Contact: #ops-conductors on Slack, pager: oncall@redwood.ai

Change log:
- 2025-11-12: Created (Ava Chen)
- 2026-02-03: Updated with KV cache scenario and revised SLA thresholds (A. Chen)

- Containment action chosen and implemented within 20 min.
- Status update posted within 10 min of bridge opening.
- Postmortem draft published within 72 hours.

10) Operational hygiene and runbook ownership
- Each runbook or incident scenario worksheet must have an owner (service steward) with a quarterly verification sign-off.
- Runbook repository: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbook-index (link)
- Retirement cadence: any runbook not exercised in 18 months must be reviewed and either updated or archived.

Appendices
A. Example mitigation CLI snippets
- Enable burst pool via CLI (internal):
  - redwood infra pool allocate --pool europe-burst --gpu-count 8 --reason \"incident: kv-cache-thrash\"
- Set per-tenant cache quota (templated JSON):
  - curl -X POST https://config.internal/api/v1/tenants/123/cache-quota -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{\"quota_mb\": 256, \"enforce\": true}'

B. Quick link map (internal links)
- Incident runbook index: /confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbook-index
- KV cache observability dashboard: https://dashboards.internal/d/kv-cache
- Eval harness and prompt-set runner: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness

C. Diagram (text):
- The orchestration diagram for the Conductor pattern: \"Conductor\" sits at the top coordinating three horizontal lanes: Observability (metrics, traces), Remediation (scaling, rollback, routing), and Communications (status page, csr). Each lane has owned actors (Bridge, Remediation lead, Customer liaison) and arrows describing decision flows (contains -> escalate -> failover).

Contact and ownership
- Document owner: Ava Chen (SRE)
- Contact: #ops-conductors on Slack, pager: oncall@redwood.ai

Change log:
- 2025-11-12: Created (Ava Chen)
- 2026-02-03: Updated with KV cache scenario and revised SLA thresholds (A. Chen)

source.title: Operator incident litmus and customer toneplaybook
source.author: Elena Rodriguez
source.content:
Summary

This page defines a compact, operator-focused incident litmus suite (checks and fast actions), a library of customer-facing tone templates, and a lightweight on-call progression + game-day checklist. It is intended for first responders, bridge operators, and SRE leads who need a reproducible, low-friction way to assess severity, act within the first 10 minutes, and communicate clearly to internal and external stakeholders.

Audience
- SREs, on-call engineers, incident commanders
- Customer success and support leads (for coordination)
- Engineering managers (for training and game days)

Scope and goals
- Provide a 10-minute "litmus" checklist operators can run immediately after alert acknowledgement.
- Reduce ambiguity in customer messaging with tiered tone templates tied to impact class.
- Standardize minimal evidence captured for regulatory and SLA purposes.
- Define a short on-call progression path (shadow -> solo -> mentor) and a repeatable game-day scenario using the litmus checks.

Key assumptions
- This playbook focuses on service-level incidents affecting inference or routing (latency spikes, cache evictions, capacity exhaustion, authentication failures).
- Model quality regressions and eval failures should follow the model-quality triage playbook; this doc covers operational availability and customer comms.

1) 10-minute incident litmus (operator checklist)
Purpose: get rapid situational awareness and either contain or escalate. Follow in order. If any step clearly indicates a safety or security event, pause and escalate to security.

Pre-ack: ensure Zoom/bridge and PagerDuty handoff details are ready.

Step 0 — Acknowledge (0:00-0:30)
- Acknowledge on PagerDuty, post an initial one-line on the bridge: "Investigating — grabbing logs and route status (T+0)."
- Assign roles if alone: Operator = you; Bridge lead = you until handed off.

Step 1 — Quick impact sweep (0:30-2:00)
- Check SLO dashboards (Platform -> Inference -> p99 latency, error rate).
- Confirm which regions/customers/routes show anomalies.
- Commands:
  - curl -s https://console.internal/redwood/slo?service=inference | jq .summary
  - grafana panels: inference/p95-latency, inference/p99-latency, inference/error-rate

Step 2 — Isolate blast radius (2:00-4:00)
- Identify if incident is: single-customer, single-region, or platform-wide.
- Query routing service: kubectl -n routing get pods -l app=router -o wide
- Inspect model-serving pool health: kubectl -n serving get hpa,deployments

Step 3 — Fast mitigations (4:00-8:00)
- Safe remediations to try in order (each with rollback):
  1) Pause non-critical canaries: curl -X POST https://ops-api/internal/traffic/pause?group=canaries
  2) Scale up critical pool: kubectl scale deploy inference-worker --replicas=+3 -n serving
  3) Switch routing policy to conservative (latency-first): opsctl routing set-policy --policy=latency-first
- If mitigations succeed, move to stabilization steps. If not, escalate.

Step 4 — Evidence collection and hypothesis (8:00-10:00)
- Collect must-have evidence:
  - Correlated service logs (router, serving runtime, admission controller) for T-15m..+10m
  - Recent deploys list: gitopsctl list-deploys --since 30m
  - Resource metrics: kubectl top pods --all-namespaces
- Produce one-sentence hypothesis and next action on the bridge.

Escalation criteria (immediate):
- Error rate > 1% across >= 3 regions for 2 consecutive 1m windows.
- p99 latency > 3x baseline and user-facing features degraded.
- Signs of data exfiltration or keys compromised -> security escalate.

2) Impact classification table (for comms)
- Use this to pick tone and status page urgency.

| Class | Symptom set | Customer impact | Status page type | Suggested SLA credit |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Sev-A | Total outage; platform unreachable | All customers impacted | Major outage | Consider SLA review |
| Sev-B | Significant degraded latency (>2x p95) or partial region outage | Multiple customers, degraded UX | Degraded service | Case-by-case |
| Sev-C | Single-customer impact or internal integration failure | Isolated customer(s) | Minor incident | N/A |

3) Tone and customer messaging templates (pick by impact class)
Guidelines: short, honest, action-oriented. Use active voice. Avoid technical jargon for external templates; internal updates can include technical details. Include placeholders: {customer}, {eta}, {mitigation}.

External short notice (Sev-A template)
- Subject: "Service interruption affecting Redwood inference — investigating"
- Body: "We are aware of a service interruption affecting inference requests. Our engineering team is actively investigating. We will provide an update within 30 minutes with affected regions and an estimated time to recovery. We apologize for the disruption and will follow with a technical postmortem once resolved."

External progress update (Sev-B template)
- Subject: "Update: degraded inference latency in {region} — mitigation in progress"
- Body: "We have identified increased latency impacting some inference requests in {region}. We are applying conservative routing and scaling measures to restore normal performance. No customer data exposure has been detected. Next update in 60 minutes or when service is stable."

Customer-targeted note (Sev-C template)
- Subject: "Redwood: brief service interruption affecting your account ({customer})"
- Body: "We detected a short-lived issue that may have affected {customer} between {start} and {end}. We have applied a fix and believe no persistent impact remains. Contact support@redwood.ai if you see anything unusual."

Internal urgent notification (bridge operator)
- "Impact: {class}. Hypothesis: {one-line-hypothesis}. Next action: {next-action}. Owners: {owner-list}."

4) Status page message structure and placeholders
- Minimal fields for public status page post:
  - headline (one sentence), impact (list of affected features/regions), status (Investigating/Identified/Monitoring/Resolved), start_time, update cadence, mitigation steps taken, next_update_eta.
- Example JSON payload for status API (operator copy):
  {
    "headline": "Investigating elevated inference latency in eu-west-1",
    "impact": "Customers in eu-west-1 may see longer response times for text generation",
    "status": "Investigating",
    "start_time": "2026-02-03T08:14:00Z",
    "next_update": "2026-02-03T08:44:00Z"
  }

5) Runbook snippets (common signatures and steps)
A. KV cache eviction cascade (signature)
- Symptoms: sudden p99 jump, gradual error rate increase during long-sequence workloads, elevated memory pressure on key-value storage nodes.
- Quick litmus: check kv-cache-metrics: curl http://metrics.internal/kv-cache | jq .evictions_per_min
- Fast mitigation: increase cache budget for hot prefixes: opsctl cache set-budget --pool=hot --mb=+1024
- Rollback: opsctl cache set-budget --pool=hot --mb=-1024

B. GPU OOM/worker crash loop
- Symptoms: many worker restarts, OOM logs in runtime, reduced throughput.
- Litmus: kubectl -n serving logs deploy/inference-worker | grep -i oom | tail -n 50
- Mitigation path:
  1) Drain affected node: kubectl cordon <node> && kubectl drain --ignore-daemonsets <node>
  2) Temporarily scale replica set to absorb load: kubectl scale rs inference-worker --replicas=+4 -n serving
  3) If due to model size, fall back to quantized variant: opsctl model set-variant --model-id {id} --variant quantized-8bit

C. Control-plane auth flap (signature)
- Symptoms: spikes in 401/403 across services, new deploy coincident.
- Litmus: curl -s https://auth.internal/health && journalctl -u auth.service | tail -n 200
- Mitigation: revert last auth roll: gitopsctl rollback auth-service --to-last-stable

6) Postmortem minimum evidence checklist (required for published postmortems)
- Timeline with events (UTC), who made key actions, trace IDs for affected requests (sample of 10), correlated deploy hashes, dashboards screenshots (p95/p99/error-rate), bridge transcript or summary, customer notices sent, remediation steps and root cause statement.
- Postmortem template fields (operator must fill):
  - Incident ID, Start/End, Impact class, Root cause (mc), Remediation, Follow-ups, Owner for each follow-up, SLA credit recommendation.

7) On-call progression and rotation brief
- Levels: Shadow (2 weeks), Paired solo (2 weeks with mentor), Solo (6 weeks), Mentor (ongoing)
- Expectations by level:
  - Shadow: observe 1-2 incidents end-to-end, run litmus under supervision, no owner responsibilities.
  - Paired solo: primary on-call but mentor present on bridge, responsible for evidence capture and initial comms.
  - Solo: independent incident ownership and bridge leadership.
  - Mentor: 1-hour weekly check-ins with paired junior on-call, approve postmortem drafts.
- Handoff checklist (before leaving shift):
  1) Confirm no active incidents or clearly documented status.
  2) Ensure next on-call has access to bridge, dashboards, and kubectl context.
  3) Post short summary in #oncall-handoffs channel.

8) Game-day scenario for litmus validation (30–90m)
- Objective: validate 10-minute litmus, customer comm templates, and status page flow.
- Scenario: Simulated KV cache eviction leading to p99 spike in us-west-2, with seeded canaries and a seeded high-priority customer alert.
- Roles: Operator, Bridge lead, Customer comms, SRE observer.
- Steps:
  1) Trigger synthetic eviction (opsctl simulate kv-eviction --pool=hot --rate=1000)
  2) Operator runs the 10-minute litmus and applies safe mitigations.
  3) Customer comms drafts external notice using Sev-B template; SRE observer reviews.
  4) Runbook owners validate evidence capture and postmortem skeleton.
- Success criteria: litmus completed within 10 minutes, status page updated within 15 minutes, rollback/migration actions applied with a clear owner assigned.

9) Ownership and escalation contacts
- Primary bridge lead: oncall-bridge@redwood.ai (pager duty: BridgeEscalation)
- SRE manager (escalation for capacity): Marcus Lin (marcus@redwood.ai)
- Security escalation: sec-oncall@redwood.ai
- Customer success lead (exec liaison): Priya Sharma (priya@redwood.ai)

10) FAQs and troubleshooting tips
- Q: Who publishes the status page? A: Bridge lead publishes public updates; Customer Success publishes targeted customer emails.
- Q: When should we offer an SLA credit? A: Use the impact classification table and consult finance/legal for Sev-A incidents.
- Q: How to capture trace IDs quickly? A: Use trace-collector query: tracectl query --service=inference --since 30m --limit 20

Appendix: quick commands and links
- Dashboards: https://grafana.internal/d/redwood/inference
- Status API: https://status.internal/api/v1/incidents POST (requires bridge token)
- Runbook source (git): git@repo.internal:runbooks/incident-litmus.git

Document maintenance
- Owner: eng-sre team. Update cadence: after every major incident or quarterly.
- Reviewers: Marcus Lin, Priya Sharma.

If you have suggestions for additional litmus checks or alternative customer tone options, add a comment to this page or open a PR against runbooks/incident-litmus.git.

4) Status page message structure and placeholders
