  - headline (one sentence), impact (list of affected features/regions), status (Investigating/Identified/Monitoring/Resolved), start_time, update cadence, mitigation steps taken, next_update_eta.
- Example JSON payload for status API (operator copy):
  {
    "headline": "Investigating elevated inference latency in eu-west-1",
    "impact": "Customers in eu-west-1 may see longer response times for text generation",
    "status": "Investigating",
    "start_time": "2026-02-03T08:14:00Z",
    "next_update": "2026-02-03T08:44:00Z"
  }

5) Runbook snippets (common signatures and steps)
A. KV cache eviction cascade (signature)
- Symptoms: sudden p99 jump, gradual error rate increase during long-sequence workloads, elevated memory pressure on key-value storage nodes.
- Quick litmus: check kv-cache-metrics: curl http://metrics.internal/kv-cache | jq .evictions_per_min
- Fast mitigation: increase cache budget for hot prefixes: opsctl cache set-budget --pool=hot --mb=+1024
- Rollback: opsctl cache set-budget --pool=hot --mb=-1024

B. GPU OOM/worker crash loop
- Symptoms: many worker restarts, OOM logs in runtime, reduced throughput.
- Litmus: kubectl -n serving logs deploy/inference-worker | grep -i oom | tail -n 50
- Mitigation path:
  1) Drain affected node: kubectl cordon <node> && kubectl drain --ignore-daemonsets <node>
  2) Temporarily scale replica set to absorb load: kubectl scale rs inference-worker --replicas=+4 -n serving
  3) If due to model size, fall back to quantized variant: opsctl model set-variant --model-id {id} --variant quantized-8bit

C. Control-plane auth flap (signature)
- Symptoms: spikes in 401/403 across services, new deploy coincident.
- Litmus: curl -s https://auth.internal/health && journalctl -u auth.service | tail -n 200
- Mitigation: revert last auth roll: gitopsctl rollback auth-service --to-last-stable

6) Postmortem minimum evidence checklist (required for published postmortems)
- Timeline with events (UTC), who made key actions, trace IDs for affected requests (sample of 10), correlated deploy hashes, dashboards screenshots (p95/p99/error-rate), bridge transcript or summary, customer notices sent, remediation steps and root cause statement.
- Postmortem template fields (operator must fill):
  - Incident ID, Start/End, Impact class, Root cause (mc), Remediation, Follow-ups, Owner for each follow-up, SLA credit recommendation.

7) On-call progression and rotation brief
- Levels: Shadow (2 weeks), Paired solo (2 weeks with mentor), Solo (6 weeks), Mentor (ongoing)
- Expectations by level:
  - Shadow: observe 1-2 incidents end-to-end, run litmus under supervision, no owner responsibilities.
  - Paired solo: primary on-call but mentor present on bridge, responsible for evidence capture and initial comms.
  - Solo: independent incident ownership and bridge leadership.
  - Mentor: 1-hour weekly check-ins with paired junior on-call, approve postmortem drafts.
- Handoff checklist (before leaving shift):
  1) Confirm no active incidents or clearly documented status.
  2) Ensure next on-call has access to bridge, dashboards, and kubectl context.
  3) Post short summary in #oncall-handoffs channel.

8) Game-day scenario for litmus validation (30–90m)
- Objective: validate 10-minute litmus, customer comm templates, and status page flow.
- Scenario: Simulated KV cache eviction leading to p99 spike in us-west-2, with seeded canaries and a seeded high-priority customer alert.
- Roles: Operator, Bridge lead, Customer comms, SRE observer.
- Steps:
  1) Trigger synthetic eviction (opsctl simulate kv-eviction --pool=hot --rate=1000)
  2) Operator runs the 10-minute litmus and applies safe mitigations.
  3) Customer comms drafts external notice using Sev-B template; SRE observer reviews.
  4) Runbook owners validate evidence capture and postmortem skeleton.
- Success criteria: litmus completed within 10 minutes, status page updated within 15 minutes, rollback/migration actions applied with a clear owner assigned.

9) Ownership and escalation contacts
- Primary bridge lead: oncall-bridge@redwood.ai (pager duty: BridgeEscalation)
- SRE manager (escalation for capacity): Marcus Lin (marcus@redwood.ai)
- Security escalation: sec-oncall@redwood.ai
- Customer success lead (exec liaison): Priya Sharma (priya@redwood.ai)

10) FAQs and troubleshooting tips
- Q: Who publishes the status page? A: Bridge lead publishes public updates; Customer Success publishes targeted customer emails.
- Q: When should we offer an SLA credit? A: Use the impact classification table and consult finance/legal for Sev-A incidents.
- Q: How to capture trace IDs quickly? A: Use trace-collector query: tracectl query --service=inference --since 30m --limit 20

Appendix: quick commands and links
- Dashboards: https://grafana.internal/d/redwood/inference
- Status API: https://status.internal/api/v1/incidents POST (requires bridge token)
- Runbook source (git): git@repo.internal:runbooks/incident-litmus.git

Document maintenance
- Owner: eng-sre team. Update cadence: after every major incident or quarterly.
- Reviewers: Marcus Lin, Priya Sharma.

If you have suggestions for additional litmus checks or alternative customer tone options, add a comment to this page or open a PR against runbooks/incident-litmus.git.

source.title: On‑call Transition Cookbook and Blameless Postmortem Cadence
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
Overview
========

Purpose: This page codifies a repeatable, SLO-driven approach for on‑call transitions, escalation ladders, and a blameless postmortem cadence. It is designed for engineers, SREs, and on‑call leads who perform handoffs, run game days, and own incident learning loops.

Scope: applies to Redwood Inference production services (hosted API, dedicated pools, private deployments) and covers: rotation transitions, short‑form handoff artifact, escalation timers, evidence collection, postmortem schedule, and customer status / status page templates.

Principles
----------
- SLO first: incidents are triaged against customer‑facing SLOs (availability and latency) and classification is driven by SLO impact.
- Time‑boxed actions: fast containment is prioritized; long investigations are deferred to postmortem.
- Psychological safety: no individual blame; focus on systemic causes and actionable remediation.
- Repeatability: written artifacts + runbook links must always accompany major handoffs.

Key SLOs and thresholds (for triage)
-----------------------------------
| SLO | Target | Triage threshold | Escalation severity |
|----|-------:|------------------:|-------------------:|
| Hosted API availability | 99.95% monthly | < 99.5% sustained 10m | Sev2 / Sev1 if customer outage |
| P95 generation latency (region) | < 250 ms | > 400 ms 5m window | Sev2 |
| Embeddings throughput (per-dedicated pool) | 95% of provisioned | < 80% throughput 10m | Sev3/Sev2 |
| Error rate (5xx) | < 1% | > 3% 5m rolling | Sev2/Sev1 |
\nRotation transition checklist (10-minute template)
--------------------------------------------------
The on‑going oncall (OOO) briefly documents the following in the team Slack #oncall-handoff and attaches a short handoff artifact to the rotation calendar event. Use the checklist below — aim for 10 minutes.

1) Current state summary (1–2 lines): services impacted, customer visible symptoms, any workarounds in place.
2) Active pages/tickets: PD incidents and JIRA IDs.
3) What I did in the last hour: mitigations, restarts, config changes, rollbacks.
4) What I intend to do next (if known) and blockers.
5) Key dashboards/queries: link(s) to the SLO dashboard, rate-limited query, and trace.
6) Runbooks attached (copy links): e.g., Runbook: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/kv-cache-eviction
7) Evidence bundle location: s3://redwood-incident-evidence/<incident-id> or /mnt/incident-evidence/<incident-id>
8) Confidence level and suggested escalation (ack windows, owners).

Example handoff message (Slack)
-------------------------------
[HANDOFF] 2026-02-03 09:50 UTC
- Summary: ~30% 5xx on hosted-text-gen-east; customers see streaming truncation.
- Active: PD#1234 (ack), JIRA INC-987
- Last hour: restarted model pool A, disabled auto-batching; latency p95 improved ~20% but errors persist.
- Next: coordinate canary rollback (owner: @diego), investigating kernel OOMs (link).
- Runbooks: kv-cache-eviction, model-fallback-policy. Evidence: s3://redwood-incident-evidence/INC-987

Escalation ladder and timers
---------------------------
- Pager acknowledgment target: 5 minutes (primary)
- Initial mitigation window: 15 minutes (if unresolved, escalate to secondary)
- Cross-team engagement: 30 minutes (platform, model-team, SRE)
- Customer status decision point: 45 minutes (if customer impact confirmed)

Escalation flow (summary): Primary -> Secondary -> Team Lead -> Oncall SRE -> Engineering Director. For customer-impacting Sev1s, trigger Incident Bridge and notify CS/Support within 30 minutes.

Containment runbook quick actions (top-level)
--------------------------------------------
1) Reduce blast radius: temporarily throttle trafffic, shift routing to a healthy region, or disable non-essential features.
2) Switch traffic to a compatible model variant (fallback): document fallback in the timeline.
3) Revert recent deploys or config churn if the timeline indicates correlation.
4) If OOMs or GPU kernel errors, drain affected nodes and re-schedule workloads. Use drain script: 
```
# Drain node (example)
okctl node drain --id i-0abc123 --reason "OOM during inference" --timeout 600
```
\nEvidence collection and preservation (must do within first hour)
----------------------------------------------------------------
- Save logs (kubectl logs --since=1h or equivalent), traces, and the current SLO dashboard snapshot to the evidence bucket.
- Capture sample requests that reproduce the error (sanitize PII).
- Tag all evidence with incident-id and owner. Failure to preserve evidence can block postmortem analysis.

Postmortem cadence and ownership
-------------------------------
- Triage postmortem required if incident meets any of: 
  - Customer‑facing outage (any SLA credit exposure)
  - Sev1 or prolonged Sev2 (> 4 hours)
  - Repeating root cause across 3 incidents in 30 days

- Timeline:
  - 0–48 hours: containment and evidence collection (operational owners)
  - 72 hours: preliminary timeline and a blameless incident summary (to #postmortems)
  - 7 calendar days: draft postmortem authored (owner: incident commander)
  - 14 calendar days: postmortem review meeting (participants: incident owner, SRE, affected service leads)
  - 30 days: action item close or re-assess (owner assigned)

- Roles:
  - Incident Commander (IC): runs the incident and is responsible for the timeline and initial writeup.
  - Evidence Lead: ensures logs, traces, and reproductions are saved.
  - RCA Lead: performs deeper analysis and drafts contributing cause matrix.
  - Action Tracker: ensures remediation tasks have clear owners and dates.

Blameless postmortem scaffold (required sections)
------------------------------------------------
- Summary (2–4 sentences)
- Impact (users, SLOs, revenue/customers)
- Timeline (bulletized, timestamps in UTC)
- Root cause (contributing causes + evidence)
- Remediation: short term (within 7d) and long term (within 90d)
- Preventative metrics and tests (what we'll add to detect recurrence)
- Owners and acceptance criteria for each action
- Lessons learned and open questions

Meeting templates and psychological-safety checklist
--------------------------------------------------
Before the postmortem review meeting, the IC must:
- Share the draft with the reviewer list at least 48 hours ahead.
- Flag any security/customer sensitive sections to Legal/Compliance.

During the meeting:
- Start with facts and timeline, not names.
- Invite questions about systems and decisions; avoid assigning blame.
- Confirm each action has an owner and a deadline.

Psychological-safety checklist (short):
- Explicitly state "this is a blameless postmortem" at the meeting start.
- Use neutral language: "contributed to" vs "caused by".
- Encourage junior engineers to speak; the facilitator should call on quieter participants.

Game day (chaos) playbook for oncall skill growth
--------------------------------------------------
Frequency: quarterly per team.
Goals: practice evidence collection, handoffs, and force‑test runbooks.
Minimum agenda:
1) 15m scenario brief (injects and objectives)
2) 30–60m incident response (live)
3) 30m hotwash (what worked, what didn’t)
4) 30m action creation and runbook updates

Scenarios to rotate: KV cache eviction, model quantization regression, regional routing failure, capacity exhaustion in dedicated pools. Each scenario must have a realistic timeline and clear termination criteria.

Status page and customer communication templates
------------------------------------------------
Decision rule: publish to status.redwood.ai if customer visible impact or any ongoing mitigation that takes >30 minutes. For high severity incidents always publish within 45 minutes.

Status page template (short)
- Title: Degraded generation in US‑East (ongoing)
- Affected: Hosted text generation API
- Time identified: 2026-02-03 09:05 UTC
- Current status: Identified — Mitigation in progress
- Next update: 2026-02-03 10:00 UTC
- Details: We are seeing an elevated 5xx rate for text generation in the US‑East region. Teams are investigating and have routed traffic away from impacted nodes. No data loss reported.

Customer support email (initial)
Subject: Service degradation impacting text generation in US‑East
Body:
Hello,

We are investigating an issue affecting text generation in the US‑East region. You may experience errors or increased response times. Our teams are actively mitigating and we will send an update within 45 minutes. We apologize for the disruption.

— Redwood Inference Support Team

Customer follow‑up template (30m)
- What changed since last update: e.g., rolled back canary, reduced traffic to region A (impact reduced 40%)
- Expected next steps and ETA (if known)
- Contact for escalations: support@redwood.ai (include account manager if enterprise customer)

Runbook hygiene and ownership matrix
-----------------------------------
- Every runbook must include: owner, last run date, test instructions, rollback steps, and a short SR (safety review) note.
- Review cadence: critical runbooks (SLO‑impacting) reviewed quarterly; lower‑risk annually.

Example runbook metadata table
ID | Name | Owner | Last tested | Next review
RB-041 | kv-cache-eviction | Marcus Patel | 2026-01-15 | 2026-04-15
RB-058 | model-fallback-policy | Lena O'Neill | 2025-11-10 | 2026-02-10

Automations and small helpers
-----------------------------
- Automated evidence snapshot: trigger via /incident snapshot <incident-id> to save dashboards and traces to the evidence bucket.
- Slack bot handoff: /handoff create will auto-post the 8‑point checklist to #oncall-handoff and attach the calendar event.

Appendices
---------
A. Quick decision ladder (cheat sheet)
- If SLOs violated and customers impacted: declare Sev1, start bridge, notify CS within 30m.
- If internal-only impact with degradation: Sev3/Sev2 depending on duration and scope.

B. Useful commands and snippets
- Collect recent logs (k8s): kubectl -n redwood logs -l app=text-gen --since=30m > /tmp/textgen-logs-30m.log
- Capture traces (example): go-trace-collector --service hosted-text-gen --start 2026-02-03T08:50:00Z --end 2026-02-03T09:10:00Z --out traces/INC-987.json

C. Related pages
- Runbook library: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks
- Incident evidence retention policy: /confluence/security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention/incident-evidence-policy

If you have improvements to this cookbook, open PR on the runbook repo or ping the SRE rota lead.

4) If OOMs or GPU kernel errors, drain affected nodes and re-schedule workloads. Use drain script: 
```
# Drain node (example)
okctl node drain --id i-0abc123 --reason "OOM during inference" --timeout 600
```

Evidence collection and preservation (must do within first hour)
----------------------------------------------------------------
- Save logs (kubectl logs --since=1h or equivalent), traces, and the current SLO dashboard snapshot to the evidence bucket.
- Capture sample requests that reproduce the error (sanitize PII).
- Tag all evidence with incident-id and owner. Failure to preserve evidence can block postmortem analysis.

Postmortem cadence and ownership
-------------------------------
- Triage postmortem required if incident meets any of: 
  - Customer‑facing outage (any SLA credit exposure)
  - Sev1 or prolonged Sev2 (> 4 hours)
  - Repeating root cause across 3 incidents in 30 days

- Timeline:
  - 0–48 hours: containment and evidence collection (operational owners)
  - 72 hours: preliminary timeline and a blameless incident summary (to #postmortems)
  - 7 calendar days: draft postmortem authored (owner: incident commander)
  - 14 calendar days: postmortem review meeting (participants: incident owner, SRE, affected service leads)
  - 30 days: action item close or re-assess (owner assigned)

- Roles:
  - Incident Commander (IC): runs the incident and is responsible for the timeline and initial writeup.
  - Evidence Lead: ensures logs, traces, and reproductions are saved.
  - RCA Lead: performs deeper analysis and drafts contributing cause matrix.
  - Action Tracker: ensures remediation tasks have clear owners and dates.

Blameless postmortem scaffold (required sections)
------------------------------------------------
- Summary (2–4 sentences)
- Impact (users, SLOs, revenue/customers)
- Timeline (bulletized, timestamps in UTC)
- Root cause (contributing causes + evidence)
- Remediation: short term (within 7d) and long term (within 90d)
- Preventative metrics and tests (what we'll add to detect recurrence)
- Owners and acceptance criteria for each action
- Lessons learned and open questions

Meeting templates and psychological-safety checklist
--------------------------------------------------
Before the postmortem review meeting, the IC must:
- Share the draft with the reviewer list at least 48 hours ahead.
- Flag any security/customer sensitive sections to Legal/Compliance.

During the meeting:
- Start with facts and timeline, not names.
- Invite questions about systems and decisions; avoid assigning blame.
- Confirm each action has an owner and a deadline.

Psychological-safety checklist (short):
- Explicitly state "this is a blameless postmortem" at the meeting start.
- Use neutral language: "contributed to" vs "caused by".
- Encourage junior engineers to speak; the facilitator should call on quieter participants.

Game day (chaos) playbook for oncall skill growth
--------------------------------------------------
Frequency: quarterly per team.
Goals: practice evidence collection, handoffs, and force‑test runbooks.
Minimum agenda:
1) 15m scenario brief (injects and objectives)
2) 30–60m incident response (live)
3) 30m hotwash (what worked, what didn’t)
4) 30m action creation and runbook updates

Scenarios to rotate: KV cache eviction, model quantization regression, regional routing failure, capacity exhaustion in dedicated pools. Each scenario must have a realistic timeline and clear termination criteria.

Status page and customer communication templates
------------------------------------------------
Decision rule: publish to status.redwood.ai if customer visible impact or any ongoing mitigation that takes >30 minutes. For high severity incidents always publish within 45 minutes.

Status page template (short)
- Title: Degraded generation in US‑East (ongoing)
- Affected: Hosted text generation API
- Time identified: 2026-02-03 09:05 UTC
- Current status: Identified — Mitigation in progress
- Next update: 2026-02-03 10:00 UTC
- Details: We are seeing an elevated 5xx rate for text generation in the US‑East region. Teams are investigating and have routed traffic away from impacted nodes. No data loss reported.

Customer support email (initial)
Subject: Service degradation impacting text generation in US‑East
Body:
Hello,

We are investigating an issue affecting text generation in the US‑East region. You may experience errors or increased response times. Our teams are actively mitigating and we will send an update within 45 minutes. We apologize for the disruption.

— Redwood Inference Support Team

Customer follow‑up template (30m)
- What changed since last update: e.g., rolled back canary, reduced traffic to region A (impact reduced 40%)
- Expected next steps and ETA (if known)
- Contact for escalations: support@redwood.ai (include account manager if enterprise customer)

Runbook hygiene and ownership matrix
-----------------------------------
- Every runbook must include: owner, last run date, test instructions, rollback steps, and a short SR (safety review) note.
- Review cadence: critical runbooks (SLO‑impacting) reviewed quarterly; lower‑risk annually.

Example runbook metadata table
ID | Name | Owner | Last tested | Next review
RB-041 | kv-cache-eviction | Marcus Patel | 2026-01-15 | 2026-04-15
RB-058 | model-fallback-policy | Lena O'Neill | 2025-11-10 | 2026-02-10

Automations and small helpers
-----------------------------
- Automated evidence snapshot: trigger via /incident snapshot <incident-id> to save dashboards and traces to the evidence bucket.
- Slack bot handoff: /handoff create will auto-post the 8‑point checklist to #oncall-handoff and attach the calendar event.

Appendices
---------
A. Quick decision ladder (cheat sheet)
- If SLOs violated and customers impacted: declare Sev1, start bridge, notify CS within 30m.
- If internal-only impact with degradation: Sev3/Sev2 depending on duration and scope.

B. Useful commands and snippets
- Collect recent logs (k8s): kubectl -n redwood logs -l app=text-gen --since=30m > /tmp/textgen-logs-30m.log
- Capture traces (example): go-trace-collector --service hosted-text-gen --start 2026-02-03T08:50:00Z --end 2026-02-03T09:10:00Z --out traces/INC-987.json

C. Related pages
- Runbook library: /confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks
- Incident evidence retention policy: /confluence/security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention/incident-evidence-policy

If you have improvements to this cookbook, open PR on the runbook repo or ping the SRE rota lead.

source.title: Controlled Failover Test Results (Game Day) — 2026-01-15
source.author: Miles Thornton
source.content:
## Summary
On 2026-01-15 we executed Game Day #1 for the Regional Failover Automation Upgrade. The exercise intentionally impaired **us-east** for a limited set of production traffic and validated our ability to (1) detect regional impairment using the new signal hierarchy, (2) safely shift traffic to **eu-west** using the failover controller with production guardrails, and (3) recover without oscillation.

**Overall outcome:** Partial success. We met the primary objectives around safe traffic movement and recovery, but identified meaningful gaps in (a) signal corroboration timing, (b) dashboard annotations, and (c) client/streaming behavior during redirects.

## Scope and setup
### Workloads included
- **Hosted API**: limited blast radius using allowlisted tenants + specific routes/models.
- **Dedicated**: one internal test pool + one consenting pilot customer pool in “observe-only” mode (no automatic drain).

### Regions
- Primary region: **us-east**
- Failover region: **eu-west**

### Mode
- Failover controller enabled with:
  - **Dry-run ON for first 20 minutes** (recommendations only)
  - **Approval hook required** for production execution
  - Anti-flap timers per ADR-043

### Key owners on the bridge
- Incident commander / execution lead: **Rafael Mendes**
- Failover architecture: **Miles Thornton**
- Routing/policy: **Mina Haddad**, **Ravi Menon**, **Jordan Lee**
- Observability: **Allison Grant**, **Sergio Costa**
- Network reachability: **Victor Alvarez**
- Support liaison: **Owen Phillips**

## Success criteria (from test plan)
| Criterion | Target | Result |
|---|---:|---|
| Detection to recommended action | ≤ 90s | **Met (62s p50; 88s p95)** |
| Approval to traffic shift start | ≤ 2 min | **Met (1m 34s)** |
| Traffic shift completion (allowlisted traffic) | ≤ 10 min | **Met (6m 12s)** |
| Customer-visible errors during exercise | No sustained increase; transient ok | **Partially met** (transient 5xx/timeouts observed) |
| No oscillation / uncontrolled rollback | None | **Partially met** (one automated rollback due to hold-down interplay) |
| Post-failover steady-state in target region | Stable p95 within +30% | **Not met for one route** (tail latency spike) |

## Timeline (UTC)
> Notes: Times are from incident bridge log + controller audit events. Dashboards lacked some annotations (see Issues) so some points are reconstructed from logs.

- **16:00** — Bridge opened. Preconditions reviewed (capacity headroom, paging routes, feature flags).
- **16:07** — Controller dry-run enabled for allowlisted traffic policies.
- **16:12** — Synthetic impairment introduced in **us-east** (network reachability degradation for a subset of edges + increased gateway error injection for test routes).
- **16:13:02** — Region health aggregator begins marking **us-east** as *degraded* (reachability signal first).
- **16:13:26** — Policy engine evaluates trigger hierarchy and produces **FailoverRecommendation: us-east → eu-west** for allowlisted routes.
- **16:14** — On-call confirms corroboration signals (5xx burn + probe reachability).
- **16:14:42** — Approval granted; controller execution toggled from dry-run to execute for allowlisted policies.
- **16:15:10** — First routing policy flips applied (Hosted API allowlist).
- **16:17** — Traffic starts shifting measurably (RPS down in us-east; up in eu-west).
- **16:21:22** — Traffic shift effectively complete for the allowlist (~95% moved).
- **16:24** — Noted tail latency increase in eu-west for one route/model combination.
- **16:27** — Automated rollback triggered for one policy due to hold-down timer + “recovery” signal mismatch (see Issues #2). Partial rollback only.
- **16:33** — Impairment removed in us-east.
- **16:38** — Controller recommends return-to-primary for allowlisted policies; held due to dampening window.
- **16:45** — Approved return-to-primary begins.
- **16:52** — Return-to-primary complete.
- **17:05** — Bridge closed; immediate hotwash begins.

## Metrics snapshot
### Failover timing
| Metric | Value |
|---|---:|
| Time to first authoritative signal (reachability) | 24s |
| Time to recommendation (policy decision) | 62s (p50), 88s (p95) |
| Approval to first policy flip | 28s |
| First flip to 95% traffic moved (allowlist) | 6m 12s |
| Total “impairment start” to 95% moved | 8m 20s |

### Customer impact (allowlisted traffic only)
- **HTTP 5xx rate**: peaked at **1.9%** for ~3 minutes; baseline ~0.15%.
- **Timeouts** (gateway): peaked at **0.8%** for ~4 minutes; baseline ~0.05%.
- **p95 latency**:
  - us-east: increased during impairment (expected)
  - eu-west: for most routes within **+18%** of baseline after shift
  - one route/model exceeded **+55% p95** for ~12 minutes (see Issues #1)

### SLO / error budget
- Multi-region availability SLO burn attributable to the exercise was within planned allowance for the allowlist, but we recorded:
  - **Fast burn alert fired** once and auto-resolved after tuning window; categorized as **expected** but created paging noise (see Follow-ups).

### Dedicated pool observations
- No automated drain executed.
- Drain readiness checks indicated eu-west headroom sufficient for the test pool, but the “safe-to-failover” gating computation was slow to update (capacity signal lag; see Issues #3).

## What worked
1. **Signal hierarchy behaved as designed**: reachability was authoritative and drove quick recommendation, with 5xx burn corroboration arriving shortly after.
2. **Dry-run output was usable** for operator confirmation (recommended action + evidence signals).
3. **Approval hook prevented accidental broad flip**; execution remained constrained to allowlisted policies.
4. **Controller did not stampede**: rate limits and anti-flap guardrails prevented repeated flips despite noisy intermediate signals.

## Issues found
### 1) Tail latency spike in eu-west after traffic shift (one route/model)
- **Symptoms**: p95 latency +55% for ~12 minutes; localized to a single route + model family.
- **Likely cause**: capacity headroom computed at region level looked healthy, but queue depth for a specific GPU pool tier was saturated; routing did not account for per-pool constraints.
- **Impact**: partial failure of “post-failover steady-state” success criteria.

### 2) Unexpected partial rollback triggered by hold-down interplay
- **Symptoms**: one policy rolled back to us-east while impairment was still partially active, due to “recovery” classification flipping earlier than expected.
- **Likely cause**: reachability probes recovered for a subset of edges while 5xx burn was still elevated; rollback logic treated recovery as sufficient for that specific policy because corroboration requirements were met inconsistently.
- **Impact**: operator confusion; increased churn in dashboards.

### 3) Capacity signal lag for Dedicated gating
- **Symptoms**: “safe-to-failover” gating for Dedicated updated slower than expected (minutes), reducing confidence in automation readiness.
- **Likely cause**: aggregation interval + caching TTL in the capacity pipeline; missing jitter alignment with region health reads.

### 4) Streaming disconnects during redirect
- **Symptoms**: streaming clients experienced disconnects and did not always reconnect cleanly when redirected across regions.
- **Impact**: most visible customer-facing issue; increases perceived downtime even when non-streaming succeeds.

### 5) Dashboards lacked automation-driven annotations and consistent timestamps
- **Symptoms**: hard to correlate flips, recommendations, and rollbacks with metrics without digging into logs.
- **Impact**: slowed triage during the exercise; raises risk during real incidents.

## Follow-up actions
### Engineering tickets (priority ordered)
1. **Fix streaming reconnect on region redirect**
   - Owner: **Peter Holtz** / Runtime + Gateway
   - Tracking: **ENG-2422**; related: **SUP-25322**
   - Target: ship to production behind flag by **2026-02-07**

2. **Adjust rollback corroboration rules + dampening**
   - Owner: **Jordan Lee** (Policy engine), review with **Miles Thornton**
   - Tracking: **ENG-2416** follow-up + ADR-043 tuning note
   - Target: propose config change + tests by **2026-02-05**

3. **Add per-pool capacity constraints into routing / failover gating**
   - Owner: **Vanessa Ortiz** + **Ravi Menon**
   - Tracking: **ENG-2414** extension
   - Target: design + metric plan by **2026-02-12**

4. **Add automation annotations to dashboards + incident timeline**
   - Owner: **Allison Grant** / **Sergio Costa**
   - Tracking: **ENG-2418** + internal support **INT-1149**
   - Target: dashboards updated before next game day (**2026-02-15**)

5. **Tune burn-rate alerts to reduce paging noise during controlled tests**
   - Owner: **Tomáš Novák**
   - Tracking: follow-up on **ENG-2418** rules; coordinate with SRE paging policy
   - Target: adjusted thresholds + “planned test suppression” runbook section by **2026-02-09**

6. **Capacity signal freshness improvements (TTL/jitter alignment)**
   - Owner: **Allison Grant** + **Vanessa Ortiz**
   - Tracking: new subtask under **ENG-2414**
   - Target: implement and validate in staging by **2026-02-14**

### Process / runbook updates
- Update Hosted API regional failover runbook to include:
  - “Known issue” guidance for streaming redirects
  - How to interpret dry-run confidence and corroboration signals
  - Explicit decision points for partial rollback scenarios
  - Owner: **Kira Thompson**; tracking: **ENG-2423**

### Support and customer comms
- Support macro for “transient 5xx during planned routing test” reviewed and published.
  - Ticket reference: **SUP-25310**
  - Owner: **Owen Phillips**

## Artifacts
- Controller audit events (selected): linked in incident thread and stored in log index `failover-audit-prod-*` for 2026-01-15.
- Dashboard set used:
  - Failover signals overview
  - Route-level error/latency panels for allowlisted traffic
  - Region health (reachability + 5xx burn + latency burn)

## Recommendations for Game Day #2
- Tighten blast radius further: single route + single model family for first 15 minutes, then expand.
- Require “per-pool headroom green” before shifting any Dedicated traffic.
- Pre-announce internal-only status page entry to exercise comms workflow without confusing customers.
- Ensure dashboard annotations are present and verified in a pre-flight checklist.

## Appendix: test configuration (high level)
- Allowlist: limited set of tenants tagged `tier=internal` and `tier=pilot`.
- Controller: execute mode requires manual approval; max flips per hour enforced.
- DNS/Anycast: no global DNS TTL changes executed during this test (routing policy only).

---
Document owner: Miles Thornton (Failover Architecture). For corrections to timeline/metrics, reply in the linked incident thread or file a doc change request in ENG-2426.

| Approval to traffic shift start | ≤ 2 min | **Met (1m 34s)** |
| Traffic shift completion (allowlisted traffic) | ≤ 10 min | **Met (6m 12s)** |
| Customer-visible errors during exercise | No sustained increase; transient ok | **Partially met** (transient 5xx/timeouts observed) |
| No oscillation / uncontrolled rollback | None | **Partially met** (one automated rollback due to hold-down interplay) |
| Post-failover steady-state in target region | Stable p95 within +30% | **Not met for one route** (tail latency spike) |

## Timeline (UTC)
> Notes: Times are from incident bridge log + controller audit events. Dashboards lacked some annotations (see Issues) so some points are reconstructed from logs.

- **16:00** — Bridge opened. Preconditions reviewed (capacity headroom, paging routes, feature flags).
- **16:07** — Controller dry-run enabled for allowlisted traffic policies.
- **16:12** — Synthetic impairment introduced in **us-east** (network reachability degradation for a subset of edges + increased gateway error injection for test routes).
- **16:13:02** — Region health aggregator begins marking **us-east** as *degraded* (reachability signal first).
- **16:13:26** — Policy engine evaluates trigger hierarchy and produces **FailoverRecommendation: us-east → eu-west** for allowlisted routes.
- **16:14** — On-call confirms corroboration signals (5xx burn + probe reachability).
- **16:14:42** — Approval granted; controller execution toggled from dry-run to execute for allowlisted policies.
- **16:15:10** — First routing policy flips applied (Hosted API allowlist).
- **16:17** — Traffic starts shifting measurably (RPS down in us-east; up in eu-west).
- **16:21:22** — Traffic shift effectively complete for the allowlist (~95% moved).
- **16:24** — Noted tail latency increase in eu-west for one route/model combination.
- **16:27** — Automated rollback triggered for one policy due to hold-down timer + “recovery” signal mismatch (see Issues #2). Partial rollback only.
- **16:33** — Impairment removed in us-east.
- **16:38** — Controller recommends return-to-primary for allowlisted policies; held due to dampening window.
- **16:45** — Approved return-to-primary begins.
- **16:52** — Return-to-primary complete.
- **17:05** — Bridge closed; immediate hotwash begins.

## Metrics snapshot
### Failover timing
| Metric | Value |
|---|---:|
| Time to first authoritative signal (reachability) | 24s |
| Time to recommendation (policy decision) | 62s (p50), 88s (p95) |
| Approval to first policy flip | 28s |
| First flip to 95% traffic moved (allowlist) | 6m 12s |
| Total “impairment start” to 95% moved | 8m 20s |

### Customer impact (allowlisted traffic only)
- **HTTP 5xx rate**: peaked at **1.9%** for ~3 minutes; baseline ~0.15%.
- **Timeouts** (gateway): peaked at **0.8%** for ~4 minutes; baseline ~0.05%.
- **p95 latency**:
  - us-east: increased during impairment (expected)
  - eu-west: for most routes within **+18%** of baseline after shift
  - one route/model exceeded **+55% p95** for ~12 minutes (see Issues #1)

### SLO / error budget
- Multi-region availability SLO burn attributable to the exercise was within planned allowance for the allowlist, but we recorded:
  - **Fast burn alert fired** once and auto-resolved after tuning window; categorized as **expected** but created paging noise (see Follow-ups).

### Dedicated pool observations
- No automated drain executed.
- Drain readiness checks indicated eu-west headroom sufficient for the test pool, but the “safe-to-failover” gating computation was slow to update (capacity signal lag; see Issues #3).

## What worked
1. **Signal hierarchy behaved as designed**: reachability was authoritative and drove quick recommendation, with 5xx burn corroboration arriving shortly after.
2. **Dry-run output was usable** for operator confirmation (recommended action + evidence signals).
3. **Approval hook prevented accidental broad flip**; execution remained constrained to allowlisted policies.
4. **Controller did not stampede**: rate limits and anti-flap guardrails prevented repeated flips despite noisy intermediate signals.

## Issues found
### 1) Tail latency spike in eu-west after traffic shift (one route/model)
- **Symptoms**: p95 latency +55% for ~12 minutes; localized to a single route + model family.
- **Likely cause**: capacity headroom computed at region level looked healthy, but queue depth for a specific GPU pool tier was saturated; routing did not account for per-pool constraints.
- **Impact**: partial failure of “post-failover steady-state” success criteria.

### 2) Unexpected partial rollback triggered by hold-down interplay
- **Symptoms**: one policy rolled back to us-east while impairment was still partially active, due to “recovery” classification flipping earlier than expected.
- **Likely cause**: reachability probes recovered for a subset of edges while 5xx burn was still elevated; rollback logic treated recovery as sufficient for that specific policy because corroboration requirements were met inconsistently.
- **Impact**: operator confusion; increased churn in dashboards.

### 3) Capacity signal lag for Dedicated gating
- **Symptoms**: “safe-to-failover” gating for Dedicated updated slower than expected (minutes), reducing confidence in automation readiness.
- **Likely cause**: aggregation interval + caching TTL in the capacity pipeline; missing jitter alignment with region health reads.

### 4) Streaming disconnects during redirect
- **Symptoms**: streaming clients experienced disconnects and did not always reconnect cleanly when redirected across regions.
- **Impact**: most visible customer-facing issue; increases perceived downtime even when non-streaming succeeds.

### 5) Dashboards lacked automation-driven annotations and consistent timestamps
- **Symptoms**: hard to correlate flips, recommendations, and rollbacks with metrics without digging into logs.
- **Impact**: slowed triage during the exercise; raises risk during real incidents.

## Follow-up actions
### Engineering tickets (priority ordered)
1. **Fix streaming reconnect on region redirect**
   - Owner: **Peter Holtz** / Runtime + Gateway
   - Tracking: **ENG-2422**; related: **SUP-25322**
   - Target: ship to production behind flag by **2026-02-07**

2. **Adjust rollback corroboration rules + dampening**
   - Owner: **Jordan Lee** (Policy engine), review with **Miles Thornton**
   - Tracking: **ENG-2416** follow-up + ADR-043 tuning note
   - Target: propose config change + tests by **2026-02-05**

3. **Add per-pool capacity constraints into routing / failover gating**
   - Owner: **Vanessa Ortiz** + **Ravi Menon**
   - Tracking: **ENG-2414** extension
   - Target: design + metric plan by **2026-02-12**

4. **Add automation annotations to dashboards + incident timeline**
   - Owner: **Allison Grant** / **Sergio Costa**
   - Tracking: **ENG-2418** + internal support **INT-1149**
   - Target: dashboards updated before next game day (**2026-02-15**)

5. **Tune burn-rate alerts to reduce paging noise during controlled tests**
   - Owner: **Tomáš Novák**
   - Tracking: follow-up on **ENG-2418** rules; coordinate with SRE paging policy
   - Target: adjusted thresholds + “planned test suppression” runbook section by **2026-02-09**

6. **Capacity signal freshness improvements (TTL/jitter alignment)**
   - Owner: **Allison Grant** + **Vanessa Ortiz**
   - Tracking: new subtask under **ENG-2414**
   - Target: implement and validate in staging by **2026-02-14**

### Process / runbook updates
- Update Hosted API regional failover runbook to include:
  - “Known issue” guidance for streaming redirects
  - How to interpret dry-run confidence and corroboration signals
  - Explicit decision points for partial rollback scenarios
  - Owner: **Kira Thompson**; tracking: **ENG-2423**

### Support and customer comms
- Support macro for “transient 5xx during planned routing test” reviewed and published.
  - Ticket reference: **SUP-25310**
  - Owner: **Owen Phillips**

## Artifacts
- Controller audit events (selected): linked in incident thread and stored in log index `failover-audit-prod-*` for 2026-01-15.
- Dashboard set used:
  - Failover signals overview
  - Route-level error/latency panels for allowlisted traffic
  - Region health (reachability + 5xx burn + latency burn)

## Recommendations for Game Day #2
- Tighten blast radius further: single route + single model family for first 15 minutes, then expand.
- Require “per-pool headroom green” before shifting any Dedicated traffic.
- Pre-announce internal-only status page entry to exercise comms workflow without confusing customers.
- Ensure dashboard annotations are present and verified in a pre-flight checklist.

## Appendix: test configuration (high level)
- Allowlist: limited set of tenants tagged `tier=internal` and `tier=pilot`.
- Controller: execute mode requires manual approval; max flips per hour enforced.
- DNS/Anycast: no global DNS TTL changes executed during this test (routing policy only).

---
Document owner: Miles Thornton (Failover Architecture). For corrections to timeline/metrics, reply in the linked incident thread or file a doc change request in ENG-2426.

source.title: Postmortem: EU API latency and elevated 5xx (edge routing cache)
source.author: Ava Chen
source.body:
h1. Summary
On 2026-01-19 we experienced elevated p95 latency and intermittent HTTP 5xx for a subset of EU-hosted API traffic. The incident lasted 47 minutes end-to-end. Customer impact was concentrated in the eu-west region and primarily affected chat completions with streaming enabled.

*Customer impact:* Yes (degraded performance + partial errors)
*Severity:* SEV-2
*Owning engineering team:* Platform Engineering (Traffic & Edge)
*Primary services:* edge-router, request-gateway, redis-edge-cache

h1. Impact
* ~12% of EU requests exceeded the latency SLO (p95 > 3.5s)
* ~1.8% request failure rate (HTTP 502/503) for EU traffic
* No data loss; retries succeeded after mitigation

h1. Timeline (UTC)
* 10:12 - Alert fired: eu-west request-gateway 5xx > 1%
* 10:15 - On-call acknowledged; triage indicates edge-router cache churn
* 10:21 - Mitigation 1: disabled new cache key format via feature flag
* 10:28 - 5xx decreases but latency still elevated
* 10:35 - Mitigation 2: increased redis-edge-cache memory limit + restarted one shard
* 10:45 - Latency returns to baseline
* 10:59 - Incident resolved; monitoring for recurrence

h1. Root cause
A configuration rollout introduced a new cache-key schema for routing policy evaluation. In eu-west the cache key cardinality was higher than expected due to a region-specific header normalization discrepancy, causing excessive churn and evictions in redis-edge-cache. Evictions increased cache miss rate, pushing more requests into the slower policy evaluation path and increasing load on request-gateway. Under load, request-gateway emitted intermittent 502/503.

h1. Detection
Detected via automated alerting on request-gateway 5xx and latency SLO burn for eu-west.

h1. What went well
* Alerts triggered quickly and were actionable.
* Feature flag allowed fast rollback of the risky cache-key change.

h1. What went poorly
* Canary coverage did not include eu-west header normalization behavior.
* Cache churn was not directly monitored; we inferred it from secondary signals.

h1. Action items
1) Add explicit metric + alert for redis-edge-cache eviction rate and cache miss ratio (Owner: Platform Eng; Due: 2026-02-15)
2) Expand routing-policy canary tests to include regional header normalization fixtures (Owner: DevEx; Due: 2026-02-28)
3) Update change-management checklist for edge-router config rollouts (Owner: Platform Eng; Due: 2026-02-10)

h1. References
* Related runbook: oncall-and-incident-response/runbooks/edge-router-triage
* Incident channel: #incidents (thread 1770036720)

source.title: Incident Seeding and Warm Recovery Playbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook explains our controlled incident seeding practice and a standard warm-recovery runloop for LLM-serving incidents. Incident seeding is a deliberate, low-risk exercise where we inject small, observable faults to validate detection, routing, and warm-handoff paths without impacting customer-facing SLOs. Warm recovery is a set of prioritized, pre-authorized mitigations that restore service quality while preserving user sessions and KV cache state.

Scope
- Services: routing-proxy, runtime-gateway, kv-cache, model-pool, rate-limiter.
- Regions: us-west-2, eu-central-1.
- Tenancy: multitenant hosted API and Dedicated pools.

Definitions
- Seeding event: a controlled fault or degradation introduced for testing (e.g., 20% KV cache eviction, isolated model fallback).
- Warm handoff: transferring active incident ownership from automated systems to oncall with minimal impact to users.
- Pre-seed window: scheduled 15–30 minute window with notification and rollback controls.

Ownership and Roles
- Incident Owner (primary oncall): implements warm recovery steps and coordinates customer comms.
- Seeding Operator (SRE): schedules and executes seed operations during game-day windows.
- Recovery SME (runtime team): provides model/fallback expertise and approves rollbacks.
- Communications Lead (support/CS): publishes status updates using templates below.

Pre-seed checklist (before any seeding event)
1. Confirm pre-seed window and notify stakeholders (email, Slack #ops, status page placeholder).
2. Verify a clean baseline: SLOs within threshold for last 48 hours.
3. Snapshot observability: export latency histograms, token-cost metrics, KV hit rate, and recent traces.
4. Pre-authorize mitigations: cold-start rollback, model-fallback map, and scaling playbook accessible.
5. Ensure rollback procedure is documented and tested locally.
6. Open a short-lived incident on our tracker (tag: seeded).

Seeding patterns (low-risk examples)
- KV cache partial eviction: Evict cache for a single availability-zone pool for 2 minutes to validate cache miss surge handling.
- Model variant redirect: Route 10% of dummy traffic to a quantized-but-compatible model to verify fallback quality metrics.
- Rate limiter spike: Temporarily increase throttling threshold on a synthetic client to observe backpressure propagation.

Warm Recovery Runbook (ordered steps, target RTO: 12 minutes for partial degradations)
Preparation: The Incident Owner must have access to the runbook, cloud accounts, and console dashboards.
1) Acknowledge and classify (0–2 minutes)
   - Confirm seeded flag on incident ticket.
   - Set bridge if incident >5 minutes expected.
   - Record initial SLO deviations (p50/p95/p99 latency, KV hit rate, error rate).
2) Stabilize traffic routing (2–6 minutes)
   - If KV miss storm observed, route affected tenants to a fallback read-through path: runbook command example: curl -X POST https://runbook.internal/redwood/route -d '{"target":"fallback-readthrough","pool":"us-west-2-fallback"}'
   - Reduce cross-tenant blast radius by enabling tenant-level throttles for non-critical customers.
3) Preserve model state (6–9 minutes)
   - Pin active model variants for sessions where possible to avoid re-compilation churn. Kubernetes example: kubectl annotate pod/<model-pod> "model-lock=seeded-session-<ticket>"
   - If a model fallback is in use, check quality signals in eval-stream (prefix-match QA checks).
4) Targeted autoscale and cache warmup (9–14 minutes)
   - Trigger predictive warm-up for model-pool using synthetic warm calls: ./scripts/prime_pool.sh --pool us-west-2-fallback --rate 20
   - Gradually ramp traffic back from fallback in 10% increments while monitoring p95 latency and coherence metrics.
5) Validate and close (14–20 minutes)
   - Confirm metrics returned to acceptable thresholds and close seeded tag.
   - Publish status update (see templates).

Escalation and rollback criteria (fast paths)
- Rollback to pre-seed state immediately if: sustained p95 latency > 2x baseline for 10+ minutes OR error-rate spike results in >0.5% 5xx for customer-facing requests.
- Escalate to Recovery SME if model coherence score drops > 15% vs baseline during fallback.

Multi-tenant sequencing notes
- Always perform seeded cache eviction on a non-critical tenant first. Observe system-wide propagation for 5 minutes before broader target.
- Use tenant labels to correlate customer impact and avoid mass throttle flapping.

Game Day Scenarios and Exercise Design
- Scenario A: KV Eviction Cascade (30-minute lab)
  - Objective: exercise detection, routing, warm handoff and customer comms.
  - Steps: seed 20% eviction in one AZ, simulate 100 synthetic concurrent sessions across 10 tenants, observe backpressure.
  - Success criteria: automated routing reduces p95 by 30% within 10 minutes, no customer-facing errors persist beyond 15 minutes.
- Scenario B: Quantized Model Fallback (45-minute lab)
  - Objective: ensure quality monitoring and rollback triggers for model fallbacks.

On-call rotation integration
- Pre-seed windows are scheduled against oncall shifts; primary oncall must opt-in to broadcast.
- If a seed overlaps with a shift handoff, follow the Warm Handoff checklist (below) before seeding begins.

Warm Handoff checklist (when transferring an active seeded incident)
- Summarize actions taken, commands executed, and current metric deltas.
- Ensure next oncall has access tokens and runbook anchor link.
- Transfer any active synthetic warm-up scripts and their PIDs or orchestrator run IDs.

Customer Status Templates (use these verbatim on status.redwood.ai and in CSR replies)
1) Initial placeholder (within 10 minutes):
   - status.redwood.ai: "We are investigating elevated latency affecting a subset of inference requests in US-West. Engineers are executing a controlled test to validate route-level mitigations. We do not expect persistent customer impact at this time. Updates every 15 minutes."
2) Mitigation in progress (after stabilization actions started):
   - "Mitigation implemented: traffic is temporarily routed to a fallback pool to preserve request reliability. We are monitoring latency and coherence metrics; no action required from customers."
3) Degraded impact (if customers affected):
   - "We are aware that some requests may see higher latency or partial errors. We have a mitigation in place and are prioritizing affected customers. If you observe severe issues, reply to this thread with request IDs."
4) Resolution and follow-up:
   - "Service restored to expected levels. We executed a controlled test and reverted seeded changes. A post-incident note and timeline will be published within 72 hours."

Post-seed evidence & postmortem guidance
- Required evidence artifacts (attach to ticket):
  - timestamped metric exports (latency histograms, KV hit rate, throughput)
  - bridge transcript and participant list
  - commands and runbook versions used (git commit hash for runbook)
  - synthetic request IDs and sample payloads (sanitized)
  - quality signal snapshots for model fallbacks (eval-stream excerpts)
- Postmortem structure (suggested sections):
  1. Summary and impact (what customers experienced)
  2. Timeline (minute-by-minute)
  3. Root cause analysis (technical and process)
  4. Mitigations executed (with links to commands and artifacts)
  5. Action items and owners (target date)

Operational thresholds and quick reference table
- SLOs referenced during seed testing: p95 latency <= baseline * 1.5, KV hit rate >= baseline - 10%, 5xx rate <= 0.25%
- Rapid rollback triggers: p95 > 2x or sustained 5xx > 0.5% or customer complaints > 3 within 15 minutes.

Quick commands (examples)
- Check model-pool health: kubectl get pods -l app=model-pool -o wide
- Route traffic to fallback (internal API): curl -X POST https://ops-api.internal/v1/route -H 'Authorization: Bearer <TOKEN>' -d '{"pool":"fallback-usw2","tenants":["synthetic-tenant-1"]}'
- Export latency histogram (Prometheus): curl -s http://prometheus.internal/api/v1/query?query=histogram_quantile(0.95, rate(request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))

Related pages and assets
- Link to the canonical incident command playbook (related_pages above).
- Repository: git@internal:redwood/incidents/seeded-runbooks.git (contains scripts and priming helpers).
- Observability dashboard: Dashboards > Incident > Seeded-runbook-latency-template.

Maintenance and governance
- Seeds must be scheduled and approved by SRE leads. Low-risk seeds may be run weekly; higher-risk seeds quarterly.
- All seeds are recorded in the Incident Seeding ledger (internal spreadsheet) with tagging for audit.

Appendix: example status page JSON template
{ "status": "investigating", "summary": "Elevated latency observed for a subset of inference requests in US-West", "impact": "Partial degradation for a subset of customers; mitigation underway", "components": ["runtime-gateway","model-pool-usw2"], "update_interval_mins": 15 }

Contact and escalation
- Oncall channel: #ops-oncall (pager: SRE rota)
- Escalation path: primary oncall -> SRE lead -> runtime team -> CTO on-call for Exec notification.

Document ownership and review cadence
- Owner: engineering (SRE). Review: quarterly or after each seeded exercise.

Last updated: 2026-01-28 (runbook refinement after Q4 game-day).
